anchors.snippet: To verify the molecular accuracy of the design process, we determined the structure of Des27.7 in the unbound form by crystallographic analysis (Extended Data Table 2; PDB 9HVB). All active-site positions aligned well with the design conception (less than $0 . 7 \mathring \mathrm { A }$ all-atom root mean squared deviation (r.m.s.d.) across 20 residues), including the catalytic Asp162, although a slight shift (r.m.s.d. 0.78 Å) was observed in the orientation of Phe113. Outside the active-site pocket, 180 of 257 positions aligned with backbone r.m.s.d. $< 0 . 6 \mathring \mathbf { A }$ , but 65 amino acids either deviated or did not exhibit significant electron density, probably due to backbone flexibility in this region (Extended Data Fig. 6a,b). This fragment is known to be dynamic in the IGPS protein family52, but it lies outside the active-site pocket and probably does not contribute directly to reactivity and substrate recognition. Taken together, our results verify a fully computational pipeline that designs an accurate de novo active site and generates a stable and high-efficiency new-to-nature enzyme.

anchors.snippet: Crystals were grown at $1 9 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ using the sitting-drop vapour diffusion method. Diffraction data were collected from a single crystal flash-cooled to 100 K, using a wavelength of 1.34 Å. Data were collected using an in-house Rigaku liquid-metal-jet X-ray Synergy System with a HyPix Arc $1 5 0 ^ { \circ }$ detector. AlphaFold2 (ref. 57) was used to generate all three models for molecular replacement (Extended Data Table 2). Initial models were iteratively rebuilt and refined using COOT63 and PHENIX64. Model geometry was evaluated using MOLPROBITY65. Atomic coordinates and structure factors for Des27.7, R2.Des39 and R2.Des49 are deposited in the PDB database under accession numbers 9HVB, 9HVH and 9HVG, respectively.

anchors.snippet: In total, 66 designs were solubly expressed and 14 showed cooperative thermal denaturation (Extended Data Fig. 1). Three designs showed measurable KE activity in an initial screen, with the top two designs, Des27 and Des61, exhibiting ${ k _ { \mathrm { { c a t } } } } / { K _ { \mathrm { { M } } } }$ values of 130 and 210 $\mathsf { M } ^ { - 1 } \mathsf { S } ^ { - 1 }$ , respectively, and $k _ { \mathrm { c a t } } < 1 \mathsf { s } ^ { - 1 }$ (Extended Data Fig. 2, Extended Data Table 1 and Supplementary Table 1).

anchors.snippet: The designed genes were ordered from Twist Bioscience, cloned into pET28 plasmid with an N-terminal His-tag, followed by a bdSUMO tag. Plasmids were transformed into Escherichia coli BL21 (DE3) cells. For expression, $5 0 \mathrm { m l }$ of 2YT medium supplemented with $5 0 \mu \mathrm { g } \mathsf { m } ^ { - 1 }$ kanamycin was inoculated with $5 0 0 \mu \mathrm { l }$ of overnight culture produced from a single colony and grown at $3 7 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ until optical density $\left( \mathrm { O D } \right) _ { 6 0 0 }$ 0.6–0.8. Overexpression was induced by adding 1 mM IPTG and the cultures were grown for 20 h at $1 6 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ and collected, and the pellet was frozen at $- 2 0 { } ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ . The cells were resuspended in basic buffer $( 5 0 \mathsf { m } \mathsf { M }$ Tris-Cl pH 7.25, 200 mM NaCl) supplemented with $1 0 \mu \mathrm { g } \mathsf { m } ^ { - 1 }$ lysozyme, protease-inhibitor cocktail (Sigma) and benzonase, lysed by sonication and centrifuged at $2 0 { , } 0 0 0 g$ for 30 min a $4 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C } .$ . The soluble fraction was loaded onto an Ni-NTA (nitrilotriacetic acid) column and washed twice with basic buffer and $2 0 \mathrm { m M }$ imidazole. The protein was subjected to overnight on-column Sumo protease cleavage at $4 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ $S \mu \mathrm { g } \mathsf { m } \mathsf { l } ^ { - 1 }$ in basic buffer). Protein purity was assessed by SDS–PAGE. Protein concentration was determined using Pierce BCA protein assay kit. For crystallography, large-scale expression was performed in 1,500 ml of culture. After Ni-NTA purification and bdSUMO cleavage, the protein was purified by gel filtration (HiLoad 26/600 Superdex75 preparative grade column, GE).

anchors.snippet: (20 designs) showed measurable KE activity with ${ k _ { \mathrm { { c a t } } } } / { K _ { \mathrm { { M } } } }$ in the range of $0 . 5 { - } 1 5 5 \mathbf { M } ^ { - 1 } \mathbf { s } ^ { - 1 }$ , demonstrating that the workflow can design stable and functional Kemp eliminases in a wide range of different starting points. As expected, designs that did not show cooperative unfolding lacked KE activity. We applied FuncLib to the active sites of six designs and tested 9–14 variants for each starting point. In five cases, catalytic efficiencies improved by 3–10-fold (Supplementary Table 1), with the highest catalytic efficiency reaching $3 0 0 \mathsf { M } ^ { - 1 } \mathsf { s } ^ { - 1 }$ (R2.Des39.2). We determined the crystallographic structure of two designs, R2.Des39 $( k _ { \mathrm { c a t } } / K _ { \mathrm { M } }$ $1 0 0 \mathsf { M } ^ { - 1 } \mathsf { s } ^ { - 1 } )$ and Des49 $( k _ { \mathrm { c a t } } / K _ { \mathrm { M } } 1 5 0 \mathsf { M } ^ { - 1 } \mathsf { s } ^ { - 1 } )$ (Extended Data Fig. 6c–i, PDB IDs 9HVH and 9HVG and Extended Data Table 2). The active sites were close to their design conceptions (r.m.s.d. $< 0 . 6$ Å and r.m.s.d. $< 0 . 8 2 \mathrm { \AA }$ , respectively), but, in both cases, several loops either lacked electron density or exhibited significant conformational changes compared with the designs, which could impede substrate entry to the active site53. To explore whether the foldability of these loops could be improved, we applied FuncLib to stabilize these regions according to the design models. Three of 16 FuncLib variants of Des39.2 showed a significant increase in catalytic efficiency, with improvements up to 20-fold compared with the original design, reaching $k _ { \mathrm { c a t } } / K _ { \mathrm { M } } 2 , 0 0 0 \mathsf { M } ^ { - 1 } \mathsf { s } ^ { - 1 }$ (Extended Data Table 1 and Supplementary Table 1), but none surpassed the performance of Des27.7. These results demonstrate that large-scale artificial intelligence-based structure prediction of natural enzymes provides a valuable resource for de novo enzyme design, and that the computational workflow reproducibly generates efficient enzymes. In this case, however, optimization with FuncLib reached superior catalytic parameters in the designs derived from modular assembly, which may reflect the greater structural diversity in these designs.
anchors.excerpt:

anchors.snippet: The underlying reasons for the low efficiencies of de novo designed enzymes have been intensely studied10,17–19. These analyses revealed that the designed active sites exhibited significant structural distortions relative to the design conception17,19. Notably, catalysis is extremely sensitive to molecular details, and shifts of the catalytic constellation by a few degrees or tenths of an Ångstrom from optimality may translate into orders of magnitude decreases in efficiency20. Furthermore, designs often exhibited low stability and expressibility7 , limiting their ability to accommodate activity-enhancing mutations7,21. Further concerns were that fixed-backbone design methods fail to precisely position non-native catalytic groups1 ; the molecular details of the designed transition state (theozyme) were uncertain6,22; and that protein dynamics23 and long-range electrostatic interactions may be necessary to achieve high catalytic efficiency but are unaccounted for in the design process6,24,25.
anchors.excerpt:

anchors.snippet: Crystals were grown at $1 9 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ using the sitting-drop vapour diffusion method. Diffraction data were collected from a single crystal flash-cooled to 100 K, using a wavelength of 1.34 Å. Data were collected using an in-house Rigaku liquid-metal-jet X-ray Synergy System with a HyPix Arc $1 5 0 ^ { \circ }$ detector. AlphaFold2 (ref. 57) was used to generate all three models for molecular replacement (Extended Data Table 2). Initial models were iteratively rebuilt and refined using COOT63 and PHENIX64. Model geometry was evaluated using MOLPROBITY65. Atomic coordinates and structure factors for Des27.7, R2.Des39 and R2.Des49 are deposited in the PDB database under accession numbers 9HVB, 9HVH and 9HVG, respectively.

anchors.snippet: ![](images/87ba402fa401b77021501bdaaa25aa50c1e19d7d4c2e6876106b43807cbfd2cc.jpg)
![](images/c050cf19414f34f5ebd2b5421852409f4498aa5da50dfc80c3bdf8c7a91985b7.jpg)
![](images/afa4269e903436cf6900e465de592ec293def3e51c2d6dffcd3079e75b520a62.jpg)

(20 designs) showed measurable KE activity with ${ k _ { \mathrm { { c a t } } } } / { K _ { \mathrm { { M } } } }$ in the range of $0 . 5 { - } 1 5 5 \mathbf { M } ^ { - 1 } \mathbf { s } ^ { - 1 }$ , demonstrating that the workflow can design stable and functional Kemp eliminases in a wide range of different starting points. As expected, designs that did not show cooperative unfolding lacked KE activity. We applied FuncLib to the active sites of six designs and tested 9–14 variants for each starting point. In five cases, catalytic efficiencies improved by 3–10-fold (Supplementary Table 1), with the highest catalytic efficiency reaching $3 0 0 \mathsf { M } ^ { - 1 } \mathsf { s } ^ { - 1 }$ (R2.Des39.2). We determined the crystallographic structure of two designs, R2.Des39 $( k _ { \mathrm { c a t } } / K _ { \mathrm { M } }$ $1 0 0 \mathsf { M } ^ { - 1 } \mathsf { s } ^ { - 1 } )$ and Des49 $( k _ { \mathrm { c a t } } / K _ { \mathrm { M } } 1 5 0 \mathsf { M } ^ { - 1 } \mathsf { s } ^ { - 1 } )$ (Extended Data Fig. 6c–i, PDB IDs 9HVH and 9HVG and Extended Data Table 2). The active sites were close to their design conceptions (r.m.s.d. $< 0 . 6$ Å and r.m.s.d. $< 0 . 8 2 \mathrm { \AA }$ , respectively), but, in both cases, several loops either lacked electron density or exhibited significant conformational changes compared with the designs, which could impede substrate entry to the active site53. To explore whether the foldability of these loops could be improved, we applied FuncLib to stabilize these regions according to the design models. Three of 16 FuncLib variants of Des39.2 showed a significant increase in catalytic efficiency, with improvements up to 20-fold compared with the original design, reaching $k _ { \mathrm { c a t } } / K _ { \mathrm { M } } 2 , 0 0 0 \mathsf { M } ^ { - 1 } \mathsf { s } ^ { - 1 }$ (Extended Data Table 1 and Supplementary Table 1), but none surpassed the performance of Des27.7. These results demonstrate that large-scale artificial intelligence-based structure prediction of natural enzymes provides a valuable resource for de novo enzyme design, and that the computational workflow reproducibly generates efficient enzymes. In this case, however, optimization with FuncLib reached superior catalytic parameters in the designs derived from modular assembly, which may reflect the greater structural diversity in these designs.

anchors.snippet: The designed genes were ordered from Twist Bioscience, cloned into pET28 plasmid with an N-terminal His-tag, followed by a bdSUMO tag. Plasmids were transformed into Escherichia coli BL21 (DE3) cells. For expression, $5 0 \mathrm { m l }$ of 2YT medium supplemented with $5 0 \mu \mathrm { g } \mathsf { m } ^ { - 1 }$ kanamycin was inoculated with $5 0 0 \mu \mathrm { l }$ of overnight culture produced from a single colony and grown at $3 7 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ until optical density $\left( \mathrm { O D } \right) _ { 6 0 0 }$ 0.6–0.8. Overexpression was induced by adding 1 mM IPTG and the cultures were grown for 20 h at $1 6 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ and collected, and the pellet was frozen at $- 2 0 { } ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ . The cells were resuspended in basic buffer $( 5 0 \mathsf { m } \mathsf { M }$ Tris-Cl pH 7.25, 200 mM NaCl) supplemented with $1 0 \mu \mathrm { g } \mathsf { m } ^ { - 1 }$ lysozyme, protease-inhibitor cocktail (Sigma) and benzonase, lysed by sonication and centrifuged at $2 0 { , } 0 0 0 g$ for 30 min a $4 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C } .$ . The soluble fraction was loaded onto an Ni-NTA (nitrilotriacetic acid) column and washed twice with basic buffer and $2 0 \mathrm { m M }$ imidazole. The protein was subjected to overnight on-column Sumo protease cleavage at $4 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ $S \mu \mathrm { g } \mathsf { m } \mathsf { l } ^ { - 1 }$ in basic buffer). Protein purity was assessed by SDS–PAGE. Protein concentration was determined using Pierce BCA protein assay kit. For crystallography, large-scale expression was performed in 1,500 ml of culture. After Ni-NTA purification and bdSUMO cleavage, the protein was purified by gel filtration (HiLoad 26/600 Superdex75 preparative grade column, GE).

anchors.snippet: To verify the molecular accuracy of the design process, we determined the structure of Des27.7 in the unbound form by crystallographic analysis (Extended Data Table 2; PDB 9HVB). All active-site positions aligned well with the design conception (less than $0 . 7 \mathring \mathrm { A }$ all-atom root mean squared deviation (r.m.s.d.) across 20 residues), including the catalytic Asp162, although a slight shift (r.m.s.d. 0.78 Å) was observed in the orientation of Phe113. Outside the active-site pocket, 180 of 257 positions aligned with backbone r.m.s.d. $< 0 . 6 \mathring \mathbf { A }$ , but 65 amino acids either deviated or did not exhibit significant electron density, probably due to backbone flexibility in this region (Extended Data Fig. 6a,b). This fragment is known to be dynamic in the IGPS protein family52, but it lies outside the active-site pocket and probably does not contribute directly to reactivity and substrate recognition. Taken together, our results verify a fully computational pipeline that designs an accurate de novo active site and generates a stable and high-efficiency new-to-nature enzyme.

As expected, designs that did not show cooperative unfolding lacked KE activity. We applied FuncLib to the active sites of six designs and tested 9–14 variants for each starting point. In five cases, catalytic efficiencies improved by 3–10-fold (Supplementary Table 1), with the highest catalytic efficiency reaching $3 0 0 \mathsf { M } ^ { - 1 } \mathsf { s } ^ { - 1 }$ (R2.Des39.2). We determined the crystallographic structure of two designs, R2.Des39 $( k _ { \mathrm { c a t } } / K _ { \mathrm { M } }$ $1 0 0 \mathsf { M } ^ { - 1 } \mathsf { s } ^ { - 1 } )$ and Des49 $( k _ { \mathrm { c a t } } / K _ { \mathrm { M } } 1 5 0 \mathsf { M } ^ { - 1 } \mathsf { s } ^ { - 1 } )$ (Extended Data Fig. 6c–i, PDB IDs 9HVH and 9HVG and Extended Data Table 2). The active sites were close to their design conceptions (r.m.s.d. $< 0 . 6$ Å and r.m.s.d. $< 0 . 8 2 \mathrm { \AA }$ , respectively), but, in both cases, several loops either lacked electron density or exhibited significant conformational changes compared with the designs, which could impede substrate entry to the active site53. To explore whether the foldability of these loops could be improved, we applied FuncLib to stabilize these regions according to the design models. Three of 16 FuncLib variants of Des39.2 showed a significant increase in catalytic efficiency, with improvements up to 20-fold compared with the original design, reaching $k _ { \mathrm { c a t } } / K _ { \mathrm { M } } 2 , 0 0 0 \mathsf { M } ^ { - 1 } \mathsf { s } ^ { - 1 }$ (Extended Data Table 1 and Supplementary Table 1), but none surpassed the performance of Des27.7. These results demonstrate that large-scale artificial intelligence-based structure prediction of natural enzymes provides a valuable resource for de novo enzyme design, and that the computational workflow reproducibly generates efficient enzymes. In this case, however, optimization with FuncLib reached superior catalytic parameters in the designs derived from modular assembly, which may reflect the greater structural diversity in these designs.

anchors.snippet: The designed genes were ordered from Twist Bioscience, cloned into pET28 plasmid with an N-terminal His-tag, followed by a bdSUMO tag. Plasmids were transformed into Escherichia coli BL21 (DE3) cells. For expression, $5 0 \mathrm { m l }$ of 2YT medium supplemented with $5 0 \mu \mathrm { g } \mathsf { m } ^ { - 1 }$ kanamycin was inoculated with $5 0 0 \mu \mathrm { l }$ of overnight culture produced from a single colony and grown at $3 7 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ until optical density $\left( \mathrm { O D } \right) _ { 6 0 0 }$ 0.6–0.8. Overexpression was induced by adding 1 mM IPTG and the cultures were grown for 20 h at $1 6 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ and collected, and the pellet was frozen at $- 2 0 { } ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ . The cells were resuspended in basic buffer $( 5 0 \mathsf { m } \mathsf { M }$ Tris-Cl pH 7.25, 200 mM NaCl) supplemented with $1 0 \mu \mathrm { g } \mathsf { m } ^ { - 1 }$ lysozyme, protease-inhibitor cocktail (Sigma) and benzonase, lysed by sonication and centrifuged at $2 0 { , } 0 0 0 g$ for 30 min a $4 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C } .$ . The soluble fraction was loaded onto an Ni-NTA (nitrilotriacetic acid) column and washed twice with basic buffer and $2 0 \mathrm { m M }$ imidazole. The protein was subjected to overnight on-column Sumo protease cleavage at $4 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ $S \mu \mathrm { g } \mathsf { m } \mathsf { l } ^ { - 1 }$ in basic buffer). Protein purity was assessed by SDS–PAGE. Protein concentration was determined using Pierce BCA protein assay kit. For crystallography, large-scale expression was performed in 1,500 ml of culture. After Ni-NTA purification and bdSUMO cleavage, the protein was purified by gel filtration (HiLoad 26/600 Superdex75 preparative grade column, GE).

Overexpression was induced by adding 1 mM IPTG and the cultures were grown for 20 h at $1 6 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ and collected, and the pellet was frozen at $- 2 0 { } ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ . The cells were resuspended in basic buffer $( 5 0 \mathsf { m } \mathsf { M }$ Tris-Cl pH 7.25, 200 mM NaCl) supplemented with $1 0 \mu \mathrm { g } \mathsf { m } ^ { - 1 }$ lysozyme, protease-inhibitor cocktail (Sigma) and benzonase, lysed by sonication and centrifuged at $2 0 { , } 0 0 0 g$ for 30 min a $4 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C } .$ . 237:

analysis_results.content: Reaction mechanism for base-catalyzed decomposition of 5-nitrobenzisoxazole: a base (B⁻) deprotonates the 3-position of 5-nitrobenzisoxazole, forming a negatively charged transition state with partial charges δ⁺ on the abstracted H and δ⁻ on the ring O, then ring opening produces 2-cyano-4-nitrophenolate anion and protonated base (BH).

Enzyme design workflow steps: 1) Modular assembly of protein domains, 2) Stability design using PROSS/TRP tool to optimize core residues, 3) Geometric matching and filtering using a logistic scoring function f = Π 1/(1 + e^((x-μ)/σ)) to match transition state geometry.

Enzyme design workflow steps: 4) Active site and core position stabilization, 5) Experimental screening including expression, purification, and kinetic assays (measuring absorbance at 380 nm as a function of substrate concentration), 6) Active-site optimization of residue positions to improve catalysis.

Bar chart of kcat/KM values (log10 scale, units M⁻¹ s⁻¹) for designed enzyme variants from Des27 to Des27.13. The highest performing variant is Des27.7 with kcat/KM ≈ 11000 M⁻¹ s⁻¹. All other variants have values ranging from ~20 M⁻¹ s⁻¹ (Des27.2) to ~4000 M⁻¹ s⁻¹ (Des27.12). A dashed line at 100 M⁻¹ s⁻¹ marks a threshold for activity.

Michaelis-Menten kinetic curve for the Des27.7 variant with 5-nitrobenisoxazole as substrate. Fitted kinetic parameters: K_M = 0.21 ± 0.06 mM, k_cat = 2.85 ± 1.2 s⁻¹, k_cat/K_M = 12696 ± 1738 M⁻¹ s⁻¹. The y-axis is initial rate V₀ in mOD min⁻¹, x-axis is substrate concentration in mM.

Crystal structure overlay of Des27.7 (gray, xtal) and the computational design model (light blue, model) showing active site residues: F113, I136, D162, V183, I216, V236, with bound substrate/transition state analog. The model closely matches the experimental crystal structure.

Sequence alignment of designed variants at positions 54, 84, 86, 92, 111, 115, 136, 183, 216, 236, with corresponding kcat/KM (M⁻¹ s⁻¹) and Asp162 van der Waals (vdW) energy (R.e.u) values. kcat/KM ranges from 21 M⁻¹ s⁻¹ (Des27.2) to 12696 M⁻¹ s⁻¹ (Des27.7). Asp162 vdW energy ranges from -5.5 to -4.9 R.e.u. Three groups of variants are separated by horizontal lines with increasing activity from top to bottom.

Conformational state distributions for Des27 and Des27.7: For Des27, 63% of states are unbound, 37% bound; of bound states, only 11% are reactive (in correct conformation), 66% are out of conformation, 22% other. For Des27.7, only 12% are unbound, 88% bound; of bound states, 23% are reactive (in correct conformation), 57% out, 19% other. Des27.7 has a ~6x higher fraction of reactive bound states than Des27.

Two bound conformations of 5-nitrobenzisoxazole in the active site: 'In' (productive, blue structure) where the 3-proton points toward the catalytic base D162, and 'Out' (non-productive, orange structure) where the substrate is flipped and the 3-proton faces away from D162.

Comparison of catalytic base packing in Des27 (left, green) and Des27.7 (right, blue): Des27 has leucines at positions 183 and 136 (L183, L136) and isoleucine at 216 (I216), while Des27.7 has valine at 183 (V183) and isoleucine at 136 (I136), creating tighter packing around the catalytic aspartate D162 to preorganize the active site for the reactive conformation.

Steric overlap relief in Des27.7: Des27 has leucine at position 236 (L236, left, green) which clashes with the substrate, while Des27.7 has valine at 236 (V236, right, blue) which removes the steric clash, allowing better substrate binding in the reactive conformation.

Ligand solvation comparison: Des27 (left, green) has phenylalanine at 92 (F92), leucine at 183 (L183), and isoleucine at 54 (I54) surrounding the substrate, while Des27.7 (right, blue) has histidine at 92 (H92), valine at 183 (V183), and valine at 54 (V54), improving solvation and stabilizing the bound substrate in the reactive conformation.

Activation free energy (ΔG‡, kcal mol⁻¹) for Des27 and Des27.7, comparing experimental values and calculated values for 'In' (reactive) and 'Out' (non-reactive) conformations: Des27 experimental ΔG‡ = 19.1 ± 0.2 kcal mol⁻¹, calculated Out = 17.6 ± 2.1 kcal mol⁻¹, calculated In = 16.7 ± 1.9 kcal mol⁻¹. Des27.7 experimental ΔG‡ = 16.7 ± 0.2 kcal mol⁻¹, calculated Out = 15.9 ± 1.4 kcal mol⁻¹, calculated In = 13.6 ± 0.4 kcal mol⁻¹. The experimental values align better with the Out conformation for both variants, but Des27.7 has ~2.4 kcal mol⁻¹ lower activation energy than Des27.

Stability (Tₘ, melting temperature in °C, left axis, yellow bars) and activity (kcat/KM, M⁻¹ s⁻¹, right log axis, blue bars) for variants with different combinations of design steps: Full design (all 4 steps: Modular assembly, PROSS (11 muts), Active site (15 muts), Core stabilization (6 muts)) gives Des27.7 with Tₘ = 86 °C and kcat/KM = 12700 M⁻¹ s⁻¹. The F113L mutant (additional active site mutation) has Tₘ = 82 °C and drastically improved kcat/KM = 123000 M⁻¹ s⁻¹. Variants missing individual design steps have lower or no detectable (ND) activity. The D162A mutant (catalytic base knockout) has Tₘ > 100 °C but no detectable activity, confirming D162 is the catalytic base.

anchors.snippet: Fig. 3 | Structural, energetic and dynamic contributions to the improved catalytic rate of Des27.7. a, Mutations (grey background) at positions 136, 216 and 236 trend with increasing catalytic efficiency, which also trends with Rosetta-computed vdW energy of the catalytic Asp162. Spearman $\rho { = } { - } 0 . 8 8$ , $P { = } 6 \times 1 0 ^ { - 5 }$ . b, Analysis of the structural basis of increased KE activity by comparing substrate-bound models of Des27 (left) and Des27.7 (right). c, Percentage of MD simulation time in which the substrate is within the active site for Des27 and Des27.7 (less than or equal to 4 Å between the substrate and the active site centre of mass; blue) or distant from the active site (wheat). For $2 4 \%$ of the time spent in the bound conformation the substrate adopts a reactive donor-acceptor geometry (highlighted arc). The bars on the right of each pie chart show the distribution of conformations (in, out and other) in the reactive mode. d, In MD simulations, 5-nitrobenzisoxazole (sticks) can assume two catalytically

payload.content: Reaction mechanism for base-catalyzed decomposition of 5-nitrobenzisoxazole: a base (B⁻) deprotonates the 3-position of 5-nitrobenzisoxazole, forming a negatively charged transition state with partial charges δ⁺ on the abstracted H and δ⁻ on the ring O, then ring opening produces 2-cyano-4-nitrophenolate anion and protonated base (BH).

Geometric matching43 and active-site (purple spheres) optimization with Rosetta yield millions of designs that are

payload.content: Enzyme design workflow steps: 1) Modular assembly of protein domains, 2) Stability design using PROSS/TRP tool to optimize core residues, 3) Geometric matching and filtering using a logistic scoring function f = Π 1/(1 + e^((x-μ)/σ)) to match transition state geometry.

payload.content: Bar chart of kcat/KM values (log10 scale, units M⁻¹ s⁻¹) for designed enzyme variants from Des27 to Des27.13. The highest performing variant is Des27.7 with kcat/KM ≈ 11000 M⁻¹ s⁻¹. All other variants have values ranging from ~20 M⁻¹ s⁻¹ (Des27.2) to ~4000 M⁻¹ s⁻¹ (Des27.12). A dashed line at 100 M⁻¹ s⁻¹ marks a threshold for activity.

payload.content: Michaelis-Menten kinetic curve for the Des27.7 variant with 5-nitrobenisoxazole as substrate. Fitted kinetic parameters: K_M = 0.21 ± 0.06 mM, k_cat = 2.85 ± 1.2 s⁻¹, k_cat/K_M = 12696 ± 1738 M⁻¹ s⁻¹. The y-axis is initial rate V₀ in mOD min⁻¹, x-axis is substrate concentration in mM.

payload.content: Sequence alignment of designed variants at positions 54, 84, 86, 92, 111, 115, 136, 183, 216, 236, with corresponding kcat/KM (M⁻¹ s⁻¹) and Asp162 van der Waals (vdW) energy (R.e.u) values. kcat/KM ranges from 21 M⁻¹ s⁻¹ (Des27.2) to 12696 M⁻¹ s⁻¹ (Des27.7). Asp162 vdW energy ranges from -5.5 to -4.9 R.e.u. Three groups of variants are separated by horizontal lines with increasing activity from top to bottom.

payload.content: Conformational state distributions for Des27 and Des27.7: For Des27, 63% of states are unbound, 37% bound; of bound states, only 11% are reactive (in correct conformation), 66% are out of conformation, 22% other. For Des27.7, only 12% are unbound, 88% bound; of bound states, 23% are reactive (in correct conformation), 57% out, 19% other. Des27.7 has a ~6x higher fraction of reactive bound states than Des27.

payload.content: Two bound conformations of 5-nitrobenzisoxazole in the active site: 'In' (productive, blue structure) where the 3-proton points toward the catalytic base D162, and 'Out' (non-productive, orange structure) where the substrate is flipped and the 3-proton faces away from D162.

payload.content: Comparison of catalytic base packing in Des27 (left, green) and Des27.7 (right, blue): Des27 has leucines at positions 183 and 136 (L183, L136) and isoleucine at 216 (I216), while Des27.7 has valine at 183 (V183) and isoleucine at 136 (I136), creating tighter packing around the catalytic aspartate D162 to preorganize the active site for the reactive conformation.

payload.content: Activation free energy (ΔG‡, kcal mol⁻¹) for Des27 and Des27.7, comparing experimental values and calculated values for 'In' (reactive) and 'Out' (non-reactive) conformations: Des27 experimental ΔG‡ = 19.1 ± 0.2 kcal mol⁻¹, calculated Out = 17.6 ± 2.1 kcal mol⁻¹, calculated In = 16.7 ± 1.9 kcal mol⁻¹. Des27.7 experimental ΔG‡ = 16.7 ± 0.2 kcal mol⁻¹, calculated Out = 15.9 ± 1.4 kcal mol⁻¹, calculated In = 13.6 ± 0.4 kcal mol⁻¹. The experimental values align better with the Out conformation for both variants, but Des27.7 has ~2.4 kcal mol⁻¹ lower activation energy than Des27.

payload.content: Stability (Tₘ, melting temperature in °C, left axis, yellow bars) and activity (kcat/KM, M⁻¹ s⁻¹, right log axis, blue bars) for variants with different combinations of design steps: Full design (all 4 steps: Modular assembly, PROSS (11 muts), Active site (15 muts), Core stabilization (6 muts)) gives Des27.7 with Tₘ = 86 °C and kcat/KM = 12700 M⁻¹ s⁻¹. The F113L mutant (additional active site mutation) has Tₘ = 82 °C and drastically improved kcat/KM = 123000 M⁻¹ s⁻¹. Variants missing individual design steps have lower or no detectable (ND) activity. The D162A mutant (catalytic base knockout) has Tₘ > 100 °C but no detectable activity, confirming D162 is the catalytic base.

analysis_results.content: Reaction mechanism for the ring opening of 5-nitrobenzisoxazole catalyzed by a designed enzyme: a base (B⁻) deprotonates the 3-position of the substrate, forming a negatively charged transition state with partial charges (δ⁺ on B, δ⁻ on O), followed by ring opening to produce 2-cyano-4-nitrophenolate and BH.

Enzyme design workflow: Step 1: Modular assembly of protein domains; Step 2: Stability design using PROSS/Trp tool; Step 3: Geometric matching and filtering using a sigmoidal filter function f = Π 1/(1 + e^((x-μ)/σ)) to evaluate active site geometry.

Enzyme design workflow continuation: Step 4: Active site and core position stabilization; Step 5: Experimental screening including protein expression, purification, and absorbance-based kinetic assays (measuring absorbance at 380 nm vs substrate concentration); Step 6: Active-site optimization of amino acid side chains.

Bar chart of kinetic efficiency kcat/KM (units: M⁻¹ s⁻¹, log10 Y-axis) for Des27 enzyme variants: Des27 (110), Des27.1 (290), Des27.2 (20), Des27.3 (1200), Des27.4 (50), Des27.5 (45), Des27.6 (80), Des27.7 (11000, highest value), Des27.9 (2000), Des27.10 (280), Des27.11 (550), Des27.12 (3500), Des27.13 (1800). Error bars represent standard deviation.

Michaelis-Menten kinetic plot for Des27.7 with 5-nitrobenzisoxazole as substrate: X-axis = [5-nitrobenzisoxazole] (mM), Y-axis = initial velocity V0 (mOD min⁻¹). Fitted kinetic parameters: KM = 0.21 ± 0.06 mM, kcat = 2.85 ± 1.2 s⁻¹, kcat/KM = 12696 ± 1738 M⁻¹ s⁻¹. Black dots are experimental measurements, black line is the fitted curve.

Active site structure overlay of Des27.7 crystal structure (Des27.7 xtal, gray) and computational model (Des27.7 model, light blue). Key active site residues labeled: F113, I136, D162, V183, I216, V236, with the substrate analog bound in the active site.

Sequence and activity table for Des27 variants: Columns show residues at positions 54, 84, 86, 92, 111, 115, 136, 183, 216, 236, followed by kcat/KM (M⁻¹ s⁻¹) and Asp162 van der Waals energy (R.e.u). The highest activity variant is Des27.7 with kcat/KM = 12696 M⁻¹ s⁻¹, followed by Des27.12 (3837), Des27.9 (2136), Des27.13 (2014), Des27.3 (1294), etc. The lowest activity variants are Des27.2 (21), Des27.5 (54), Des27.4 (59).

Binding state distribution pie charts for Des27 and Des27.7: Des27: 63% Unbound, remainder Bound; of bound reactive conformations: 11% In, 66% Out, 22% Other. Des27.7: 12% Unbound, 88% Bound; of bound reactive conformations: 23% In, 57% Out, 19% Other. Des27.7 has a much higher fraction of bound substrate than Des27.

Two substrate binding modes: 'In' (blue substrate, reactive orientation suitable for catalysis, nitro group facing the catalytic base) and 'Out' (orange substrate, non-reactive orientation, nitro group facing away from the catalytic base).

Comparison of catalytic base (D162) packing: Des27 model (green residues: I216, L183, L136) has suboptimal packing around D162; Des27.7 model (blue residues: I216, V183, I136) has improved packing stabilizing the D162 catalytic base.

Steric overlap comparison: Des27 model has L236 which causes steric clash with the substrate; Des27.7 model has V236 (smaller residue) which relieves steric overlap, allowing better substrate binding.

Ligand solvation comparison: Des27 model (green residues: L183, I54, F92) has more hydrophobic residues around the substrate leading to higher desolvation energy; Des27.7 model (blue residues: V183, V54, H92) has improved solvation properties reducing desolvation penalty for substrate binding.

Activation free energy (ΔG‡, kcal mol⁻¹) bar chart: Des27: Experimental ΔG‡ = 19.2 ± 0.2 kcal mol⁻¹, Out conformation ΔG‡ = 17.7 ± 2.2 kcal mol⁻¹, In conformation ΔG‡ = 16.7 ± 2.0 kcal mol⁻¹; Des27.7: Experimental ΔG‡ = 16.7 ± 0.2 kcal mol⁻¹, Out conformation ΔG‡ = 15.9 ± 1.5 kcal mol⁻¹, In conformation ΔG‡ = 13.6 ± 0.4 kcal mol⁻¹. Des27.7 has ~2.5 kcal mol⁻¹ lower experimental activation energy than Des27.

Protein stability (Tm, °C, left X-axis) and activity (kcat/KM, M⁻¹ s⁻¹, right X-axis, log scale) for design variants combining different optimization steps: Des27.7 (all steps: modular assembly + PROSS (11 muts) + active site (15 muts) + core stabilization (6 muts)) has Tm = 86 °C, kcat/KM = 12700 M⁻¹ s⁻¹. Variant missing PROSS: Tm = 57 °C, ND (no detectable activity). Variant missing active site: Tm = 69 °C, ND. Variant missing core stabilization: Tm = 60 °C, kcat/KM = 2900 M⁻¹ s⁻¹. D162A active site mutant: Tm >100 °C, ND. F113L mutant: Tm = 82 °C, kcat/KM = 123000 M⁻¹ s⁻¹ (10x higher activity than Des27.7).

payload.content: Protein stability (Tm, °C, left X-axis) and activity (kcat/KM, M⁻¹ s⁻¹, right X-axis, log scale) for design variants combining different optimization steps: Des27.7 (all steps: modular assembly + PROSS (11 muts) + active site (15 muts) + core stabilization (6 muts)) has Tm = 86 °C, kcat/KM = 12700 M⁻¹ s⁻¹. Variant missing PROSS: Tm = 57 °C, ND (no detectable activity). Variant missing active site: Tm = 69 °C, ND. Variant missing core stabilization: Tm = 60 °C, kcat/KM = 2900 M⁻¹ s⁻¹. D162A active site mutant: Tm >100 °C, ND. F113L mutant: Tm = 82 °C, kcat/KM = 123000 M⁻¹ s⁻¹ (10x higher activity than Des27.7).

anchors.snippet: The underlying reasons for the low efficiencies of de novo designed enzymes have been intensely studied10,17–19. These analyses revealed that the designed active sites exhibited significant structural distortions relative to the design conception17,19. Notably, catalysis is extremely sensitive to molecular details, and shifts of the catalytic constellation by a few degrees or tenths of an Ångstrom from optimality may translate into orders of magnitude decreases in efficiency20. Furthermore, designs often exhibited low stability and expressibility7 , limiting their ability to accommodate activity-enhancing mutations7,21. Further concerns were that fixed-backbone design methods fail to precisely position non-native catalytic groups1 ; the molecular details of the designed transition state (theozyme) were uncertain6,22; and that protein dynamics23 and long-range electrostatic interactions may be necessary to achieve high catalytic efficiency but are unaccounted for in the design process6,24,25.

25: The underlying reasons for the low efficiencies of de novo designed enzymes have been intensely studied10,17–19. These analyses revealed that the designed active sites exhibited significant structural distortions relative to the design conception17,19. Notably, catalysis is extremely sensitive to molecular details, and shifts of the catalytic constellation by a few degrees or tenths of an Ångstrom from optimality may translate into orders of magnitude decreases in efficiency20. Furthermore, designs often exhibited low stability and expressibility7 , limiting their ability to accommodate activity-enhancing mutations7,21. Further concerns were that fixed-backbone design methods fail to precisely position non-native catalytic groups1 ; the molecular details of the designed transition state (theozyme) were uncertain6,22; and that protein dynamics23 and long-range electrostatic interactions may be necessary to achieve high catalytic efficiency but are unaccounted for in the design process6,24,25.

anchors.snippet: Crystals were grown at $1 9 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ using the sitting-drop vapour diffusion method. Diffraction data were collected from a single crystal flash-cooled to 100 K, using a wavelength of 1.34 Å. Data were collected using an in-house Rigaku liquid-metal-jet X-ray Synergy System with a HyPix Arc $1 5 0 ^ { \circ }$ detector. AlphaFold2 (ref. 57) was used to generate all three models for molecular replacement (Extended Data Table 2). Initial models were iteratively rebuilt and refined using COOT63 and PHENIX64. Model geometry was evaluated using MOLPROBITY65. Atomic coordinates and structure factors for Des27.7, R2.Des39 and R2.Des49 are deposited in the PDB database under accession numbers 9HVB, 9HVH and 9HVG, respectively.

anchors.snippet: In total, 66 designs were solubly expressed and 14 showed cooperative thermal denaturation (Extended Data Fig. 1). Three designs showed measurable KE activity in an initial screen, with the top two designs, Des27 and Des61, exhibiting ${ k _ { \mathrm { { c a t } } } } / { K _ { \mathrm { { M } } } }$ values of 130 and 210 $\mathsf { M } ^ { - 1 } \mathsf { S } ^ { - 1 }$ , respectively, and $k _ { \mathrm { c a t } } < 1 \mathsf { s } ^ { - 1 }$ (Extended Data Fig. 2, Extended Data Table 1 and Supplementary Table 1).

Three designs showed measurable KE activity in an initial screen, with the top two designs, Des27 and Des61, exhibiting ${ k _ { \mathrm { { c a t } } } } / { K _ { \mathrm { { M } } } }$ values of 130 and 210 $\mathsf { M } ^ { - 1 } \mathsf { S } ^ { - 1 }$ , respectively, and $k _ { \mathrm { c a t } } < 1 \mathsf { s } ^ { - 1 }$ (Extended Data Fig. 2, Extended Data Table 1 and Supplementary Table 1).

anchors.snippet: ![](images/87ba402fa401b77021501bdaaa25aa50c1e19d7d4c2e6876106b43807cbfd2cc.jpg)
![](images/c050cf19414f34f5ebd2b5421852409f4498aa5da50dfc80c3bdf8c7a91985b7.jpg)
![](images/afa4269e903436cf6900e465de592ec293def3e51c2d6dffcd3079e75b520a62.jpg)

(20 designs) showed measurable KE activity with ${ k _ { \mathrm { { c a t } } } } / { K _ { \mathrm { { M } } } }$ in the range of $0 . 5 { - } 1 5 5 \mathbf { M } ^ { - 1 } \mathbf { s } ^ { - 1 }$ , demonstrating that the workflow can design stable and functional Kemp eliminases in a wide range of different starting points. As expected, designs that did not show cooperative unfolding lacked KE activity. We applied FuncLib to the active sites of six designs and tested 9–14 variants for each starting point. In five cases, catalytic efficiencies improved by 3–10-fold (Supplementary Table 1), with the highest catalytic efficiency reaching $3 0 0 \mathsf { M } ^ { - 1 } \mathsf { s } ^ { - 1 }$ (R2.Des39.2). We determined the crystallographic structure of two designs, R2.Des39 $( k _ { \mathrm { c a t } } / K _ { \mathrm { M } }$ $1 0 0 \mathsf { M } ^ { - 1 } \mathsf { s } ^ { - 1 } )$ and Des49 $( k _ { \mathrm { c a t } } / K _ { \mathrm { M } } 1 5 0 \mathsf { M } ^ { - 1 } \mathsf { s } ^ { - 1 } )$ (Extended Data Fig. 6c–i, PDB IDs 9HVH and 9HVG and Extended Data Table 2). The active sites were close to their design conceptions (r.m.s.d. $< 0 . 6$ Å and r.m.s.d. $< 0 . 8 2 \mathrm { \AA }$ , respectively), but, in both cases, several loops either lacked electron density or exhibited significant conformational changes compared with the designs, which could impede substrate entry to the active site53. To explore whether the foldability of these loops could be improved, we applied FuncLib to stabilize these regions according to the design models. Three of 16 FuncLib variants of Des39.2 showed a significant increase in catalytic efficiency, with improvements up to 20-fold compared with the original design, reaching $k _ { \mathrm { c a t } } / K _ { \mathrm { M } } 2 , 0 0 0 \mathsf { M } ^ { - 1 } \mathsf { s } ^ { - 1 }$ (Extended Data Table 1 and Supplementary Table 1), but none surpassed the performance of Des27.7. These results demonstrate that large-scale artificial intelligence-based structure prediction of natural enzymes provides a valuable resource for de novo enzyme design, and that the computational workflow reproducibly generates efficient enzymes. In this case, however, optimization with FuncLib reached superior catalytic parameters in the designs derived from modular assembly, which may reflect the greater structural diversity in these designs.

anchors.snippet: The designed genes were ordered from Twist Bioscience, cloned into pET28 plasmid with an N-terminal His-tag, followed by a bdSUMO tag. Plasmids were transformed into Escherichia coli BL21 (DE3) cells. For expression, $5 0 \mathrm { m l }$ of 2YT medium supplemented with $5 0 \mu \mathrm { g } \mathsf { m } ^ { - 1 }$ kanamycin was inoculated with $5 0 0 \mu \mathrm { l }$ of overnight culture produced from a single colony and grown at $3 7 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ until optical density $\left( \mathrm { O D } \right) _ { 6 0 0 }$ 0.6–0.8. Overexpression was induced by adding 1 mM IPTG and the cultures were grown for 20 h at $1 6 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ and collected, and the pellet was frozen at $- 2 0 { } ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ . The cells were resuspended in basic buffer $( 5 0 \mathsf { m } \mathsf { M }$ Tris-Cl pH 7.25, 200 mM NaCl) supplemented with $1 0 \mu \mathrm { g } \mathsf { m } ^ { - 1 }$ lysozyme, protease-inhibitor cocktail (Sigma) and benzonase, lysed by sonication and centrifuged at $2 0 { , } 0 0 0 g$ for 30 min a $4 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C } .$ . The soluble fraction was loaded onto an Ni-NTA (nitrilotriacetic acid) column and washed twice with basic buffer and $2 0 \mathrm { m M }$ imidazole. The protein was subjected to overnight on-column Sumo protease cleavage at $4 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ $S \mu \mathrm { g } \mathsf { m } \mathsf { l } ^ { - 1 }$ in basic buffer). Protein purity was assessed by SDS–PAGE. Protein concentration was determined using Pierce BCA protein assay kit. For crystallography, large-scale expression was performed in 1,500 ml of culture. After Ni-NTA purification and bdSUMO cleavage, the protein was purified by gel filtration (HiLoad 26/600 Superdex75 preparative grade column, GE).

anchors.snippet: To verify the molecular accuracy of the design process, we determined the structure of Des27.7 in the unbound form by crystallographic analysis (Extended Data Table 2; PDB 9HVB). All active-site positions aligned well with the design conception (less than $0 . 7 \mathring \mathrm { A }$ all-atom root mean squared deviation (r.m.s.d.) across 20 residues), including the catalytic Asp162, although a slight shift (r.m.s.d. 0.78 Å) was observed in the orientation of Phe113. Outside the active-site pocket, 180 of 257 positions aligned with backbone r.m.s.d. $< 0 . 6 \mathring \mathbf { A }$ , but 65 amino acids either deviated or did not exhibit significant electron density, probably due to backbone flexibility in this region (Extended Data Fig. 6a,b). This fragment is known to be dynamic in the IGPS protein family52, but it lies outside the active-site pocket and probably does not contribute directly to reactivity and substrate recognition. Taken together, our results verify a fully computational pipeline that designs an accurate de novo active site and generates a stable and high-efficiency new-to-nature enzyme.

anchors.snippet: Crystals were grown at $1 9 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ using the sitting-drop vapour diffusion method. Diffraction data were collected from a single crystal flash-cooled to 100 K, using a wavelength of 1.34 Å. Data were collected using an in-house Rigaku liquid-metal-jet X-ray Synergy System with a HyPix Arc $1 5 0 ^ { \circ }$ detector. AlphaFold2 (ref. 57) was used to generate all three models for molecular replacement (Extended Data Table 2). Initial models were iteratively rebuilt and refined using COOT63 and PHENIX64. Model geometry was evaluated using MOLPROBITY65. Atomic coordinates and structure factors for Des27.7, R2.Des39 and R2.Des49 are deposited in the PDB database under accession numbers 9HVB, 9HVH and 9HVG, respectively.

anchors.snippet: In total, 66 designs were solubly expressed and 14 showed cooperative thermal denaturation (Extended Data Fig. 1). Three designs showed measurable KE activity in an initial screen, with the top two designs, Des27 and Des61, exhibiting ${ k _ { \mathrm { { c a t } } } } / { K _ { \mathrm { { M } } } }$ values of 130 and 210 $\mathsf { M } ^ { - 1 } \mathsf { S } ^ { - 1 }$ , respectively, and $k _ { \mathrm { c a t } } < 1 \mathsf { s } ^ { - 1 }$ (Extended Data Fig. 2, Extended Data Table 1 and Supplementary Table 1).

anchors.snippet: The designed genes were ordered from Twist Bioscience, cloned into pET28 plasmid with an N-terminal His-tag, followed by a bdSUMO tag. Plasmids were transformed into Escherichia coli BL21 (DE3) cells. For expression, $5 0 \mathrm { m l }$ of 2YT medium supplemented with $5 0 \mu \mathrm { g } \mathsf { m } ^ { - 1 }$ kanamycin was inoculated with $5 0 0 \mu \mathrm { l }$ of overnight culture produced from a single colony and grown at $3 7 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ until optical density $\left( \mathrm { O D } \right) _ { 6 0 0 }$ 0.6–0.8. Overexpression was induced by adding 1 mM IPTG and the cultures were grown for 20 h at $1 6 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ and collected, and the pellet was frozen at $- 2 0 { } ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ . The cells were resuspended in basic buffer $( 5 0 \mathsf { m } \mathsf { M }$ Tris-Cl pH 7.25, 200 mM NaCl) supplemented with $1 0 \mu \mathrm { g } \mathsf { m } ^ { - 1 }$ lysozyme, protease-inhibitor cocktail (Sigma) and benzonase, lysed by sonication and centrifuged at $2 0 { , } 0 0 0 g$ for 30 min a $4 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C } .$ . The soluble fraction was loaded onto an Ni-NTA (nitrilotriacetic acid) column and washed twice with basic buffer and $2 0 \mathrm { m M }$ imidazole. The protein was subjected to overnight on-column Sumo protease cleavage at $4 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ $S \mu \mathrm { g } \mathsf { m } \mathsf { l } ^ { - 1 }$ in basic buffer). Protein purity was assessed by SDS–PAGE. Protein concentration was determined using Pierce BCA protein assay kit. For crystallography, large-scale expression was performed in 1,500 ml of culture. After Ni-NTA purification and bdSUMO cleavage, the protein was purified by gel filtration (HiLoad 26/600 Superdex75 preparative grade column, GE).

anchors.snippet: (20 designs) showed measurable KE activity with ${ k _ { \mathrm { { c a t } } } } / { K _ { \mathrm { { M } } } }$ in the range of $0 . 5 { - } 1 5 5 \mathbf { M } ^ { - 1 } \mathbf { s } ^ { - 1 }$ , demonstrating that the workflow can design stable and functional Kemp eliminases in a wide range of different starting points. As expected, designs that did not show cooperative unfolding lacked KE activity. We applied FuncLib to the active sites of six designs and tested 9–14 variants for each starting point. In five cases, catalytic efficiencies improved by 3–10-fold (Supplementary Table 1), with the highest catalytic efficiency reaching $3 0 0 \mathsf { M } ^ { - 1 } \mathsf { s } ^ { - 1 }$ (R2.Des39.2). We determined the crystallographic structure of two designs, R2.Des39 $( k _ { \mathrm { c a t } } / K _ { \mathrm { M } }$ $1 0 0 \mathsf { M } ^ { - 1 } \mathsf { s } ^ { - 1 } )$ and Des49 $( k _ { \mathrm { c a t } } / K _ { \mathrm { M } } 1 5 0 \mathsf { M } ^ { - 1 } \mathsf { s } ^ { - 1 } )$ (Extended Data Fig. 6c–i, PDB IDs 9HVH and 9HVG and Extended Data Table 2). The active sites were close to their design conceptions (r.m.s.d. $< 0 . 6$ Å and r.m.s.d. $< 0 . 8 2 \mathrm { \AA }$ , respectively), but, in both cases, several loops either lacked electron density or exhibited significant conformational changes compared with the designs, which could impede substrate entry to the active site53. To explore whether the foldability of these loops could be improved, we applied FuncLib to stabilize these regions according to the design models. Three of 16 FuncLib variants of Des39.2 showed a significant increase in catalytic efficiency, with improvements up to 20-fold compared with the original design, reaching $k _ { \mathrm { c a t } } / K _ { \mathrm { M } } 2 , 0 0 0 \mathsf { M } ^ { - 1 } \mathsf { s } ^ { - 1 }$ (Extended Data Table 1 and Supplementary Table 1), but none surpassed the performance of Des27.7. These results demonstrate that large-scale artificial intelligence-based structure prediction of natural enzymes provides a valuable resource for de novo enzyme design, and that the computational workflow reproducibly generates efficient enzymes. In this case, however, optimization with FuncLib reached superior catalytic parameters in the designs derived from modular assembly, which may reflect the greater structural diversity in these designs.

anchors.snippet: The underlying reasons for the low efficiencies of de novo designed enzymes have been intensely studied10,17–19. These analyses revealed that the designed active sites exhibited significant structural distortions relative to the design conception17,19. Notably, catalysis is extremely sensitive to molecular details, and shifts of the catalytic constellation by a few degrees or tenths of an Ångstrom from optimality may translate into orders of magnitude decreases in efficiency20. Furthermore, designs often exhibited low stability and expressibility7 , limiting their ability to accommodate activity-enhancing mutations7,21. Further concerns were that fixed-backbone design methods fail to precisely position non-native catalytic groups1 ; the molecular details of the designed transition state (theozyme) were uncertain6,22; and that protein dynamics23 and long-range electrostatic interactions may be necessary to achieve high catalytic efficiency but are unaccounted for in the design process6,24,25.

anchors.snippet: Crystals were grown at $1 9 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ using the sitting-drop vapour diffusion method. Diffraction data were collected from a single crystal flash-cooled to 100 K, using a wavelength of 1.34 Å. Data were collected using an in-house Rigaku liquid-metal-jet X-ray Synergy System with a HyPix Arc $1 5 0 ^ { \circ }$ detector. AlphaFold2 (ref. 57) was used to generate all three models for molecular replacement (Extended Data Table 2). Initial models were iteratively rebuilt and refined using COOT63 and PHENIX64. Model geometry was evaluated using MOLPROBITY65. Atomic coordinates and structure factors for Des27.7, R2.Des39 and R2.Des49 are deposited in the PDB database under accession numbers 9HVB, 9HVH and 9HVG, respectively.

anchors.snippet: In total, 66 designs were solubly expressed and 14 showed cooperative thermal denaturation (Extended Data Fig. 1). Three designs showed measurable KE activity in an initial screen, with the top two designs, Des27 and Des61, exhibiting ${ k _ { \mathrm { { c a t } } } } / { K _ { \mathrm { { M } } } }$ values of 130 and 210 $\mathsf { M } ^ { - 1 } \mathsf { S } ^ { - 1 }$ , respectively, and $k _ { \mathrm { c a t } } < 1 \mathsf { s } ^ { - 1 }$ (Extended Data Fig. 2, Extended Data Table 1 and Supplementary Table 1).

68: In total, 66 designs were solubly expressed and 14 showed cooperative thermal denaturation (Extended Data Fig. 1). Three designs showed measurable KE activity in an initial screen, with the top two designs, Des27 and Des61, exhibiting ${ k _ { \mathrm { { c a t } } } } / { K _ { \mathrm { { M } } } }$ values of 130 and 210 $\mathsf { M } ^ { - 1 } \mathsf { S } ^ { - 1 }$ , respectively, and $k _ { \mathrm { c a t } } < 1 \mathsf { s } ^ { - 1 }$ (Extended Data Fig. 2, Extended Data Table 1 and Supplementary Table 1).

anchors.snippet: ![](images/87ba402fa401b77021501bdaaa25aa50c1e19d7d4c2e6876106b43807cbfd2cc.jpg)
![](images/c050cf19414f34f5ebd2b5421852409f4498aa5da50dfc80c3bdf8c7a91985b7.jpg)
![](images/afa4269e903436cf6900e465de592ec293def3e51c2d6dffcd3079e75b520a62.jpg)

(20 designs) showed measurable KE activity with ${ k _ { \mathrm { { c a t } } } } / { K _ { \mathrm { { M } } } }$ in the range of $0 . 5 { - } 1 5 5 \mathbf { M } ^ { - 1 } \mathbf { s } ^ { - 1 }$ , demonstrating that the workflow can design stable and functional Kemp eliminases in a wide range of different starting points. As expected, designs that did not show cooperative unfolding lacked KE activity. We applied FuncLib to the active sites of six designs and tested 9–14 variants for each starting point. In five cases, catalytic efficiencies improved by 3–10-fold (Supplementary Table 1), with the highest catalytic efficiency reaching $3 0 0 \mathsf { M } ^ { - 1 } \mathsf { s } ^ { - 1 }$ (R2.Des39.2). We determined the crystallographic structure of two designs, R2.Des39 $( k _ { \mathrm { c a t } } / K _ { \mathrm { M } }$ $1 0 0 \mathsf { M } ^ { - 1 } \mathsf { s } ^ { - 1 } )$ and Des49 $( k _ { \mathrm { c a t } } / K _ { \mathrm { M } } 1 5 0 \mathsf { M } ^ { - 1 } \mathsf { s } ^ { - 1 } )$ (Extended Data Fig. 6c–i, PDB IDs 9HVH and 9HVG and Extended Data Table 2). The active sites were close to their design conceptions (r.m.s.d. $< 0 . 6$ Å and r.m.s.d. $< 0 . 8 2 \mathrm { \AA }$ , respectively), but, in both cases, several loops either lacked electron density or exhibited significant conformational changes compared with the designs, which could impede substrate entry to the active site53. To explore whether the foldability of these loops could be improved, we applied FuncLib to stabilize these regions according to the design models. Three of 16 FuncLib variants of Des39.2 showed a significant increase in catalytic efficiency, with improvements up to 20-fold compared with the original design, reaching $k _ { \mathrm { c a t } } / K _ { \mathrm { M } } 2 , 0 0 0 \mathsf { M } ^ { - 1 } \mathsf { s } ^ { - 1 }$ (Extended Data Table 1 and Supplementary Table 1), but none surpassed the performance of Des27.7. These results demonstrate that large-scale artificial intelligence-based structure prediction of natural enzymes provides a valuable resource for de novo enzyme design, and that the computational workflow reproducibly generates efficient enzymes. In this case, however, optimization with FuncLib reached superior catalytic parameters in the designs derived from modular assembly, which may reflect the greater structural diversity in these designs.

anchors.snippet: The designed genes were ordered from Twist Bioscience, cloned into pET28 plasmid with an N-terminal His-tag, followed by a bdSUMO tag. Plasmids were transformed into Escherichia coli BL21 (DE3) cells. For expression, $5 0 \mathrm { m l }$ of 2YT medium supplemented with $5 0 \mu \mathrm { g } \mathsf { m } ^ { - 1 }$ kanamycin was inoculated with $5 0 0 \mu \mathrm { l }$ of overnight culture produced from a single colony and grown at $3 7 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ until optical density $\left( \mathrm { O D } \right) _ { 6 0 0 }$ 0.6–0.8. Overexpression was induced by adding 1 mM IPTG and the cultures were grown for 20 h at $1 6 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ and collected, and the pellet was frozen at $- 2 0 { } ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ . The cells were resuspended in basic buffer $( 5 0 \mathsf { m } \mathsf { M }$ Tris-Cl pH 7.25, 200 mM NaCl) supplemented with $1 0 \mu \mathrm { g } \mathsf { m } ^ { - 1 }$ lysozyme, protease-inhibitor cocktail (Sigma) and benzonase, lysed by sonication and centrifuged at $2 0 { , } 0 0 0 g$ for 30 min a $4 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C } .$ . The soluble fraction was loaded onto an Ni-NTA (nitrilotriacetic acid) column and washed twice with basic buffer and $2 0 \mathrm { m M }$ imidazole. The protein was subjected to overnight on-column Sumo protease cleavage at $4 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ $S \mu \mathrm { g } \mathsf { m } \mathsf { l } ^ { - 1 }$ in basic buffer). Protein purity was assessed by SDS–PAGE. Protein concentration was determined using Pierce BCA protein assay kit. For crystallography, large-scale expression was performed in 1,500 ml of culture. After Ni-NTA purification and bdSUMO cleavage, the protein was purified by gel filtration (HiLoad 26/600 Superdex75 preparative grade column, GE).

anchors.snippet: The underlying reasons for the low efficiencies of de novo designed enzymes have been intensely studied10,17–19. These analyses revealed that the designed active sites exhibited significant structural distortions relative to the design conception17,19. Notably, catalysis is extremely sensitive to molecular details, and shifts of the catalytic constellation by a few degrees or tenths of an Ångstrom from optimality may translate into orders of magnitude decreases in efficiency20. Furthermore, designs often exhibited low stability and expressibility7 , limiting their ability to accommodate activity-enhancing mutations7,21. Further concerns were that fixed-backbone design methods fail to precisely position non-native catalytic groups1 ; the molecular details of the designed transition state (theozyme) were uncertain6,22; and that protein dynamics23 and long-range electrostatic interactions may be necessary to achieve high catalytic efficiency but are unaccounted for in the design process6,24,25.

Furthermore, designs often exhibited low stability and expressibility7 , limiting their ability to accommodate activity-enhancing mutations7,21. Further concerns were that fixed-backbone design methods fail to precisely position non-native catalytic groups1 ; the molecular details of the designed transition state (theozyme) were uncertain6,22; and that protein dynamics23 and long-range electrostatic interactions may be necessary to achieve high catalytic efficiency but are unaccounted for in the design process6,24,25.

To explore whether the foldability of these loops could be improved, we applied FuncLib to stabilize these regions according to the design models. Three of 16 FuncLib variants of Des39.2 showed a significant increase in catalytic efficiency, with improvements up to 20-fold compared with the original design, reaching $k _ { \mathrm { c a t } } / K _ { \mathrm { M } } 2 , 0 0 0 \mathsf { M } ^ { - 1 } \mathsf { s } ^ { - 1 }$ (Extended Data Table 1 and Supplementary Table 1), but none surpassed the performance of Des27.7. These results demonstrate that large-scale artificial intelligence-based structure prediction of natural enzymes provides a valuable resource for de novo enzyme design, and that the computational workflow reproducibly generates efficient enzymes. In this case, however, optimization with FuncLib reached superior catalytic parameters in the designs derived from modular assembly, which may reflect the greater structural diversity in these designs.

anchors.snippet: The designed genes were ordered from Twist Bioscience, cloned into pET28 plasmid with an N-terminal His-tag, followed by a bdSUMO tag. Plasmids were transformed into Escherichia coli BL21 (DE3) cells. For expression, $5 0 \mathrm { m l }$ of 2YT medium supplemented with $5 0 \mu \mathrm { g } \mathsf { m } ^ { - 1 }$ kanamycin was inoculated with $5 0 0 \mu \mathrm { l }$ of overnight culture produced from a single colony and grown at $3 7 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ until optical density $\left( \mathrm { O D } \right) _ { 6 0 0 }$ 0.6–0.8. Overexpression was induced by adding 1 mM IPTG and the cultures were grown for 20 h at $1 6 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ and collected, and the pellet was frozen at $- 2 0 { } ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ . The cells were resuspended in basic buffer $( 5 0 \mathsf { m } \mathsf { M }$ Tris-Cl pH 7.25, 200 mM NaCl) supplemented with $1 0 \mu \mathrm { g } \mathsf { m } ^ { - 1 }$ lysozyme, protease-inhibitor cocktail (Sigma) and benzonase, lysed by sonication and centrifuged at $2 0 { , } 0 0 0 g$ for 30 min a $4 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C } .$ . The soluble fraction was loaded onto an Ni-NTA (nitrilotriacetic acid) column and washed twice with basic buffer and $2 0 \mathrm { m M }$ imidazole. The protein was subjected to overnight on-column Sumo protease cleavage at $4 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ $S \mu \mathrm { g } \mathsf { m } \mathsf { l } ^ { - 1 }$ in basic buffer). Protein purity was assessed by SDS–PAGE. Protein concentration was determined using Pierce BCA protein assay kit. For crystallography, large-scale expression was performed in 1,500 ml of culture. After Ni-NTA purification and bdSUMO cleavage, the protein was purified by gel filtration (HiLoad 26/600 Superdex75 preparative grade column, GE).

anchors.snippet: The underlying reasons for the low efficiencies of de novo designed enzymes have been intensely studied10,17–19. These analyses revealed that the designed active sites exhibited significant structural distortions relative to the design conception17,19. Notably, catalysis is extremely sensitive to molecular details, and shifts of the catalytic constellation by a few degrees or tenths of an Ångstrom from optimality may translate into orders of magnitude decreases in efficiency20. Furthermore, designs often exhibited low stability and expressibility7 , limiting their ability to accommodate activity-enhancing mutations7,21. Further concerns were that fixed-backbone design methods fail to precisely position non-native catalytic groups1 ; the molecular details of the designed transition state (theozyme) were uncertain6,22; and that protein dynamics23 and long-range electrostatic interactions may be necessary to achieve high catalytic efficiency but are unaccounted for in the design process6,24,25.

anchors.snippet: Crystals were grown at $1 9 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ using the sitting-drop vapour diffusion method. Diffraction data were collected from a single crystal flash-cooled to 100 K, using a wavelength of 1.34 Å. Data were collected using an in-house Rigaku liquid-metal-jet X-ray Synergy System with a HyPix Arc $1 5 0 ^ { \circ }$ detector. AlphaFold2 (ref. 57) was used to generate all three models for molecular replacement (Extended Data Table 2). Initial models were iteratively rebuilt and refined using COOT63 and PHENIX64. Model geometry was evaluated using MOLPROBITY65. Atomic coordinates and structure factors for Des27.7, R2.Des39 and R2.Des49 are deposited in the PDB database under accession numbers 9HVB, 9HVH and 9HVG, respectively.

anchors.snippet: To verify the molecular accuracy of the design process, we determined the structure of Des27.7 in the unbound form by crystallographic analysis (Extended Data Table 2; PDB 9HVB). All active-site positions aligned well with the design conception (less than $0 . 7 \mathring \mathrm { A }$ all-atom root mean squared deviation (r.m.s.d.) across 20 residues), including the catalytic Asp162, although a slight shift (r.m.s.d. 0.78 Å) was observed in the orientation of Phe113. Outside the active-site pocket, 180 of 257 positions aligned with backbone r.m.s.d. $< 0 . 6 \mathring \mathbf { A }$ , but 65 amino acids either deviated or did not exhibit significant electron density, probably due to backbone flexibility in this region (Extended Data Fig. 6a,b). This fragment is known to be dynamic in the IGPS protein family52, but it lies outside the active-site pocket and probably does not contribute directly to reactivity and substrate recognition. Taken together, our results verify a fully computational pipeline that designs an accurate de novo active site and generates a stable and high-efficiency new-to-nature enzyme.
anchors.excerpt:
101: 
102: To verify the molecular accuracy of the design process, we determined the structure of Des27.7 in the unbound form by crystallographic analysis (Extended Data Table 2; PDB 9HVB). All active-site positions aligned well with the design conception (less than $0 . 7 \mathring \mathrm { A }$ all-atom root mean squared deviation (r.m.s.d.) across 20 residues), including the catalytic Asp162, although a slight shift (r.m.s.d. 0.78 Å) was observed in the orientation of Phe113. Outside the active-site pocket, 180 of 257 positions aligned with backbone r.m.s.d. $< 0 . 6 \mathring \mathbf { A }$ , but 65 amino acids either deviated or did not exhibit significant electron density, probably due to backbone flexibility in this region (Extended Data Fig. 6a,b). This fragment is known to be dynamic in the IGPS protein family52, but it lies outside the active-site pocket and probably does not contribute directly to reactivity and substrate recognition. Taken together, our results verify a fully computational pipeline that designs an accurate de novo active site and generates a stable and high-efficiency new-to-nature enzyme.

anchors.snippet: Crystals were grown at $1 9 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ using the sitting-drop vapour diffusion method. Diffraction data were collected from a single crystal flash-cooled to 100 K, using a wavelength of 1.34 Å. Data were collected using an in-house Rigaku liquid-metal-jet X-ray Synergy System with a HyPix Arc $1 5 0 ^ { \circ }$ detector. AlphaFold2 (ref. 57) was used to generate all three models for molecular replacement (Extended Data Table 2). Initial models were iteratively rebuilt and refined using COOT63 and PHENIX64. Model geometry was evaluated using MOLPROBITY65. Atomic coordinates and structure factors for Des27.7, R2.Des39 and R2.Des49 are deposited in the PDB database under accession numbers 9HVB, 9HVH and 9HVG, respectively.

anchors.snippet: In total, 66 designs were solubly expressed and 14 showed cooperative thermal denaturation (Extended Data Fig. 1). Three designs showed measurable KE activity in an initial screen, with the top two designs, Des27 and Des61, exhibiting ${ k _ { \mathrm { { c a t } } } } / { K _ { \mathrm { { M } } } }$ values of 130 and 210 $\mathsf { M } ^ { - 1 } \mathsf { S } ^ { - 1 }$ , respectively, and $k _ { \mathrm { c a t } } < 1 \mathsf { s } ^ { - 1 }$ (Extended Data Fig. 2, Extended Data Table 1 and Supplementary Table 1).

anchors.snippet: The designed genes were ordered from Twist Bioscience, cloned into pET28 plasmid with an N-terminal His-tag, followed by a bdSUMO tag. Plasmids were transformed into Escherichia coli BL21 (DE3) cells. For expression, $5 0 \mathrm { m l }$ of 2YT medium supplemented with $5 0 \mu \mathrm { g } \mathsf { m } ^ { - 1 }$ kanamycin was inoculated with $5 0 0 \mu \mathrm { l }$ of overnight culture produced from a single colony and grown at $3 7 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ until optical density $\left( \mathrm { O D } \right) _ { 6 0 0 }$ 0.6–0.8. Overexpression was induced by adding 1 mM IPTG and the cultures were grown for 20 h at $1 6 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ and collected, and the pellet was frozen at $- 2 0 { } ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ . The cells were resuspended in basic buffer $( 5 0 \mathsf { m } \mathsf { M }$ Tris-Cl pH 7.25, 200 mM NaCl) supplemented with $1 0 \mu \mathrm { g } \mathsf { m } ^ { - 1 }$ lysozyme, protease-inhibitor cocktail (Sigma) and benzonase, lysed by sonication and centrifuged at $2 0 { , } 0 0 0 g$ for 30 min a $4 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C } .$ . The soluble fraction was loaded onto an Ni-NTA (nitrilotriacetic acid) column and washed twice with basic buffer and $2 0 \mathrm { m M }$ imidazole. The protein was subjected to overnight on-column Sumo protease cleavage at $4 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ $S \mu \mathrm { g } \mathsf { m } \mathsf { l } ^ { - 1 }$ in basic buffer). Protein purity was assessed by SDS–PAGE. Protein concentration was determined using Pierce BCA protein assay kit. For crystallography, large-scale expression was performed in 1,500 ml of culture. After Ni-NTA purification and bdSUMO cleavage, the protein was purified by gel filtration (HiLoad 26/600 Superdex75 preparative grade column, GE).

anchors.snippet: (20 designs) showed measurable KE activity with ${ k _ { \mathrm { { c a t } } } } / { K _ { \mathrm { { M } } } }$ in the range of $0 . 5 { - } 1 5 5 \mathbf { M } ^ { - 1 } \mathbf { s } ^ { - 1 }$ , demonstrating that the workflow can design stable and functional Kemp eliminases in a wide range of different starting points. As expected, designs that did not show cooperative unfolding lacked KE activity. We applied FuncLib to the active sites of six designs and tested 9–14 variants for each starting point. In five cases, catalytic efficiencies improved by 3–10-fold (Supplementary Table 1), with the highest catalytic efficiency reaching $3 0 0 \mathsf { M } ^ { - 1 } \mathsf { s } ^ { - 1 }$ (R2.Des39.2). We determined the crystallographic structure of two designs, R2.Des39 $( k _ { \mathrm { c a t } } / K _ { \mathrm { M } }$ $1 0 0 \mathsf { M } ^ { - 1 } \mathsf { s } ^ { - 1 } )$ and Des49 $( k _ { \mathrm { c a t } } / K _ { \mathrm { M } } 1 5 0 \mathsf { M } ^ { - 1 } \mathsf { s } ^ { - 1 } )$ (Extended Data Fig. 6c–i, PDB IDs 9HVH and 9HVG and Extended Data Table 2). The active sites were close to their design conceptions (r.m.s.d. $< 0 . 6$ Å and r.m.s.d. $< 0 . 8 2 \mathrm { \AA }$ , respectively), but, in both cases, several loops either lacked electron density or exhibited significant conformational changes compared with the designs, which could impede substrate entry to the active site53. To explore whether the foldability of these loops could be improved, we applied FuncLib to stabilize these regions according to the design models. Three of 16 FuncLib variants of Des39.2 showed a significant increase in catalytic efficiency, with improvements up to 20-fold compared with the original design, reaching $k _ { \mathrm { c a t } } / K _ { \mathrm { M } } 2 , 0 0 0 \mathsf { M } ^ { - 1 } \mathsf { s } ^ { - 1 }$ (Extended Data Table 1 and Supplementary Table 1), but none surpassed the performance of Des27.7. These results demonstrate that large-scale artificial intelligence-based structure prediction of natural enzymes provides a valuable resource for de novo enzyme design, and that the computational workflow reproducibly generates efficient enzymes. In this case, however, optimization with FuncLib reached superior catalytic parameters in the designs derived from modular assembly, which may reflect the greater structural diversity in these designs.

analysis_results.content: Reaction mechanism for base-catalyzed ring opening of 5-nitrobenzisoxazole: a base B⁻ deprotonates the 3-position of the substrate, forming a cyclic transition state with partial charges (δ⁺ on B, δ⁻ on O), leading to product 2-cyano-4-nitrophenolate (anionic phenol with a cyano group ortho to O⁻ and para nitro group) and BH.

3-step initial design workflow: 1) Modular assembly of protein domains (colored surface representation), 2) Stability design using PROSS TRP tool (pink spheres indicating positions modified for stability, on gray protein backbone), 3) Geometric matching and filtering using a logistic scoring function f = Π 1/(1 + e^((x-μ)/σ)), with a bound substrate/ligand active site shown in the inset, purple spheres indicating geometrically matched positions.

3-step final design workflow: 4) Active site and core position stabilization (purple spheres: active site/core positions, green spheres: surrounding stabilization positions, on gray backbone), 5) Experimental characterization: expression, purification, and kinetic screening via absorbance at 380 nm vs [substrate] Michaelis-Menten plots in microwell plates, 6) Active-site optimization (multiple colored amino acid sidechains in the active site, indicating mutational optimization).

Bar chart of catalytic efficiency kcat/KM (log scale, M⁻¹ s⁻¹) for Des27 variants: Des27: 110, Des27.1: 300, Des27.2: 20, Des27.3: 1100, Des27.4: 50, Des27.5: 45, Des27.6: 80, Des27.7: 11000 (highest value), Des27.9: 2000, Des27.10: 280, Des27.11: 550, Des27.12: 3500, Des27.13: 1700. Y-axis is log scale from 10² to 10⁴ M⁻¹ s⁻¹.

Michaelis-Menten plot for Des27.7 with 5-nitrobenzisoxazole substrate: X-axis [5-nitrobenzoisoxazole] (mM, 0 to 1.0), Y-axis initial velocity V0 (mOD min⁻¹, 0 to 50). Fitted kinetic parameters: KM = 0.21 ± 0.06 mM, kcat = 2.85 ± 1.2 s⁻¹, kcat/KM = 12696 ± 1738 M⁻¹ s⁻¹, matching the bar chart value for Des27.7.

Active site structural overlay for Des27.7: white sticks = crystal structure (xtal), light blue sticks = computational model. Key active site residues labeled: F113, I136, D162 (catalytic base), V183, I216, V236. Substrate/ligand is bound in the active site, showing close alignment between model and crystal structure.

Sequence alignment table for Des27 variants, showing amino acid identities at positions 54, 84, 86, 92, 111, 115, 136, 183, 216, 236, plus kcat/KM (M⁻¹ s⁻¹) and Asp162 van der Waals (vdW) energy (R.e.u). Rows are ordered by increasing kcat/KM: Des27.2: 21 M⁻¹ s⁻¹, vdW -4.9; Des27.5: 54, -4.9; Des27.4: 59, -4.9; Des27.6: 90, -4.9; Des27: 130, -5.1; Des27.10: 327, -5.2; Des27.1: 344, -5.2; Des27.11: 718, -5.2; Des27.3: 1294, -5.2; Des27.13: 2014, -5.1; Des27.9: 2136, -5.5; Des27.12: 3837, -5.4; Des27.7: 12696, -5.4. Higher kcat/KM variants have more negative (favorable) Asp162 vdW energy.

Binding state population pie charts and reactive conformation breakdown for Des27 vs Des27.7: Des27: 63% Unbound, 37% Bound; of bound states: 11% reactive 'In' conformation, 66% 'Out' nonreactive conformation, 22% other. Des27.7: 12% Unbound, 88% Bound; of bound states: 23% reactive 'In' conformation, 57% 'Out' nonreactive conformation, 19% other. Des27.7 has higher bound population and higher fraction of reactive bound conformations.

Structures of the two bound conformations: 'In' (blue ligand) is the catalytically productive conformation with the 3-proton facing the D162 catalytic base; 'Out' (orange ligand) is nonproductive with the reactive proton facing away from the base.

Comparison of catalytic base (D162) packing in Des27 model (green residues) vs Des27.7 model (blue residues): Des27 has L136, L183, I216 around D162; Des27.7 has I136, V183, I216, with tighter packing to fix D162 in a catalytically favorable orientation.

Steric overlap relief comparison: Des27 model has L236 which clashes with the bound substrate; Des27.7 has V236 (smaller sidechain) which eliminates steric clash with the substrate, allowing better binding.
Ligand solvation comparison: Des27 model has I54, L183, F92 surrounding the bound substrate; Des27.7 has V54, V183, H92 which optimizes solvation and binding interactions with the substrate.

Activation energy ΔG‡ (kcal mol⁻¹) bar chart for Des27 and Des27.7: Des27 experimental ΔG‡ = 19.1 ± 0.2; Out (nonreactive) conformation ΔG‡ = 17.7 ± 2.2; In (reactive) conformation ΔG‡ = 16.7 ± 2.1. Des27.7 experimental ΔG‡ = 16.7 ± 0.2; Out ΔG‡ = 15.9 ± 1.5; In ΔG‡ = 13.6 ± 0.4. The reactive 'In' conformation of Des27.7 has the lowest activation energy, matching experimental values closely.

Dual-axis plot for variants with different design steps: left axis = melting temperature Tm (°C, reverse scale: 100 to 60), right axis = kcat/KM (M⁻¹ s⁻¹, log scale 10³ to 10⁵). Des27.7 (all design steps included: modular assembly, PROSS 11 muts, active site 15 muts, core stabilization 6 muts): Tm = 86 °C, kcat/KM = 12700 M⁻¹ s⁻¹. Variant missing PROSS: Tm = 57 °C, no detectable activity (ND). Variant missing active site: Tm = 69 °C, ND. Variant missing PROSS + active site: Tm = 60 °C, kcat/KM = 2900 M⁻¹ s⁻¹. Variant missing core stabilization: Tm = 81 °C, kcat/KM = 11500 M⁻¹ s⁻¹. D162A catalytic dead mutant: Tm > 100 °C, ND. F113L mutant: Tm = 82 °C, kcat/KM = 123000 M⁻¹ s⁻¹ (highest activity). Beige bars = Tm, blue bars = kcat/KM, ND = no detectable activity.

payload.content: Reaction mechanism for base-catalyzed ring opening of 5-nitrobenzisoxazole: a base B⁻ deprotonates the 3-position of the substrate, forming a cyclic transition state with partial charges (δ⁺ on B, δ⁻ on O), leading to product 2-cyano-4-nitrophenolate (anionic phenol with a cyano group ortho to O⁻ and para nitro group) and BH.

payload.content: 3-step initial design workflow: 1) Modular assembly of protein domains (colored surface representation), 2) Stability design using PROSS TRP tool (pink spheres indicating positions modified for stability, on gray protein backbone), 3) Geometric matching and filtering using a logistic scoring function f = Π 1/(1 + e^((x-μ)/σ)), with a bound substrate/ligand active site shown in the inset, purple spheres indicating geometrically matched positions.

payload.content: Activation energy ΔG‡ (kcal mol⁻¹) bar chart for Des27 and Des27.7: Des27 experimental ΔG‡ = 19.1 ± 0.2; Out (nonreactive) conformation ΔG‡ = 17.7 ± 2.2; In (reactive) conformation ΔG‡ = 16.7 ± 2.1. Des27.7 experimental ΔG‡ = 16.7 ± 0.2; Out ΔG‡ = 15.9 ± 1.5; In ΔG‡ = 13.6 ± 0.4. The reactive 'In' conformation of Des27.7 has the lowest activation energy, matching experimental values closely.

payload.content: Dual-axis plot for variants with different design steps: left axis = melting temperature Tm (°C, reverse scale: 100 to 60), right axis = kcat/KM (M⁻¹ s⁻¹, log scale 10³ to 10⁵). Des27.7 (all design steps included: modular assembly, PROSS 11 muts, active site 15 muts, core stabilization 6 muts): Tm = 86 °C, kcat/KM = 12700 M⁻¹ s⁻¹. Variant missing PROSS: Tm = 57 °C, no detectable activity (ND). Variant missing active site: Tm = 69 °C, ND. Variant missing PROSS + active site: Tm = 60 °C, kcat/KM = 2900 M⁻¹ s⁻¹. Variant missing core stabilization: Tm = 81 °C, kcat/KM = 11500 M⁻¹ s⁻¹. D162A catalytic dead mutant: Tm > 100 °C, ND. F113L mutant: Tm = 82 °C, kcat/KM = 123000 M⁻¹ s⁻¹ (highest activity). Beige bars = Tm, blue bars = kcat/KM, ND = no detectable activity.
