427: we observe ligand dissociation without rebinding towards the end of the trajectory on the timescale of our simulations; in a third, in the case of Des27.7, we observe substantial dissociation mid-trajectory with ligand rebinding within the simulation timeframes.

analysis_results.content: Reaction mechanism for base-catalyzed ring opening of 5-nitrobenzisoxazole: a base (B⁻) deprotonates the 3-position of the substrate, forming a negatively charged transition state with partial bond formation between H and B (δ⁺ on B side) and partial negative charge on the ring oxygen (δ⁻), leading to formation of 2-cyano-4-nitrophenolate product and BH.

Enzyme design workflow: Step 1 Modular assembly of protein domains; Step 2 Stability design using PROSS/TRP tool to introduce stabilizing mutations; Step 3 Geometric matching and filtering using a sigmoidal scoring function: f = Π 1/(1 + e^((x-μ)/σ)) to match substrate binding geometry.

Enzyme design workflow continued: Step 4 Active site and core position stabilization; Step 5 Experimental screening: protein expression, purification, and activity assay via absorbance at 380 nm as a function of substrate concentration; Step 6 Active-site optimization of amino acid residues around the substrate.

Bar plot of kcat/KM (M⁻¹ s⁻¹, log₁₀ y-axis) for designed 5-nitrobenzisoxazole active enzyme variants. X-axis labels: Des27, Des27.1, Des27.2, Des27.3, Des27.4, Des27.5, Des27.6, Des27.7, Des27.9, Des27.10, Des27.11, Des27.12, Des27.13. The highest activity variant is Des27.7 with kcat/KM ~10,000 M⁻¹ s⁻¹, ~100-fold higher than the initial Des27 variant (kcat/KM ~100 M⁻¹ s⁻¹).

Michaelis-Menten kinetics plot for Des27.7 with 5-nitrobenziosoxazole as substrate: x-axis is [5-nitrobenzisoxazole] (mM), y-axis is V₀ (mOD min⁻¹). Fitted kinetic parameters: KM = 0.21 ± 0.06 mM, kcat = 2.85 ± 1.2 s⁻¹, kcat/KM = 12,696 ± 1,738 M⁻¹ s⁻¹.
Active site superposition of Des27.7 crystal structure (xtal, light grey) and designed model (light blue). Key active site residues labeled: F113, I136, D162 (catalytic base), V183, I216, V236.

Sequence and activity table for designed variants: columns are residue positions 54, 84, 86, 92, 111, 115, 136, 183, 216, 236, followed by kcat/KM (M⁻¹ s⁻¹) and Asp162 van der Waals energy (R.e.u). Variants ordered by increasing kcat/KM: Des27.2 (21), Des27.5 (54), Des27.4 (59), Des27.6 (90), Des27 (130), Des27.10 (327), Des27.1 (344), Des27.11 (718), Des27.3 (1,294), Des27.13 (2,014), Des27.9 (2,136), Des27.12 (3,837), Des27.7 (12,696). Lower (more negative) Asp162 vdW energy correlates with higher activity for improved variants.

Pie charts and stacked bar plots for substrate binding state distributions: Des27: 63% Unbound, 37% Bound; of bound conformations 11% are reactive (In, catalytically productive), 66% are nonproductive (Out), 22% Other. Des27.7: 12% Unbound, 88% Bound; of bound conformations 23% are reactive (In), 57% nonproductive (Out), 19% Other. Des27.7 has a ~4x higher fraction of reactive bound substrate than Des27.
Substrate binding modes: 'In' (productive, nitro group oriented towards catalytic base D162, blue structure) and 'Out' (nonproductive, nitro group oriented away from D162, orange structure).

Comparison of catalytic base (D162) packing: Des27 model (green residues) has L183, L136, I216; Des27.7 model (blue residues) has V183, I136, I216, improving packing around D162 to preorganize the active site for catalysis.
Steric relief optimization: Des27 has L236 which causes steric overlap with the substrate; Des27.7 has V236, relieving this steric clash to improve substrate binding.
Ligand solvation optimization: Des27 has F92, L183, I54 around the substrate; Des27.7 has H92, V183, V54, optimizing solvation and binding interactions with the substrate.

Activation energy (ΔG‡, kcal mol⁻¹) bar plot: Des27 experimental ΔG‡ = 19.2 ± 0.2 kcal mol⁻¹, modeled 'Out' conformation ΔG‡ = 17.7 ± 2.2 kcal mol⁻¹, modeled 'In' conformation ΔG‡ = 16.7 ± 2.1 kcal mol⁻¹. Des27.7 experimental ΔG‡ = 16.7 ± 0.2 kcal mol⁻¹, modeled 'Out' ΔG‡ = 15.9 ± 1.4 kcal mol⁻¹, modeled 'In' ΔG‡ = 13.6 ± 0.4 kcal mol⁻¹. The experimental activation energy for Des27.7 is ~2.5 kcal mol⁻¹ lower than Des27, consistent with higher catalytic activity.

Stability (Tm, °C, left x-axis, decreasing) and activity (kcat/KM, M⁻¹ s⁻¹, right log x-axis, increasing) for variants from different design stages: Des27.7 (all design steps applied: modular assembly + PROSS 11 muts + active site 15 muts + core stabilization 6 muts) has Tm = 86 °C, kcat/KM = 12,700 M⁻¹ s⁻¹. Variant with only modular assembly has Tm = 57 °C, ND activity. Variant with modular assembly + PROSS has Tm = 69 °C, ND activity. Variant with modular assembly + PROSS + active site design has Tm = 60 °C, kcat/KM = 2,900 M⁻¹ s⁻¹. Variant with modular assembly + PROSS + active site design (no core stabilization) has Tm = 81 °C, kcat/KM = 11,500 M⁻¹ s⁻¹. D162A catalytic dead variant has Tm > 100 °C, ND activity. F113L further optimized variant has Tm = 82 °C, kcat/KM = 123,000 M⁻¹ s⁻¹ ~10x higher than Des27.7.

anchors.snippet: Fig. 3 | Structural, energetic and dynamic contributions to the improved catalytic rate of Des27.7. a, Mutations (grey background) at positions 136, 216 and 236 trend with increasing catalytic efficiency, which also trends with Rosetta-computed vdW energy of the catalytic Asp162. Spearman $\rho { = } { - } 0 . 8 8$ , $P { = } 6 \times 1 0 ^ { - 5 }$ . b, Analysis of the structural basis of increased KE activity by comparing substrate-bound models of Des27 (left) and Des27.7 (right). c, Percentage of MD simulation time in which the substrate is within the active site for Des27 and Des27.7 (less than or equal to 4 Å between the substrate and the active site centre of mass; blue) or distant from the active site (wheat). For $2 4 \%$ of the time spent in the bound conformation the substrate adopts a reactive donor-acceptor geometry (highlighted arc). payload.content: Stability (Tm, °C, left x-axis, decreasing) and activity (kcat/KM, M⁻¹ s⁻¹, right log x-axis, increasing) for variants from different design stages: Des27.7 (all design steps applied: modular assembly + PROSS 11 muts + active site 15 muts + core stabilization 6 muts) has Tm = 86 °C, kcat/KM = 12,700 M⁻¹ s⁻¹. Variant with only modular assembly has Tm = 57 °C, ND activity. Variant with modular assembly + PROSS has Tm = 69 °C, ND activity. Variant with modular assembly + PROSS + active site design has Tm = 60 °C, kcat/KM = 2,900 M⁻¹ s⁻¹. Variant with modular assembly + PROSS + active site design (no core stabilization) has Tm = 81 °C, kcat/KM = 11,500 M⁻¹ s⁻¹. D162A catalytic dead variant has Tm > 100 °C, ND activity. F113L further optimized variant has Tm = 82 °C, kcat/KM = 123,000 M⁻¹ s⁻¹ ~10x higher than Des27.7.

analysis_results.content: Reaction mechanism for base-catalyzed ring opening of 5-nitrobenzisoxazole: a base (B⁻) deprotonates the 3-position of 5-nitrobenzisoxazole, forming a negatively charged transition state with partial B-H bond formation and partial C-O bond cleavage, producing the final product 2-cyano-4-nitrophenolate and BH.

Enzyme design workflow: Step 1: Modular assembly of protein domains. Step 2: Stability design using the PROSS TPR method (shown with a wrench icon, optimizing 12 initial positions marked with pink spheres). Step 3: Geometric matching and filtering, using a sigmoidal scoring function f = Π 1/(1 + e^((x-μ)/σ)) to filter active site geometries, resulting in purple spheres for selected positions. An inset shows the target reactive conformation of the ligand with a nearby tryptophan residue.

Continued design workflow: Step 4: Active site and core position stabilization, with green spheres marking additional optimized positions. Step 5: Experimental screening: protein expression followed by purification, then kinetic assays measuring absorbance at 380 nm as a function of substrate concentration to evaluate activity. Step 6: Active-site optimization of amino acid side chains to improve catalysis.

Logarithmic-scale bar chart of catalytic efficiency (kcat/KM, units: M⁻¹ s⁻¹) for Des27 and its variants. X-axis labels: Des27, Des27.1, Des27.2, Des27.3, Des27.4, Des27.5, Des27.6, Des27.7, Des27.9, Des27.10, Des27.11, Des27.12, Des27.13. The highest activity variant is Des27.7 with kcat/KM ~11,000 M⁻¹ s⁻¹ (closest to the 10⁴ tick), followed by Des27.12 (~3,500 M⁻¹ s⁻¹) and Des27.9 (~2,000 M⁻¹ s⁻¹). Des27.2 has the lowest activity ~20 M⁻¹ s⁻¹, below the 10² dashed line.

Michaelis-Menten curve for Des27.7 with 5-nitrobenzisoxazole substrate: X-axis: [5-nitrobenziosoxazole] (mM, linear 0 to 1.0), Y-axis: V₀ (mOD min⁻¹, linear 0 to 50). Fitted kinetic parameters: K_M = 0.21 ± 0.06 mM, k_cat = 2.85 ± 1.2 s⁻¹, k_cat/K_M = 12,696 ± 1,738 M⁻¹ s⁻¹. Scatter points are experimental measurements, solid line is the fitted curve.

Active site overlay of the Des27.7 crystal structure (grey) and design model (light blue). Key active site residues labeled: F113, I136, D162 (catalytic base), V183, I216, V236. The ligand conformation matches well between crystal and model.

Sequence alignment table for Des27 variants at positions 54, 84, 86, 92, 111, 115, 136, 183, 216, 236, with columns for kcat/KM (M⁻¹ s⁻¹) and Asp162 van der Waals (vdW) interaction energy (R.e.u). Values: Des27.2: 21, -4.9; Des27.5: 54, -4.9; Des27.4:59, -4.9; Des27.6:90, -4.9; Des27:130, -5.1; Des27.10:327, -5.2; Des27.1:344, -5.2; Des27.11:718, -5.2; Des27.3:1294, -5.2; Des27.13:2014, -5.1; Des27.9:2136, -5.5; Des27.12:3837, -5.4; Des27.7:12696, -5.4. Higher activity correlates with more favorable (more negative) Asp162 vdW energy.

Simulated binding state distributions for Des27 (left) and Des27.7 (right): Des27: 63% Unbound, 37% Bound. Of bound states: 11% Reactive "In" conformation, 66% "Out" non-reactive bound, 22% Other. Des27.7: 12% Unbound, 88% Bound. Of bound states: 23% Reactive "In" conformation, 57% "Out" non-reactive bound, 19% Other. Des27.7 has significantly higher bound fraction and higher reactive conformation fraction among bound states.

Two ligand binding conformations: "In" (left, blue) is the reactive conformation where the 3-position hydrogen is oriented toward the catalytic base D162 for deprotonation; "Out" (right, orange) is the non-reactive conformation where the nitro group is oriented toward D162, precluding reaction.

Comparison of catalytic base (D162) packing between Des27 (left, green) and Des27.7 (right, blue): Des27 has L183, L136, I216 around D162; Des27.7 has V183, I136, I216. The V183 mutation in Des27.7 improves packing around D162, fixing its orientation for catalysis.
Steric clash relief: Des27 has L236 which causes steric overlap with the bound ligand; Des27.7 has V236 (smaller side chain) which eliminates this clash, allowing more favorable ligand binding.

Ligand solvation comparison: Des27 has L183, I54, F92 surrounding the ligand; Des27.7 has V183, V54, H92, creating a more favorable solvation environment for the polar nitro group of the substrate, increasing binding affinity for the reactive conformation.

Experimental and computed activation free energies (ΔG‡, kcal mol⁻¹): Des27: Experimental = 19.1 ± 0.2 kcal mol⁻¹; computed "Out" = 17.6 kcal mol⁻¹; computed "In" = 16.6 kcal mol⁻¹. Des27.7: Experimental = 16.7 ± 0.2 kcal mol⁻¹; computed "Out" = 15.9 kcal mol⁻¹; computed "In" = 13.6 kcal mol⁻¹. The experimental activation energy for Des27.7 is ~2.4 kcal mol⁻¹ lower than Des27, consistent with its ~100x higher catalytic efficiency.

Dual-axis plot: Left axis (T_m, melting temperature, °C, lower is more stable? Wait correction: x-axis left is T_m (°C, values 60, 80, 100; lower value = higher stability: 86°C for Des27.7, 82°C for F113L variant, 57-81°C for other variants). Right axis (kcat/KM, M⁻¹ s⁻¹, log scale 10³ to 10⁵). Design steps: Modular assembly, PROSS (11 muts), Active site (15 muts), Core stabilization (6 muts). Des27.7: T_m = 86°C, kcat/KM = 12,700 M⁻¹ s⁻¹. F113L variant: T_m =82°C, kcat/KM=123,000 M⁻¹ s⁻¹ (10x higher activity). Other variants: 2,900 and 11,500 M⁻¹ s⁻¹; 3 variants show ND (no detected activity). D162A catalytic dead variant has T_m >100°C (very stable, no activity).

payload.content: Reaction mechanism for base-catalyzed ring opening of 5-nitrobenzisoxazole: a base (B⁻) deprotonates the 3-position of 5-nitrobenzisoxazole, forming a negatively charged transition state with partial B-H bond formation and partial C-O bond cleavage, producing the final product 2-cyano-4-nitrophenolate and BH.

payload.content: Enzyme design workflow: Step 1: Modular assembly of protein domains. Step 2: Stability design using the PROSS TPR method (shown with a wrench icon, optimizing 12 initial positions marked with pink spheres). Step 3: Geometric matching and filtering, using a sigmoidal scoring function f = Π 1/(1 + e^((x-μ)/σ)) to filter active site geometries, resulting in purple spheres for selected positions. An inset shows the target reactive conformation of the ligand with a nearby tryptophan residue.

Step 6: Active-site optimization of amino acid side chains to improve catalysis.

payload.content: Logarithmic-scale bar chart of catalytic efficiency (kcat/KM, units: M⁻¹ s⁻¹) for Des27 and its variants. X-axis labels: Des27, Des27.1, Des27.2, Des27.3, Des27.4, Des27.5, Des27.6, Des27.7, Des27.9, Des27.10, Des27.11, Des27.12, Des27.13. The highest activity variant is Des27.7 with kcat/KM ~11,000 M⁻¹ s⁻¹ (closest to the 10⁴ tick), followed by Des27.12 (~3,500 M⁻¹ s⁻¹) and Des27.9 (~2,000 M⁻¹ s⁻¹). Des27.2 has the lowest activity ~20 M⁻¹ s⁻¹, below the 10² dashed line.

payload.content: Experimental and computed activation free energies (ΔG‡, kcal mol⁻¹): Des27: Experimental = 19.1 ± 0.2 kcal mol⁻¹; computed "Out" = 17.6 kcal mol⁻¹; computed "In" = 16.6 kcal mol⁻¹. Des27.7: Experimental = 16.7 ± 0.2 kcal mol⁻¹; computed "Out" = 15.9 kcal mol⁻¹; computed "In" = 13.6 kcal mol⁻¹. The experimental activation energy for Des27.7 is ~2.4 kcal mol⁻¹ lower than Des27, consistent with its ~100x higher catalytic efficiency.

payload.content: Dual-axis plot: Left axis (T_m, melting temperature, °C, lower is more stable? Wait correction: x-axis left is T_m (°C, values 60, 80, 100; lower value = higher stability: 86°C for Des27.7, 82°C for F113L variant, 57-81°C for other variants). Right axis (kcat/KM, M⁻¹ s⁻¹, log scale 10³ to 10⁵). Design steps: Modular assembly, PROSS (11 muts), Active site (15 muts), Core stabilization (6 muts). Des27.7: T_m = 86°C, kcat/KM = 12,700 M⁻¹ s⁻¹. F113L variant: T_m =82°C, kcat/KM=123,000 M⁻¹ s⁻¹ (10x higher activity). Other variants: 2,900 and 11,500 M⁻¹ s⁻¹; 3 variants show ND (no detected activity). D162A catalytic dead variant has T_m >100°C (very stable, no activity).

analysis_results.content: Reaction mechanism for base-catalyzed ring opening of 5-nitrobenzoisoxazole: a base (B⁻) deprotonates the 3-position of 5-nitrobenzisoxazole, forming a negatively charged transition state with partial positive charge on the abstracted hydrogen (δ⁺) and partial negative charge on the oxazole oxygen (δ⁻), followed by ring opening to produce 2-cyano-4-nitrophenolate anion and BH conjugate acid.

Enzyme design workflow, 3 initial steps: 1) Modular assembly of protein subunits to form a base scaffold; 2) Stability design using PROSS/TRP (shown with a wrench icon) to introduce stabilizing mutations (red spheres indicate residue positions selected for stabilization); 3) Geometric matching and filtering using a sigmoidal filter function f = Π 1/(1+e^((x-μ)/σ)) to identify active site configurations that match the substrate binding geometry (inset shows the substrate bound in the designed active site with yellow lines indicating geometric constraints between substrate and active site residues).

Enzyme design workflow, 3 final steps: 4) Active site and core position stabilization (purple spheres = core/active site positions, green spheres = additional stabilizing positions); 5) Experimental screening: protein expression, purification, and kinetic assay (monitoring absorbance at 380 nm as a function of substrate concentration, shown with a 96-well plate for high-throughput screening); 6) Active-site optimization: iterative mutagenesis of active site residues (stick representations of amino acids in the binding pocket) to improve catalytic activity.

Bar chart of catalytic efficiency (kcat/KM, units M⁻¹ s⁻¹, logarithmic Y-axis from 10² to 10⁴ M⁻¹ s⁻¹) for Des27 enzyme variants. The variants (X-axis) include Des27, Des27.1, Des27.2, Des27.3, Des27.4, Des27.5, Des27.6, Des27.7, Des27.9, Des27.10, Des27.11, Des27.12, Des27.13. The highest efficiency is for Des27.7 at ~11,000 M⁻¹ s⁻¹ (measured by visual estimation, ±10% uncertainty), followed by Des27.12 (~3,500 M⁻¹ s⁻¹), Des27.13 (~2,000 M⁻¹ s⁻¹), Des27.9 (~1,800 M⁻¹ s⁻¹), Des27.3 (~1,200 M⁻¹ s⁻¹), Des27.1 (~300 M⁻¹ s⁻¹), Des27 (~100 M⁻¹ s⁻¹), and the lowest is Des27.2 at ~20 M⁻¹ s⁻¹.

Michaelis-Menten kinetic curve for Des27.7 with 5-nitrobenzisoxazole as substrate. X-axis = [5-nitrobenzisoxazole] (mM, 0 to 1.0), Y-axis = initial velocity V₀ (mOD min⁻¹, 0 to 50). Black dots are measured data points, black line is the fitted Michaelis-Menten curve. Annotated kinetic parameters: K_M = 0.21 ± 0.06 mM, k_cat = 2.85 ± 1.2 s⁻¹, k_cat/K_M = 12,696 ± 1,738 M⁻¹ s⁻¹.

Crystal structure (xtal, white sticks) vs computational model (light blue sticks) of the Des27.7 active site, with bound substrate. Labeled active site residues: F113, I136, D162, V183, I216, V236. The substrate is positioned in the binding pocket with close contact to the catalytic base D162, and the model shows strong agreement with the experimental crystal structure for all residue positions and substrate binding orientation.

Table of residue identities at positions 54, 84, 86, 92, 111, 115, 136, 183, 216, 236 for all Des27 variants, alongside measured kcat/KM (M⁻¹ s⁻¹) and calculated Asp162 van der Waals (vdW) energy (R.e.u, Rosetta energy units). Values range from the lowest performing Des27.2 (kcat/KM=21 M⁻¹ s⁻¹, vdW=-4.9 R.e.u) to the highest Des27.7 (kcat/KM=12,696 M⁻¹ s⁻¹, vdW=-5.4 R.e.u).

Pie charts of conformational state distributions for Des27 and Des27.7, plus stacked bar charts of reactive state subpopulations. For Des27: 63% of states are unbound, 37% are bound; of the bound reactive states, 11% are "In" (productive binding orientation), 66% are "Out" (non-productive), 22% are other. For Des27.7: 12% of states are unbound, 88% are bound; of the bound reactive states, 23% are "In", 57% are "Out", 19% are other. Des27.7 has a ~2x higher fraction of productive In binding states relative to Des27, and a much higher bound population overall.

Structural representation of the two substrate binding orientations: "In" (blue substrate, productive orientation with the reactive C-H bond positioned towards the catalytic base D162) and "Out" (orange substrate, non-productive orientation pointing away from the base), shown in the active site with grey protein backbone.

Comparison of catalytic base (D162) packing in Des27 (green) vs Des27.7 (blue) models. Des27 has hydrophobic residues L183, L136, I216 surrounding D162, while Des27.7 has smaller residues V183, I136, I216 leading to tighter packing of the catalytic base, reducing conformational flexibility and improving positioning for proton abstraction.

Comparison of residue 236 in Des27 (L236, green) vs Des27.7 (V236, blue). The bulkier leucine in Des27 creates steric overlap with the substrate (red/purple overlap spheres), while the smaller valine in Des27.7 relieves this clash, allowing tighter substrate binding and higher activity.

Comparison of substrate binding pocket solvation in Des27 (green) vs Des27.7 (blue). Des27 has hydrophobic residues L183, I54, F92 surrounding the substrate, while Des27.7 has smaller residues V183, V54, H92, which reduces the desolvation penalty for substrate binding and improves the hydrophobic match to the substrate, increasing binding affinity.

Bar chart of activation free energy (ΔG‡, kcal mol⁻¹) for Des27 and Des27.7, comparing experimental values (white bars) with computed values for Out (orange) and In (blue) binding orientations. For Des27: experimental ΔG‡ = 19.2 ± 0.3 kcal mol⁻¹, calculated Out ΔG‡ = 17.7 ± 2.2 kcal mol⁻¹, calculated In ΔG‡ = 16.7 ± 2.1 kcal mol⁻¹. For Des27.7: experimental ΔG‡ = 16.7 ± 0.2 kcal mol⁻¹, calculated Out ΔG‡ = 15.9 ± 1.6 kcal mol⁻¹, calculated In ΔG‡ = 13.6 ± 0.5 kcal mol⁻¹. The measured activation energy for Des27.7 is ~2.5 kcal mol⁻¹ lower than Des27, matching the ~100x higher kcat/KM for Des27.7 relative to the parent Des27.

Plot of melting temperature (Tm, °C, left X-axis, reverse scale 100 to 50) and kcat/KM (M⁻¹ s⁻¹, right X-axis, log scale 10² to 10⁵) for design variants with different combinations of design steps. Beige bars = Tm, light blue bars = kcat/KM, grey dots = individual replicate measurements, ND = no detectable activity. The full design (all 4 steps: modular assembly, PROSS (11 mutations), active site (15 mutations), core stabilization (6 mutations)) gives Des27.7: Tm = 86 °C, kcat/KM = 12,700 M⁻¹ s⁻¹. Removing core stabilization: Tm = 81 °C, kcat/KM = 11,500 M⁻¹ s⁻¹. Removing active site design: Tm = 60 °C, kcat/KM = 2,900 M⁻¹ s⁻¹. Removing PROSS: no detectable activity, Tm ~57-69 °C. A single F113L mutation added to Des27.7 gives a further improved variant: Tm = 82 °C, kcat/KM = 123,000 M⁻¹ s⁻¹ (the highest activity measured). The catalytic dead D162A mutant has Tm >100 °C with no detectable activity.

payload.content: Crystal structure (xtal, white sticks) vs computational model (light blue sticks) of the Des27.7 active site, with bound substrate. Labeled active site residues: F113, I136, D162, V183, I216, V236. The substrate is positioned in the binding pocket with close contact to the catalytic base D162, and the model shows strong agreement with the experimental crystal structure for all residue positions and substrate binding orientation.

payload.content: Pie charts of conformational state distributions for Des27 and Des27.7, plus stacked bar charts of reactive state subpopulations. For Des27: 63% of states are unbound, 37% are bound; of the bound reactive states, 11% are "In" (productive binding orientation), 66% are "Out" (non-productive), 22% are other. For Des27.7: 12% of states are unbound, 88% are bound; of the bound reactive states, 23% are "In", 57% are "Out", 19% are other. Des27.7 has a ~2x higher fraction of productive In binding states relative to Des27, and a much higher bound population overall.

payload.content: Comparison of catalytic base (D162) packing in Des27 (green) vs Des27.7 (blue) models. Des27 has hydrophobic residues L183, L136, I216 surrounding D162, while Des27.7 has smaller residues V183, I136, I216 leading to tighter packing of the catalytic base, reducing conformational flexibility and improving positioning for proton abstraction.

payload.content: Comparison of residue 236 in Des27 (L236, green) vs Des27.7 (V236, blue). The bulkier leucine in Des27 creates steric overlap with the substrate (red/purple overlap spheres), while the smaller valine in Des27.7 relieves this clash, allowing tighter substrate binding and higher activity.

payload.content: Comparison of substrate binding pocket solvation in Des27 (green) vs Des27.7 (blue). Des27 has hydrophobic residues L183, I54, F92 surrounding the substrate, while Des27.7 has smaller residues V183, V54, H92, which reduces the desolvation penalty for substrate binding and improves the hydrophobic match to the substrate, increasing binding affinity.

payload.content: Bar chart of activation free energy (ΔG‡, kcal mol⁻¹) for Des27 and Des27.7, comparing experimental values (white bars) with computed values for Out (orange) and In (blue) binding orientations. For Des27: experimental ΔG‡ = 19.2 ± 0.3 kcal mol⁻¹, calculated Out ΔG‡ = 17.7 ± 2.2 kcal mol⁻¹, calculated In ΔG‡ = 16.7 ± 2.1 kcal mol⁻¹. For Des27.7: experimental ΔG‡ = 16.7 ± 0.2 kcal mol⁻¹, calculated Out ΔG‡ = 15.9 ± 1.6 kcal mol⁻¹, calculated In ΔG‡ = 13.6 ± 0.5 kcal mol⁻¹. The measured activation energy for Des27.7 is ~2.5 kcal mol⁻¹ lower than Des27, matching the ~100x higher kcat/KM for Des27.7 relative to the parent Des27.

payload.content: Plot of melting temperature (Tm, °C, left X-axis, reverse scale 100 to 50) and kcat/KM (M⁻¹ s⁻¹, right X-axis, log scale 10² to 10⁵) for design variants with different combinations of design steps. Beige bars = Tm, light blue bars = kcat/KM, grey dots = individual replicate measurements, ND = no detectable activity. The full design (all 4 steps: modular assembly, PROSS (11 mutations), active site (15 mutations), core stabilization (6 mutations)) gives Des27.7: Tm = 86 °C, kcat/KM = 12,700 M⁻¹ s⁻¹. Removing core stabilization: Tm = 81 °C, kcat/KM = 11,500 M⁻¹ s⁻¹. Removing active site design: Tm = 60 °C, kcat/KM = 2,900 M⁻¹ s⁻¹. Removing PROSS: no detectable activity, Tm ~57-69 °C. A single F113L mutation added to Des27.7 gives a further improved variant: Tm = 82 °C, kcat/KM = 123,000 M⁻¹ s⁻¹ (the highest activity measured). The catalytic dead D162A mutant has Tm >100 °C with no detectable activity.

analysis_results.content: Reaction mechanism for base-catalyzed ring opening of 5-nitrobenzisoxazole: A base (B⁻) deprotonates the 3-position of 5-nitrobenzisoxazole, forming a negatively charged transition state with partial charges (δ⁺ on B, δ⁻ on O), followed by ring opening to produce 2-cyano-4-nitrophenolate and BH.

Three-step protein design workflow: 1) Modular assembly of protein domains, 2) Stability design using the PROSS tool (wrench icon) to identify mutation positions (pink spheres), 3) Geometric matching and filtering using a sigmoidal scoring function (f = Π 1/(1 + e^((x-μ)/σ))) to optimize active site geometry, with a bound ligand shown in the inset.

Final three design workflow steps: 4) Active site and core position stabilization (green spheres: core positions, light purple spheres: active site positions), 5) Experimental screening including protein expression, purification, and kinetic assay measuring absorbance at 380 nm as a function of substrate concentration, 6) Active-site optimization with modeled amino acid sidechains in the binding pocket.

analysis_results.figure_id: Figure 2a (kcat/KM bar chart for Des27 variants)
analysis_results.content: Bar chart showing kcat/KM (log₁₀ scale, units: M⁻¹ s⁻¹) for 12 Des27 enzyme variants. Highest activity is for Des27.7 (12,696 M⁻¹ s⁻¹, ±~5% error). Other variants: Des27: 110, Des27.1: 300, Des27.2: 20, Des27.3: 1200, Des27.4: 50, Des27.5: 45, Des27.6: 80, Des27.9: 2000, Des27.10: 280, Des27.11: 600, Des27.12: 3800, Des27.13: 1800. The y-axis ranges from 10¹ to 10⁴ M⁻¹ s⁻¹.

Michaelis-Menten curve for Des27.7 with 5-nitrobenziisoxazole as substrate. Fitted kinetic parameters: K_M = 0.21 ± 0.06 mM, k_cat = 2.85 ± 1.2 s⁻¹, k_cat/K_M = 12,696 ± 1,738 M⁻¹ s⁻¹. X-axis: [5-nitrobenzisoxazole] (mM, 0 to 1.0), Y-axis: initial velocity V₀ (mOD min⁻¹, 0 to 50). Scatter points are experimental measurements, black line is the fitted curve.

Active site alignment of Des27.7 crystal structure (gray, xtal) and computational model (light blue). Key active site residues labeled: F113, I136, D162 (catalytic base), V183, I216, V236. The bound substrate analog is shown in the center with overlapping poses from crystal and model.

analysis_results.figure_id: Figure 3a (sequence and activity table for Des27 variants)
analysis_results.content: Sequence alignment table of Des27 variants at positions 54, 84, 86, 92, 111, 115, 136, 183, 216, 236, with corresponding k_cat/K_M (M⁻¹ s⁻¹) and Asp162 van der Waals (vdW) score (R.e.u). Activities range from 21 M⁻¹ s⁻¹ (Des27.2) to 12,696 M⁻¹ s⁻¹ (Des27.7). Asp162 vdW scores range from -4.9 to -5.5 R.e.u, with more negative (favorable) values correlating with higher activity for top variants.

analysis_results.figure_id: Figure 3c (bound state composition pie charts for Des27 and Des27.7)
analysis_results.content: Pie charts of simulated substrate binding states for Des27 and Des27.7: Des27 has 63% Unbound, 37% Bound; of the bound state, 11% are In (reactive orientation, nitro group inside the pocket), 66% Out (nitro group outside), 22% Other. Des27.7 has 12% Unbound, 88% Bound; of the bound state, 23% In, 57% Out, 19% Other. The fraction of reactive In conformations is 2x higher in Des27.7.

analysis_results.figure_id: Figure 3d (In vs Out substrate binding poses)
analysis_results.content: Two substrate binding poses: In pose (light blue, nitro group pointing inward toward catalytic base D162, reactive orientation) and Out pose (orange, nitro group pointing outward, non-reactive orientation), shown within the enzyme active site.

Comparison of catalytic base (D162, red) packing in Des27 (green) and Des27.7 (blue) models. Des27 has L136, L183, I216 surrounding D162; Des27.7 has I136, V183, I216, improving packing around D162 to fix its orientation for catalysis.

Steric overlap relief at position 236: Des27 has L236 (green) which clashes with the bound substrate; Des27.7 has V236 (blue, smaller sidechain) which removes the steric clash, allowing tighter substrate binding.

Ligand solvation shell comparison: Des27 (green) has I54, L183, F92 surrounding the substrate; Des27.7 (blue) has V54, V183, H92, optimizing hydrophobic and polar interactions to stabilize the bound substrate in the reactive In orientation.

Activation free energy (ΔG‡, kcal mol⁻¹) for Des27 and Des27.7, comparing experimental values and simulated values for In and Out poses: Des27: Experimental = 19.2 ± 0.2, Out = 17.7 ± 2.1, In = 16.7 ± 2.0. Des27.7: Experimental = 16.7 ± 0.2, Out = 15.9 ± 1.4, In = 13.6 ± 0.5. The In pose activation energy matches the experimental trend of lower ΔG‡ for the improved variant Des27.7.

Tradeoff between melting temperature (Tₘ, °C, left axis, decreasing rightward) and kcat/KM (M⁻¹ s⁻¹, right axis, log scale increasing rightward) for designed variants. The top variant Des27.7 combines 11 PROSS mutations, 15 active site mutations, 6 core stabilization mutations: Tₘ = 86 °C, k_cat/K_M = 12,700 M⁻¹ s⁻¹. The F113L mutant of Des27.7 further improves activity to 123,000 M⁻¹ s⁻¹ with Tₘ = 82 °C. The D162A (catalytic base knockout) variant has Tₘ >100 °C but no detectable activity (ND). Variants missing design steps have lower stability (Tₘ 57–81 °C) and/or no detectable activity.

payload.figure_id: Figure 3a (sequence and activity table for Des27 variants)
payload.content: Sequence alignment table of Des27 variants at positions 54, 84, 86, 92, 111, 115, 136, 183, 216, 236, with corresponding k_cat/K_M (M⁻¹ s⁻¹) and Asp162 van der Waals (vdW) score (R.e.u). Activities range from 21 M⁻¹ s⁻¹ (Des27.2) to 12,696 M⁻¹ s⁻¹ (Des27.7). Asp162 vdW scores range from -4.9 to -5.5 R.e.u, with more negative (favorable) values correlating with higher activity for top variants.

anchors.snippet: Fig. 3 | Structural, energetic and dynamic contributions to the improved catalytic rate of Des27.7. a, Mutations (grey background) at positions 136, 216 and 236 trend with increasing catalytic efficiency, which also trends with Rosetta-computed vdW energy of the catalytic Asp162. payload.content: Tradeoff between melting temperature (Tₘ, °C, left axis, decreasing rightward) and kcat/KM (M⁻¹ s⁻¹, right axis, log scale increasing rightward) for designed variants. The top variant Des27.7 combines 11 PROSS mutations, 15 active site mutations, 6 core stabilization mutations: Tₘ = 86 °C, k_cat/K_M = 12,700 M⁻¹ s⁻¹. The F113L mutant of Des27.7 further improves activity to 123,000 M⁻¹ s⁻¹ with Tₘ = 82 °C. The D162A (catalytic base knockout) variant has Tₘ >100 °C but no detectable activity (ND). Variants missing design steps have lower stability (Tₘ 57–81 °C) and/or no detectable activity.

anchors.snippet: Fig. 4 | Deconvoluting the contributions of the mutations in Des27.7 reveals necessary and sufficient components for effective de novo enzyme design. anchors.snippet: To verify the molecular accuracy of the design process, we determined the structure of Des27.7 in the unbound form by crystallographic analysis (Extended Data Table 2; PDB 9HVB). All active-site positions aligned well with the design conception (less than $0 . 7 \mathring \mathrm { A }$ all-atom root mean squared deviation (r.m.s.d.) across 20 residues), including the catalytic Asp162, although a slight shift (r.m.s.d. 0.78 Å) was observed in the orientation of Phe113. Outside the active-site pocket, 180 of 257 positions aligned with backbone r.m.s.d. $< 0 . 6 \mathring \mathbf { A }$ , but 65 amino acids either deviated or did not exhibit significant electron density, probably due to backbone flexibility in this region (Extended Data Fig. 6a,b). This fragment is known to be dynamic in the IGPS protein family52, but it lies outside the active-site pocket and probably does not contribute directly to reactivity and substrate recognition. Taken together, our results verify a fully computational pipeline that designs an accurate de novo active site and generates a stable and high-efficiency new-to-nature enzyme.

102: To verify the molecular accuracy of the design process, we determined the structure of Des27.7 in the unbound form by crystallographic analysis (Extended Data Table 2; PDB 9HVB). All active-site positions aligned well with the design conception (less than $0 . 7 \mathring \mathrm { A }$ all-atom root mean squared deviation (r.m.s.d.) across 20 residues), including the catalytic Asp162, although a slight shift (r.m.s.d. 0.78 Å) was observed in the orientation of Phe113. Outside the active-site pocket, 180 of 257 positions aligned with backbone r.m.s.d. $< 0 . 6 \mathring \mathbf { A }$ , but 65 amino acids either deviated or did not exhibit significant electron density, probably due to backbone flexibility in this region (Extended Data Fig. 6a,b). This fragment is known to be dynamic in the IGPS protein family52, but it lies outside the active-site pocket and probably does not contribute directly to reactivity and substrate recognition. Taken together, our results verify a fully computational pipeline that designs an accurate de novo active site and generates a stable and high-efficiency new-to-nature enzyme.

anchors.snippet: Crystals were grown at $1 9 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ using the sitting-drop vapour diffusion method. Diffraction data were collected from a single crystal flash-cooled to 100 K, using a wavelength of 1.34 Å. Data were collected using an in-house Rigaku liquid-metal-jet X-ray Synergy System with a HyPix Arc $1 5 0 ^ { \circ }$ detector. AlphaFold2 (ref. 57) was used to generate all three models for molecular replacement (Extended Data Table 2). Initial models were iteratively rebuilt and refined using COOT63 and PHENIX64. Model geometry was evaluated using MOLPROBITY65. Atomic coordinates and structure factors for Des27.7, R2.Des39 and R2.Des49 are deposited in the PDB database under accession numbers 9HVB, 9HVH and 9HVG, respectively.

anchors.snippet: In total, 66 designs were solubly expressed and 14 showed cooperative thermal denaturation (Extended Data Fig. 1). Three designs showed measurable KE activity in an initial screen, with the top two designs, Des27 and Des61, exhibiting ${ k _ { \mathrm { { c a t } } } } / { K _ { \mathrm { { M } } } }$ values of 130 and 210 $\mathsf { M } ^ { - 1 } \mathsf { S } ^ { - 1 }$ , respectively, and $k _ { \mathrm { c a t } } < 1 \mathsf { s } ^ { - 1 }$ (Extended Data Fig. 2, Extended Data Table 1 and Supplementary Table 1).

anchors.snippet: The designed genes were ordered from Twist Bioscience, cloned into pET28 plasmid with an N-terminal His-tag, followed by a bdSUMO tag. Plasmids were transformed into Escherichia coli BL21 (DE3) cells. For expression, $5 0 \mathrm { m l }$ of 2YT medium supplemented with $5 0 \mu \mathrm { g } \mathsf { m } ^ { - 1 }$ kanamycin was inoculated with $5 0 0 \mu \mathrm { l }$ of overnight culture produced from a single colony and grown at $3 7 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ until optical density $\left( \mathrm { O D } \right) _ { 6 0 0 }$ 0.6–0.8. Overexpression was induced by adding 1 mM IPTG and the cultures were grown for 20 h at $1 6 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ and collected, and the pellet was frozen at $- 2 0 { } ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ . The cells were resuspended in basic buffer $( 5 0 \mathsf { m } \mathsf { M }$ Tris-Cl pH 7.25, 200 mM NaCl) supplemented with $1 0 \mu \mathrm { g } \mathsf { m } ^ { - 1 }$ lysozyme, protease-inhibitor cocktail (Sigma) and benzonase, lysed by sonication and centrifuged at $2 0 { , } 0 0 0 g$ for 30 min a $4 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C } .$ . The soluble fraction was loaded onto an Ni-NTA (nitrilotriacetic acid) column and washed twice with basic buffer and $2 0 \mathrm { m M }$ imidazole. The protein was subjected to overnight on-column Sumo protease cleavage at $4 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ $S \mu \mathrm { g } \mathsf { m } \mathsf { l } ^ { - 1 }$ in basic buffer). Protein purity was assessed by SDS–PAGE. Protein concentration was determined using Pierce BCA protein assay kit. For crystallography, large-scale expression was performed in 1,500 ml of culture. After Ni-NTA purification and bdSUMO cleavage, the protein was purified by gel filtration (HiLoad 26/600 Superdex75 preparative grade column, GE).

anchors.snippet: (20 designs) showed measurable KE activity with ${ k _ { \mathrm { { c a t } } } } / { K _ { \mathrm { { M } } } }$ in the range of $0 . 5 { - } 1 5 5 \mathbf { M } ^ { - 1 } \mathbf { s } ^ { - 1 }$ , demonstrating that the workflow can design stable and functional Kemp eliminases in a wide range of different starting points. As expected, designs that did not show cooperative unfolding lacked KE activity. We applied FuncLib to the active sites of six designs and tested 9–14 variants for each starting point. In five cases, catalytic efficiencies improved by 3–10-fold (Supplementary Table 1), with the highest catalytic efficiency reaching $3 0 0 \mathsf { M } ^ { - 1 } \mathsf { s } ^ { - 1 }$ (R2.Des39.2). We determined the crystallographic structure of two designs, R2.Des39 $( k _ { \mathrm { c a t } } / K _ { \mathrm { M } }$ $1 0 0 \mathsf { M } ^ { - 1 } \mathsf { s } ^ { - 1 } )$ and Des49 $( k _ { \mathrm { c a t } } / K _ { \mathrm { M } } 1 5 0 \mathsf { M } ^ { - 1 } \mathsf { s } ^ { - 1 } )$ (Extended Data Fig. 6c–i, PDB IDs 9HVH and 9HVG and Extended Data Table 2). The active sites were close to their design conceptions (r.m.s.d. $< 0 . 6$ Å and r.m.s.d. $< 0 . 8 2 \mathrm { \AA }$ , respectively), but, in both cases, several loops either lacked electron density or exhibited significant conformational changes compared with the designs, which could impede substrate entry to the active site53. To explore whether the foldability of these loops could be improved, we applied FuncLib to stabilize these regions according to the design models. Three of 16 FuncLib variants of Des39.2 showed a significant increase in catalytic efficiency, with improvements up to 20-fold compared with the original design, reaching $k _ { \mathrm { c a t } } / K _ { \mathrm { M } } 2 , 0 0 0 \mathsf { M } ^ { - 1 } \mathsf { s } ^ { - 1 }$ (Extended Data Table 1 and Supplementary Table 1), but none surpassed the performance of Des27.7. These results demonstrate that large-scale artificial intelligence-based structure prediction of natural enzymes provides a valuable resource for de novo enzyme design, and that the computational workflow reproducibly generates efficient enzymes. In this case, however, optimization with FuncLib reached superior catalytic parameters in the designs derived from modular assembly, which may reflect the greater structural diversity in these designs.

anchors.snippet: Crystals were grown at $1 9 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ using the sitting-drop vapour diffusion method. Diffraction data were collected from a single crystal flash-cooled to 100 K, using a wavelength of 1.34 Å. Data were collected using an in-house Rigaku liquid-metal-jet X-ray Synergy System with a HyPix Arc $1 5 0 ^ { \circ }$ detector. AlphaFold2 (ref. 57) was used to generate all three models for molecular replacement (Extended Data Table 2). Initial models were iteratively rebuilt and refined using COOT63 and PHENIX64. Model geometry was evaluated using MOLPROBITY65. Atomic coordinates and structure factors for Des27.7, R2.Des39 and R2.Des49 are deposited in the PDB database under accession numbers 9HVB, 9HVH and 9HVG, respectively.

anchors.snippet: In total, 66 designs were solubly expressed and 14 showed cooperative thermal denaturation (Extended Data Fig. 1). Three designs showed measurable KE activity in an initial screen, with the top two designs, Des27 and Des61, exhibiting ${ k _ { \mathrm { { c a t } } } } / { K _ { \mathrm { { M } } } }$ values of 130 and 210 $\mathsf { M } ^ { - 1 } \mathsf { S } ^ { - 1 }$ , respectively, and $k _ { \mathrm { c a t } } < 1 \mathsf { s } ^ { - 1 }$ (Extended Data Fig. 2, Extended Data Table 1 and Supplementary Table 1).

anchors.snippet: The designed genes were ordered from Twist Bioscience, cloned into pET28 plasmid with an N-terminal His-tag, followed by a bdSUMO tag. Plasmids were transformed into Escherichia coli BL21 (DE3) cells. For expression, $5 0 \mathrm { m l }$ of 2YT medium supplemented with $5 0 \mu \mathrm { g } \mathsf { m } ^ { - 1 }$ kanamycin was inoculated with $5 0 0 \mu \mathrm { l }$ of overnight culture produced from a single colony and grown at $3 7 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ until optical density $\left( \mathrm { O D } \right) _ { 6 0 0 }$ 0.6–0.8. Overexpression was induced by adding 1 mM IPTG and the cultures were grown for 20 h at $1 6 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ and collected, and the pellet was frozen at $- 2 0 { } ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ . The cells were resuspended in basic buffer $( 5 0 \mathsf { m } \mathsf { M }$ Tris-Cl pH 7.25, 200 mM NaCl) supplemented with $1 0 \mu \mathrm { g } \mathsf { m } ^ { - 1 }$ lysozyme, protease-inhibitor cocktail (Sigma) and benzonase, lysed by sonication and centrifuged at $2 0 { , } 0 0 0 g$ for 30 min a $4 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C } .$ . The soluble fraction was loaded onto an Ni-NTA (nitrilotriacetic acid) column and washed twice with basic buffer and $2 0 \mathrm { m M }$ imidazole. The protein was subjected to overnight on-column Sumo protease cleavage at $4 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ $S \mu \mathrm { g } \mathsf { m } \mathsf { l } ^ { - 1 }$ in basic buffer). Protein purity was assessed by SDS–PAGE. Protein concentration was determined using Pierce BCA protein assay kit. For crystallography, large-scale expression was performed in 1,500 ml of culture. After Ni-NTA purification and bdSUMO cleavage, the protein was purified by gel filtration (HiLoad 26/600 Superdex75 preparative grade column, GE).

anchors.snippet: (20 designs) showed measurable KE activity with ${ k _ { \mathrm { { c a t } } } } / { K _ { \mathrm { { M } } } }$ in the range of $0 . 5 { - } 1 5 5 \mathbf { M } ^ { - 1 } \mathbf { s } ^ { - 1 }$ , demonstrating that the workflow can design stable and functional Kemp eliminases in a wide range of different starting points. As expected, designs that did not show cooperative unfolding lacked KE activity. We applied FuncLib to the active sites of six designs and tested 9–14 variants for each starting point. In five cases, catalytic efficiencies improved by 3–10-fold (Supplementary Table 1), with the highest catalytic efficiency reaching $3 0 0 \mathsf { M } ^ { - 1 } \mathsf { s } ^ { - 1 }$ (R2.Des39.2). We determined the crystallographic structure of two designs, R2.Des39 $( k _ { \mathrm { c a t } } / K _ { \mathrm { M } }$ $1 0 0 \mathsf { M } ^ { - 1 } \mathsf { s } ^ { - 1 } )$ and Des49 $( k _ { \mathrm { c a t } } / K _ { \mathrm { M } } 1 5 0 \mathsf { M } ^ { - 1 } \mathsf { s } ^ { - 1 } )$ (Extended Data Fig. 6c–i, PDB IDs 9HVH and 9HVG and Extended Data Table 2). The active sites were close to their design conceptions (r.m.s.d. $< 0 . 6$ Å and r.m.s.d. $< 0 . 8 2 \mathrm { \AA }$ , respectively), but, in both cases, several loops either lacked electron density or exhibited significant conformational changes compared with the designs, which could impede substrate entry to the active site53. To explore whether the foldability of these loops could be improved, we applied FuncLib to stabilize these regions according to the design models. Three of 16 FuncLib variants of Des39.2 showed a significant increase in catalytic efficiency, with improvements up to 20-fold compared with the original design, reaching $k _ { \mathrm { c a t } } / K _ { \mathrm { M } } 2 , 0 0 0 \mathsf { M } ^ { - 1 } \mathsf { s } ^ { - 1 }$ (Extended Data Table 1 and Supplementary Table 1), but none surpassed the performance of Des27.7. These results demonstrate that large-scale artificial intelligence-based structure prediction of natural enzymes provides a valuable resource for de novo enzyme design, and that the computational workflow reproducibly generates efficient enzymes. In this case, however, optimization with FuncLib reached superior catalytic parameters in the designs derived from modular assembly, which may reflect the greater structural diversity in these designs.

anchors.snippet: The underlying reasons for the low efficiencies of de novo designed enzymes have been intensely studied10,17–19. These analyses revealed that the designed active sites exhibited significant structural distortions relative to the design conception17,19. Notably, catalysis is extremely sensitive to molecular details, and shifts of the catalytic constellation by a few degrees or tenths of an Ångstrom from optimality may translate into orders of magnitude decreases in efficiency20. Furthermore, designs often exhibited low stability and expressibility7 , limiting their ability to accommodate activity-enhancing mutations7,21. Further concerns were that fixed-backbone design methods fail to precisely position non-native catalytic groups1 ; the molecular details of the designed transition state (theozyme) were uncertain6,22; and that protein dynamics23 and long-range electrostatic interactions may be necessary to achieve high catalytic efficiency but are unaccounted for in the design process6,24,25.
anchors.excerpt:

Furthermore, designs often exhibited low stability and expressibility7 , limiting their ability to accommodate activity-enhancing mutations7,21. Further concerns were that fixed-backbone design methods fail to precisely position non-native catalytic groups1 ; the molecular details of the designed transition state (theozyme) were uncertain6,22; and that protein dynamics23 and long-range electrostatic interactions may be necessary to achieve high catalytic efficiency but are unaccounted for in the design process6,24,25.

248: Crystals were grown at $1 9 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ using the sitting-drop vapour diffusion method. Diffraction data were collected from a single crystal flash-cooled to 100 K, using a wavelength of 1.34 Å. Data were collected using an in-house Rigaku liquid-metal-jet X-ray Synergy System with a HyPix Arc $1 5 0 ^ { \circ }$ detector. AlphaFold2 (ref. 57) was used to generate all three models for molecular replacement (Extended Data Table 2). Initial models were iteratively rebuilt and refined using COOT63 and PHENIX64. Model geometry was evaluated using MOLPROBITY65. Atomic coordinates and structure factors for Des27.7, R2.Des39 and R2.Des49 are deposited in the PDB database under accession numbers 9HVB, 9HVH and 9HVG, respectively.

anchors.snippet: In total, 66 designs were solubly expressed and 14 showed cooperative thermal denaturation (Extended Data Fig. 1). Three designs showed measurable KE activity in an initial screen, with the top two designs, Des27 and Des61, exhibiting ${ k _ { \mathrm { { c a t } } } } / { K _ { \mathrm { { M } } } }$ values of 130 and 210 $\mathsf { M } ^ { - 1 } \mathsf { S } ^ { - 1 }$ , respectively, and $k _ { \mathrm { c a t } } < 1 \mathsf { s } ^ { - 1 }$ (Extended Data Fig. 2, Extended Data Table 1 and Supplementary Table 1).

Three designs showed measurable KE activity in an initial screen, with the top two designs, Des27 and Des61, exhibiting ${ k _ { \mathrm { { c a t } } } } / { K _ { \mathrm { { M } } } }$ values of 130 and 210 $\mathsf { M } ^ { - 1 } \mathsf { S } ^ { - 1 }$ , respectively, and $k _ { \mathrm { c a t } } < 1 \mathsf { s } ^ { - 1 }$ (Extended Data Fig. 2, Extended Data Table 1 and Supplementary Table 1).

anchors.snippet: ![](images/87ba402fa401b77021501bdaaa25aa50c1e19d7d4c2e6876106b43807cbfd2cc.jpg)
![](images/c050cf19414f34f5ebd2b5421852409f4498aa5da50dfc80c3bdf8c7a91985b7.jpg)
![](images/afa4269e903436cf6900e465de592ec293def3e51c2d6dffcd3079e75b520a62.jpg)

(20 designs) showed measurable KE activity with ${ k _ { \mathrm { { c a t } } } } / { K _ { \mathrm { { M } } } }$ in the range of $0 . 5 { - } 1 5 5 \mathbf { M } ^ { - 1 } \mathbf { s } ^ { - 1 }$ , demonstrating that the workflow can design stable and functional Kemp eliminases in a wide range of different starting points. As expected, designs that did not show cooperative unfolding lacked KE activity. We applied FuncLib to the active sites of six designs and tested 9–14 variants for each starting point. In five cases, catalytic efficiencies improved by 3–10-fold (Supplementary Table 1), with the highest catalytic efficiency reaching $3 0 0 \mathsf { M } ^ { - 1 } \mathsf { s } ^ { - 1 }$ (R2.Des39.2). We determined the crystallographic structure of two designs, R2.Des39 $( k _ { \mathrm { c a t } } / K _ { \mathrm { M } }$ $1 0 0 \mathsf { M } ^ { - 1 } \mathsf { s } ^ { - 1 } )$ and Des49 $( k _ { \mathrm { c a t } } / K _ { \mathrm { M } } 1 5 0 \mathsf { M } ^ { - 1 } \mathsf { s } ^ { - 1 } )$ (Extended Data Fig. 6c–i, PDB IDs 9HVH and 9HVG and Extended Data Table 2). The active sites were close to their design conceptions (r.m.s.d. $< 0 . 6$ Å and r.m.s.d. $< 0 . 8 2 \mathrm { \AA }$ , respectively), but, in both cases, several loops either lacked electron density or exhibited significant conformational changes compared with the designs, which could impede substrate entry to the active site53. To explore whether the foldability of these loops could be improved, we applied FuncLib to stabilize these regions according to the design models. Three of 16 FuncLib variants of Des39.2 showed a significant increase in catalytic efficiency, with improvements up to 20-fold compared with the original design, reaching $k _ { \mathrm { c a t } } / K _ { \mathrm { M } } 2 , 0 0 0 \mathsf { M } ^ { - 1 } \mathsf { s } ^ { - 1 }$ (Extended Data Table 1 and Supplementary Table 1), but none surpassed the performance of Des27.7. These results demonstrate that large-scale artificial intelligence-based structure prediction of natural enzymes provides a valuable resource for de novo enzyme design, and that the computational workflow reproducibly generates efficient enzymes. In this case, however, optimization with FuncLib reached superior catalytic parameters in the designs derived from modular assembly, which may reflect the greater structural diversity in these designs.

anchors.snippet: The designed genes were ordered from Twist Bioscience, cloned into pET28 plasmid with an N-terminal His-tag, followed by a bdSUMO tag. Plasmids were transformed into Escherichia coli BL21 (DE3) cells. For expression, $5 0 \mathrm { m l }$ of 2YT medium supplemented with $5 0 \mu \mathrm { g } \mathsf { m } ^ { - 1 }$ kanamycin was inoculated with $5 0 0 \mu \mathrm { l }$ of overnight culture produced from a single colony and grown at $3 7 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ until optical density $\left( \mathrm { O D } \right) _ { 6 0 0 }$ 0.6–0.8. Overexpression was induced by adding 1 mM IPTG and the cultures were grown for 20 h at $1 6 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ and collected, and the pellet was frozen at $- 2 0 { } ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ . The cells were resuspended in basic buffer $( 5 0 \mathsf { m } \mathsf { M }$ Tris-Cl pH 7.25, 200 mM NaCl) supplemented with $1 0 \mu \mathrm { g } \mathsf { m } ^ { - 1 }$ lysozyme, protease-inhibitor cocktail (Sigma) and benzonase, lysed by sonication and centrifuged at $2 0 { , } 0 0 0 g$ for 30 min a $4 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C } .$ . The soluble fraction was loaded onto an Ni-NTA (nitrilotriacetic acid) column and washed twice with basic buffer and $2 0 \mathrm { m M }$ imidazole. The protein was subjected to overnight on-column Sumo protease cleavage at $4 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ $S \mu \mathrm { g } \mathsf { m } \mathsf { l } ^ { - 1 }$ in basic buffer). Protein purity was assessed by SDS–PAGE. Protein concentration was determined using Pierce BCA protein assay kit. For crystallography, large-scale expression was performed in 1,500 ml of culture. After Ni-NTA purification and bdSUMO cleavage, the protein was purified by gel filtration (HiLoad 26/600 Superdex75 preparative grade column, GE).

analysis_results.content: Deprotonation reaction mechanism of 5-nitrobenzisoxazole catalyzed by a base (B⁻): 1) Base deprotonates the 3-position of 5-nitrobenzisoxazole, forming a negatively charged transition state with partial charges (δ⁺ on B, δ⁻ on O), 2) Ring opening occurs to produce 2-cyano-4-nitrophenolate (product) plus BH.

Three-step computational enzyme design workflow: Step 1: Modular assembly of protein building blocks. Step 2: Stability design using the PROSS method. Step 3: Geometric matching and filtering using a sigmoid scoring function f = ∏ 1/(1+e^((x-μ)/σ)) to match active site geometry to the transition state.

Continuation of design and experimental workflow: Step 4: Active site and core position stabilization. Step 5: Experimental screening: protein expression, purification, and kinetic assay monitoring absorbance at 380 nm as a function of substrate concentration. Step 6: Active-site optimization of residue interactions.

Bar chart of catalytic efficiency (k_cat/K_M, units: M⁻¹ s⁻¹, log₁₀ y-axis) for 12 designed enzyme variants (Des27, Des27.1 to Des27.13, excluding Des27.8). The highest efficiency variant is Des27.7 with k_cat/K_M = ~10⁴ M⁻¹ s⁻¹, followed by Des27.12 (~3.5×10³), Des27.9 (~2×10³), Des27.3 (~1×10³), Des27.13 (~1.8×10³), Des27.11 (~6×10²), Des27.10 (~3×10²), Des27.1 (~3×10²), Des27 (~1×10²), Des27.6 (~8×10¹), Des27.4 (~5×10¹), Des27.5 (~4×10¹), Des27.2 (~2×10¹). A dashed horizontal line marks 10² M⁻¹ s⁻¹.

Michaelis-Menten kinetic curve for Des27.7 with 5-nitrobenzisoxazole as substrate, measuring initial velocity V₀ (units: mOD min⁻¹) as a function of substrate concentration (units: mM). The fitted kinetic parameters are: K_M = 0.21 ± 0.06 mM, k_cat = 2.85 ± 1.2 s⁻¹, k_cat/K_M = 12,696 ± 1,738 M⁻¹ s⁻¹. Measured data points are plotted as black circles, with the fitted curve as a black line.

Crystal structure (xtal) overlay with computational model of Des27.7 active site: key active site residues are F113, I136, D162 (catalytic base), V183, I216, V236. The model (light blue) and crystal structure (gray) show high agreement for the bound substrate/transition state analog and surrounding residues.

Sequence and activity table for designed variants, showing amino acid identities at positions 54, 84, 86, 92, 111, 115, 136, 183, 216, 236, along with measured k_cat/K_M (M⁻¹ s⁻¹) and calculated Asp162 van der Waals (vdW) energy (R.e.u, negative values indicate more favorable packing). The variants ordered by increasing k_cat/K_M: Des27.2 (21, -4.9), Des27.5 (54, -4.9), Des27.4 (59, -4.9), Des27.6 (90, -4.9), Des27 (130, -5.1), Des27.10 (327, -5.2), Des27.1 (344, -5.2), Des27.11 (718, -5.2), Des27.3 (1294, -5.2), Des27.13 (2014, -5.1), Des27.9 (2136, -5.5), Des27.12 (3837, -5.4), Des27.7 (12696, -5.4).

Conformational state distribution pie/stacked bar charts for Des27 and Des27.7: For Des27: 63% Unbound, 37% Bound; of bound states, 11% are In (reactive conformation, nucleophile aligned for attack), 66% are Out (non-reactive), 22% Other. For Des27.7: 12% Unbound, 88% Bound; of bound states, 23% are In, 57% are Out, 19% Other. The improved variant has higher bound fraction and higher fraction of reactive bound conformations.

Structural definition of reactive ('In') and non-reactive ('Out') substrate binding conformations: In the In conformation (left, blue), the substrate C3 proton is oriented toward the catalytic base D162 for deprotonation. In the Out conformation (right, orange), the substrate is flipped, so the proton is facing away from D162, preventing reaction.

Comparison of catalytic base (D162) packing in Des27 (left, green) and Des27.7 (right, blue): Des27 has L183, L136, I216 surrounding D162; Des27.7 has smaller residues V183, I136, I216, improving packing of the catalytic base and reducing conformational flexibility.

Steric clash comparison: Des27 (left, green) has L236 which causes steric overlap with the substrate/transition state. Des27.7 (right, blue) has a smaller V236 residue, eliminating this steric clash and improving substrate binding.

Ligand binding environment comparison: Des27 (left, green) has hydrophobic residues L183, I54, F92 surrounding the ligand. Des27.7 (right, blue) has smaller/ more polar residues V183, V54, H92, optimizing desolvation and binding interactions for the substrate.

Activation free energy (ΔG‡, units: kcal mol⁻¹) bar chart for Des27 and Des27.7, comparing experimental values and calculated values for In and Out conformations: For Des27: Experimental ΔG‡ = 19.1 ± 0.3 kcal mol⁻¹, Out ΔG‡ = 17.7 ± 2.2 kcal mol⁻¹, In ΔG‡ = 16.7 ± 2.1 kcal mol⁻¹. For Des27.7: Experimental ΔG‡ = 16.7 ± 0.2 kcal mol⁻¹, Out ΔG‡ = 15.9 ± 1.7 kcal mol⁻¹, In ΔG‡ = 13.6 ± 0.6 kcal mol⁻¹. The experimental value aligns closer to the Out conformation energy for Des27, and is intermediate for Des27.7, consistent with the population of both states.

Stability (melting temperature T_m, left axis, °C) and catalytic efficiency (k_cat/K_M, right axis, M⁻¹ s⁻¹, log scale) for designed variants with different combinations of design steps: 1) Des27.7 (all steps: modular assembly, PROSS (11 muts), active site (15 muts), core stabilization (6 muts)): T_m = 86 °C, k_cat/K_M = 12,700 M⁻¹ s⁻¹. 2) Variant without PROSS: T_m = 57 °C, k_cat/K_M = ND (not detectable). 3) Variant without active site optimization: T_m = 69 °C, k_cat/K_M = ND. 4) Variant without core stabilization: T_m = 60 °C, k_cat/K_M = 2,900 M⁻¹ s⁻¹. 5) Variant without core stabilization + alternative mutations: T_m = 81 °C, k_cat/K_M = 11,500 M⁻¹ s⁻¹. 6) D162A catalytic base mutant: T_m >100 °C, k_cat/K_M = ND. 7) F113L mutant: T_m = 82 °C, k_cat/K_M = 123,000 M⁻¹ s⁻¹ (highest efficiency variant). ND indicates no detectable activity.

The dotted gray line at 4 Å marks the threshold for defining the ligand as being within the active site. In two out of ten replicas across the two systems,
427: we observe ligand dissociation without rebinding towards the end of the trajectory on the timescale of our simulations; in a third, in the case of Des27.7, we observe substantial dissociation mid-trajectory with ligand rebinding within the simulation timeframes.

payload.content: Deprotonation reaction mechanism of 5-nitrobenzisoxazole catalyzed by a base (B⁻): 1) Base deprotonates the 3-position of 5-nitrobenzisoxazole, forming a negatively charged transition state with partial charges (δ⁺ on B, δ⁻ on O), 2) Ring opening occurs to produce 2-cyano-4-nitrophenolate (product) plus BH.

ND indicates no detectable activity.

analysis_results.content: Reaction mechanism for base-mediated cleavage of 5-nitrobenzisoxazole: a base (B⁻) deprotonates the 3-position of 5-nitrobenzisoxazole, forming a negatively charged transition state with partial positive charge on the abstracted hydrogen (δ⁺) and partial negative charge on the oxazole oxygen (δ⁻). The products are 2-cyano-4-nitrophenolate and BH. This is the reaction catalyzed by the designed enzymes.

Enzyme design workflow: Step 1: Modular assembly of protein domains; Step 2: Stability design using the PROSS/TRP tool (red spheres indicate mutation positions); Step 3: Geometric matching and filtering using a Fermi function scoring: f = Π [1 / (1 + e^((x-μ)/σ))], followed by ligand matching to the active site.

Continued enzyme design workflow: Step 4: Active site and core position stabilization (green spheres: core positions, purple spheres: active site positions); Step 5: Experimental screening including protein expression, purification, and absorbance-based kinetic assay at 380 nm measuring rate as a function of substrate concentration; Step 6: Active-site optimization of residue positions to improve catalysis.

Bar chart of kcat/KM (units: M⁻¹ s⁻¹, log10 Y-axis) for 12 designed enzyme variants: Des27 (110 M⁻¹ s⁻¹), Des27.1 (280 M⁻¹ s⁻¹), Des27.2 (18 M⁻¹ s⁻¹), Des27.3 (1200 M⁻¹ s⁻¹), Des27.4 (45 M⁻¹ s⁻¹), Des27.5 (40 M⁻¹ s⁻¹), Des27.6 (75 M⁻¹ s⁻¹), Des27.7 (11500 M⁻¹ s⁻¹), Des27.9 (2000 M⁻¹ s⁻¹), Des27.10 (280 M⁻¹ s⁻¹), Des27.11 (550 M⁻¹ s⁻¹), Des27.12 (3800 M⁻¹ s⁻¹), Des27.13 (1800 M⁻¹ s⁻¹). The highest activity is observed for Des27.7, ~100-fold higher than the parent Des27.

Michaelis-Menten kinetic curve for Des27.7 with 5-nitrobenzisoxazole substrate (X-axis: [5-nitrobenzisoxazole] in mM, Y-axis: initial rate V₀ in mOD min⁻¹). Fitted kinetic parameters: K_M = 0.21 ± 0.06 mM, k_cat = 2.85 ± 1.2 s⁻¹, k_cat/K_M = 12696 ± 1738 M⁻¹ s⁻¹.

Crystal structure of Des27.7 active site (Des27.7 xtal, gray sticks) overlaid with the computational design model (Des27.7 model, light blue sticks). Key labeled active site residues: F113, I136, D162 (catalytic base), V183, I216, V236, all surrounding the bound substrate analog.

Sequence and activity table for 12 designed variants, showing residues at positions 54, 84, 86, 92, 111, 115, 136, 183, 216, 236, with corresponding kcat/KM values (M⁻¹ s⁻¹) and Asp162 van der Waals (vdW) interaction energy (R.e.u, negative values indicate favorable interactions). The highest activity variant Des27.7 has Ile at positions 54, 86, 136, 216, His at 92, Ile at 111, Phe at 115, Val at 183, Val at 236, kcat/KM = 12696 M⁻¹ s⁻¹, and vdW = -5.4 R.e.u.

Binding pose population analysis for Des27 and Des27.7: Des27: 63% unbound, 37% bound; of bound poses, 11% are reactive (correct orientation for catalysis, 'In'), 66% are non-reactive ('Out'), 22% other. Des27.7: 12% unbound, 88% bound; of bound poses, 23% are reactive ('In'), 57% are non-reactive ('Out'), 19% other. Des27.7 has a ~2x higher fraction of reactive bound poses than Des27, plus higher total bound fraction.

Two binding poses of 5-nitrobenzisoxazole: 'In' (reactive, blue pose, correct orientation for deprotonation by D162) and 'Out' (non-reactive, orange pose, flipped orientation not suitable for catalysis).

Catalytic base packing comparison: Des27 model (green) has Leu at positions 136, 183, Ile at 216, packing around catalytic base D162. Des27.7 model (blue) has Ile at 136, Val at 183, Ile at 216, resulting in tighter packing around D162 to stabilize the charged transition state.

Steric overlap relief: Des27 has Leu at position 236, which causes steric clash with the substrate (green). Des27.7 has Val at position 236 (smaller side chain), eliminating steric overlap with the substrate (blue) to improve binding.

Ligand solvation comparison: Des27 (green) has Ile at 54, Leu at 183, Phe at 92, forming a more hydrophobic active site. Des27.7 (blue) has Val at 54, Val at 183, His at 92, improving polar interactions with the substrate nitro group and optimizing solvation for the charged transition state.

Activation free energy (ΔG‡, kcal mol⁻¹) for Des27 and Des27.7: Des27: experimental ΔG‡ = 19.1 kcal mol⁻¹, predicted ΔG‡ for 'Out' pose = 17.7 kcal mol⁻¹, predicted ΔG‡ for 'In' pose = 16.7 kcal mol⁻¹. Des27.7: experimental ΔG‡ = 16.7 kcal mol⁻¹, predicted ΔG‡ for 'Out' pose = 15.9 kcal mol⁻¹, predicted ΔG‡ for 'In' pose = 13.6 kcal mol⁻¹. The experimental values align closely with the predicted 'In' (reactive) pose energies, confirming the reactive orientation.

Stability-activity tradeoff analysis for designed variants, showing Tm (melting temperature, °C, left X-axis) and kcat/KM (M⁻¹ s⁻¹, right log X-axis): Des27.7 has Tm = 86 °C, kcat/KM = 12700 M⁻¹ s⁻¹; another variant has Tm = 57 °C, ND (no detected activity); variant with Tm = 69 °C, ND; variant with Tm = 60 °C, kcat/KM = 2900 M⁻¹ s⁻¹; variant with Tm = 81 °C, kcat/KM = 11500 M⁻¹ s⁻¹; D162A mutant (catalytic base knockout) has Tm >100 °C, ND; F113L mutant has Tm = 82 °C, kcat/KM = 123000 M⁻¹ s⁻¹ (10-fold higher activity than Des27.7, with similar stability). The design steps: Modular assembly, PROSS (11 mutations, stability), Active site (15 mutations), Core stabilization (6 mutations) are shown as checkboxes for each variant.

payload.content: Reaction mechanism for base-mediated cleavage of 5-nitrobenzisoxazole: a base (B⁻) deprotonates the 3-position of 5-nitrobenzisoxazole, forming a negatively charged transition state with partial positive charge on the abstracted hydrogen (δ⁺) and partial negative charge on the oxazole oxygen (δ⁻). The products are 2-cyano-4-nitrophenolate and BH. This is the reaction catalyzed by the designed enzymes.

Enzyme design workflow: Step 1: Modular assembly of protein domains; Step 2: Stability design using the PROSS/TRP tool (red spheres indicate mutation positions); Step 3: Geometric matching and filtering using a Fermi function scoring: f = Π [1 / (1 + e^((x-μ)/σ))], followed by ligand matching to the active site.

payload.content: Activation free energy (ΔG‡, kcal mol⁻¹) for Des27 and Des27.7: Des27: experimental ΔG‡ = 19.1 kcal mol⁻¹, predicted ΔG‡ for 'Out' pose = 17.7 kcal mol⁻¹, predicted ΔG‡ for 'In' pose = 16.7 kcal mol⁻¹. Des27.7: experimental ΔG‡ = 16.7 kcal mol⁻¹, predicted ΔG‡ for 'Out' pose = 15.9 kcal mol⁻¹, predicted ΔG‡ for 'In' pose = 13.6 kcal mol⁻¹. The experimental values align closely with the predicted 'In' (reactive) pose energies, confirming the reactive orientation.

payload.content: Stability-activity tradeoff analysis for designed variants, showing Tm (melting temperature, °C, left X-axis) and kcat/KM (M⁻¹ s⁻¹, right log X-axis): Des27.7 has Tm = 86 °C, kcat/KM = 12700 M⁻¹ s⁻¹; another variant has Tm = 57 °C, ND (no detected activity); variant with Tm = 69 °C, ND; variant with Tm = 60 °C, kcat/KM = 2900 M⁻¹ s⁻¹; variant with Tm = 81 °C, kcat/KM = 11500 M⁻¹ s⁻¹; D162A mutant (catalytic base knockout) has Tm >100 °C, ND; F113L mutant has Tm = 82 °C, kcat/KM = 123000 M⁻¹ s⁻¹ (10-fold higher activity than Des27.7, with similar stability). The design steps: Modular assembly, PROSS (11 mutations, stability), Active site (15 mutations), Core stabilization (6 mutations) are shown as checkboxes for each variant.

analysis_results.content: Reaction mechanism for base-catalyzed ring opening of 5-nitrobenzisoxazole: (1) Base (B⁻) deprotonates the 3-position of 5-nitrobenzisoxazole, forming a negatively charged transition state with partial negative charge on oxygen (δ⁻) and partial positive charge on the protonated base (δ⁺). (2) The ring opens to form 2-cyano-4-nitrophenolate anion and protonated base (BH).

Protein engineering workflow: Step 1: Modular assembly of protein scaffolds. Step 2: Stability design using the PROSS algorithm (wrench icon labeled 'PROSS TRP') to identify stabilizing mutations (pink spheres on protein backbone). Step 3: Geometric matching and filtering using a Fermi function f = Π 1/(1 + e^((x-μ)/σ)) to identify designs with matching active site geometry (inset shows ligand bound in active site).

Continued protein engineering workflow: Step 4: Active site and core position stabilization (green spheres represent additional stabilizing mutations). Step 5: Experimental screening: protein expression, purification, and kinetic assay measuring absorbance at 380 nm as a function of substrate concentration, performed in 96-well plates. Step 6: Active-site optimization of residue positions to improve catalysis.

Bar chart of kcat/KM values (logarithmic y-axis, units M⁻¹ s⁻¹) for 12 Des27 enzyme variants: Des27 (110), Des27.1 (320), Des27.2 (20), Des27.3 (1200), Des27.4 (50), Des27.5 (45), Des27.6 (80), Des27.7 (11500), Des27.9 (2100), Des27.10 (280), Des27.11 (600), Des27.12 (3700), Des27.13 (1900). Des27.7 is the highest-performing variant, with ~100-fold higher activity than the parent Des27.

Michaelis-Menten kinetics plot for variant Des27.7, with x-axis [5-nitrobenzisoxazole] (mM) and y-axis initial velocity V₀ (mOD min⁻¹). Fitted kinetic parameters: K_M = 0.21 ± 0.06 mM, k_cat = 2.85 ± 1.2 s⁻¹, k_cat/K_M = 12696 ± 1738 M⁻¹ s⁻¹. Scatter points are experimental measurements, solid line is the fitted Michaelis-Menten curve.

Crystal structure alignment of Des27.7 (gray, 'Des27.7 xtal') and the computational design model (light blue, 'Des27.7 model'), showing active site residues: F113, I136, D162, V183, I216, V236, plus the 5-nitrobenzisoxazole ligand. The crystal structure matches the designed active site geometry closely.

Sequence alignment table for Des27 variants, showing amino acid identities at 10 positions (54, 84, 86, 92, 111, 115, 136, 183, 216, 236), with measured k_cat/K_M (M⁻¹ s⁻¹) and calculated Asp162 van der Waals (vdW) interaction energy (R.e.u.). Activity ranges from 21 M⁻¹ s⁻¹ (Des27.2) to 12696 M⁻¹ s⁻¹ (Des27.7). Asp162 vdW energy is between -4.9 and -5.5 R.e.u. across all variants, with more negative values (stronger interactions) for higher-activity variants.

Pie and stacked bar charts comparing binding and reactive conformer populations for Des27 (left) and Des27.7 (right): Des27: 63% unbound, 37% bound; of bound conformers, 11% are reactive 'In' (proton close to catalytic base), 66% 'Out' (proton far from base), 22% other. Des27.7: 12% unbound, 88% bound; of bound conformers, 23% 'In', 57% 'Out', 19% other. Des27.7 has a higher fraction of bound substrate and a 2x higher fraction of reactive bound conformers.

Structural representation of the two substrate binding modes: 'In' (blue): nitro group oriented toward the catalytic base (productive for deprotonation); 'Out' (orange): nitro group oriented away from the catalytic base (non-productive).

Active site packing comparison: Des27 model (left, green) has residues L136, L183, I216 surrounding catalytic base D162 (red). Des27.7 model (right, blue) has mutations L136I and L183V, which tighten packing around D162, improving its positioning for proton abstraction.

Steric packing comparison: Des27 model (left, green) has L236 which clashes with the substrate, causing steric overlap. Des27.7 model (right, blue) has mutation L236V, which reduces the side chain size and eliminates steric conflict with the ligand.

Ligand solvation comparison: Des27 model (left, green) has residues I54, L183, F92 surrounding the ligand. Des27.7 model (right, blue) has mutations I54V, L183V, F92H, which improve ligand binding and solvation in the active site.

Bar chart of activation free energy ΔG‡ (kcal mol⁻¹) for Des27 and Des27.7: Experimental values: Des27 = 19.2 kcal mol⁻¹, Des27.7 = 16.7 kcal mol⁻¹. Calculated values for 'Out' binding mode: Des27 = 17.7 kcal mol⁻¹, Des27.7 = 15.9 kcal mol⁻¹. Calculated values for 'In' binding mode: Des27 = 16.7 kcal mol⁻¹, Des27.7 = 13.6 kcal mol⁻¹. The experimental activation energy is 2.5 kcal mol⁻¹ lower for Des27.7, consistent with its higher catalytic activity.

Bar chart showing melting temperature Tₘ (°C, left axis, yellow bars) and k_cat/K_M (M⁻¹ s⁻¹, right logarithmic axis, blue bars) for design variants with different combinations of engineering steps: (1) Full design (all steps: modular assembly + PROSS (11 muts) + active site (15 muts) + core stabilization (6 muts)): Des27.7 Tₘ = 86 °C, k_cat/K_M = 12700 M⁻¹ s⁻¹; F113L mutant Tₘ = 82 °C, k_cat/K_M = 123000 M⁻¹ s⁻¹ (10x higher activity). (2) Designs missing PROSS step: two variants with ND (not detected) activity, Tₘ = 57/69 °C. (3) Design missing active site step: Tₘ = 60 °C, k_cat/K_M = 2900 M⁻¹ s⁻¹. (4) Design missing core stabilization step: Tₘ = 81 °C, k_cat/K_M = 11500 M⁻¹ s⁻¹. (5) D162A catalytic dead mutant: Tₘ > 100 °C, no activity (ND). Combining all engineering steps yields high stability and high activity.

payload.content: Sequence alignment table for Des27 variants, showing amino acid identities at 10 positions (54, 84, 86, 92, 111, 115, 136, 183, 216, 236), with measured k_cat/K_M (M⁻¹ s⁻¹) and calculated Asp162 van der Waals (vdW) interaction energy (R.e.u.). Activity ranges from 21 M⁻¹ s⁻¹ (Des27.2) to 12696 M⁻¹ s⁻¹ (Des27.7). Asp162 vdW energy is between -4.9 and -5.5 R.e.u. across all variants, with more negative values (stronger interactions) for higher-activity variants.

payload.content: Bar chart showing melting temperature Tₘ (°C, left axis, yellow bars) and k_cat/K_M (M⁻¹ s⁻¹, right logarithmic axis, blue bars) for design variants with different combinations of engineering steps: (1) Full design (all steps: modular assembly + PROSS (11 muts) + active site (15 muts) + core stabilization (6 muts)): Des27.7 Tₘ = 86 °C, k_cat/K_M = 12700 M⁻¹ s⁻¹; F113L mutant Tₘ = 82 °C, k_cat/K_M = 123000 M⁻¹ s⁻¹ (10x higher activity). (2) Designs missing PROSS step: two variants with ND (not detected) activity, Tₘ = 57/69 °C. (3) Design missing active site step: Tₘ = 60 °C, k_cat/K_M = 2900 M⁻¹ s⁻¹. (4) Design missing core stabilization step: Tₘ = 81 °C, k_cat/K_M = 11500 M⁻¹ s⁻¹. (5) D162A catalytic dead mutant: Tₘ > 100 °C, no activity (ND). Combining all engineering steps yields high stability and high activity.

anchors.snippet: The underlying reasons for the low efficiencies of de novo designed enzymes have been intensely studied10,17–19. These analyses revealed that the designed active sites exhibited significant structural distortions relative to the design conception17,19. Notably, catalysis is extremely sensitive to molecular details, and shifts of the catalytic constellation by a few degrees or tenths of an Ångstrom from optimality may translate into orders of magnitude decreases in efficiency20. Furthermore, designs often exhibited low stability and expressibility7 , limiting their ability to accommodate activity-enhancing mutations7,21. Further concerns were that fixed-backbone design methods fail to precisely position non-native catalytic groups1 ; the molecular details of the designed transition state (theozyme) were uncertain6,22; and that protein dynamics23 and long-range electrostatic interactions may be necessary to achieve high catalytic efficiency but are unaccounted for in the design process6,24,25.
anchors.excerpt:

25: The underlying reasons for the low efficiencies of de novo designed enzymes have been intensely studied10,17–19. These analyses revealed that the designed active sites exhibited significant structural distortions relative to the design conception17,19. Notably, catalysis is extremely sensitive to molecular details, and shifts of the catalytic constellation by a few degrees or tenths of an Ångstrom from optimality may translate into orders of magnitude decreases in efficiency20. Furthermore, designs often exhibited low stability and expressibility7 , limiting their ability to accommodate activity-enhancing mutations7,21. Further concerns were that fixed-backbone design methods fail to precisely position non-native catalytic groups1 ; the molecular details of the designed transition state (theozyme) were uncertain6,22; and that protein dynamics23 and long-range electrostatic interactions may be necessary to achieve high catalytic efficiency but are unaccounted for in the design process6,24,25.

anchors.snippet: Crystals were grown at $1 9 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ using the sitting-drop vapour diffusion method. Diffraction data were collected from a single crystal flash-cooled to 100 K, using a wavelength of 1.34 Å. Data were collected using an in-house Rigaku liquid-metal-jet X-ray Synergy System with a HyPix Arc $1 5 0 ^ { \circ }$ detector. AlphaFold2 (ref. 57) was used to generate all three models for molecular replacement (Extended Data Table 2). Initial models were iteratively rebuilt and refined using COOT63 and PHENIX64. Model geometry was evaluated using MOLPROBITY65. Atomic coordinates and structure factors for Des27.7, R2.Des39 and R2.Des49 are deposited in the PDB database under accession numbers 9HVB, 9HVH and 9HVG, respectively.

anchors.snippet: In total, 66 designs were solubly expressed and 14 showed cooperative thermal denaturation (Extended Data Fig. 1). Three designs showed measurable KE activity in an initial screen, with the top two designs, Des27 and Des61, exhibiting ${ k _ { \mathrm { { c a t } } } } / { K _ { \mathrm { { M } } } }$ values of 130 and 210 $\mathsf { M } ^ { - 1 } \mathsf { S } ^ { - 1 }$ , respectively, and $k _ { \mathrm { c a t } } < 1 \mathsf { s } ^ { - 1 }$ (Extended Data Fig. 2, Extended Data Table 1 and Supplementary Table 1).
