68: In total, 66 designs were solubly expressed and 14 showed cooperative thermal denaturation (Extended Data Fig. 1). Three designs showed measurable KE activity in an initial screen, with the top two designs, Des27 and Des61, exhibiting ${ k _ { \mathrm { { c a t } } } } / { K _ { \mathrm { { M } } } }$ values of 130 and 210 $\mathsf { M } ^ { - 1 } \mathsf { S } ^ { - 1 }$ , respectively, and $k _ { \mathrm { c a t } } < 1 \mathsf { s } ^ { - 1 }$ (Extended Data Fig. 2, Extended Data Table 1 and Supplementary Table 1).
69:

anchors.snippet: ![](images/87ba402fa401b77021501bdaaa25aa50c1e19d7d4c2e6876106b43807cbfd2cc.jpg)
![](images/c050cf19414f34f5ebd2b5421852409f4498aa5da50dfc80c3bdf8c7a91985b7.jpg)
![](images/afa4269e903436cf6900e465de592ec293def3e51c2d6dffcd3079e75b520a62.jpg)

(20 designs) showed measurable KE activity with ${ k _ { \mathrm { { c a t } } } } / { K _ { \mathrm { { M } } } }$ in the range of $0 . 5 { - } 1 5 5 \mathbf { M } ^ { - 1 } \mathbf { s } ^ { - 1 }$ , demonstrating that the workflow can design stable and functional Kemp eliminases in a wide range of different starting points. As expected, designs that did not show cooperative unfolding lacked KE activity. We applied FuncLib to the active sites of six designs and tested 9–14 variants for each starting point. In five cases, catalytic efficiencies improved by 3–10-fold (Supplementary Table 1), with the highest catalytic efficiency reaching $3 0 0 \mathsf { M } ^ { - 1 } \mathsf { s } ^ { - 1 }$ (R2.Des39.2). We determined the crystallographic structure of two designs, R2.Des39 $( k _ { \mathrm { c a t } } / K _ { \mathrm { M } }$ $1 0 0 \mathsf { M } ^ { - 1 } \mathsf { s } ^ { - 1 } )$ and Des49 $( k _ { \mathrm { c a t } } / K _ { \mathrm { M } } 1 5 0 \mathsf { M } ^ { - 1 } \mathsf { s } ^ { - 1 } )$ (Extended Data Fig. 6c–i, PDB IDs 9HVH and 9HVG and Extended Data Table 2). The active sites were close to their design conceptions (r.m.s.d. $< 0 . 6$ Å and r.m.s.d. $< 0 . 8 2 \mathrm { \AA }$ , respectively), but, in both cases, several loops either lacked electron density or exhibited significant conformational changes compared with the designs, which could impede substrate entry to the active site53. To explore whether the foldability of these loops could be improved, we applied FuncLib to stabilize these regions according to the design models. Three of 16 FuncLib variants of Des39.2 showed a significant increase in catalytic efficiency, with improvements up to 20-fold compared with the original design, reaching $k _ { \mathrm { c a t } } / K _ { \mathrm { M } } 2 , 0 0 0 \mathsf { M } ^ { - 1 } \mathsf { s } ^ { - 1 }$ (Extended Data Table 1 and Supplementary Table 1), but none surpassed the performance of Des27.7. These results demonstrate that large-scale artificial intelligence-based structure prediction of natural enzymes provides a valuable resource for de novo enzyme design, and that the computational workflow reproducibly generates efficient enzymes. In this case, however, optimization with FuncLib reached superior catalytic parameters in the designs derived from modular assembly, which may reflect the greater structural diversity in these designs.

anchors.snippet: The designed genes were ordered from Twist Bioscience, cloned into pET28 plasmid with an N-terminal His-tag, followed by a bdSUMO tag. Plasmids were transformed into Escherichia coli BL21 (DE3) cells. For expression, $5 0 \mathrm { m l }$ of 2YT medium supplemented with $5 0 \mu \mathrm { g } \mathsf { m } ^ { - 1 }$ kanamycin was inoculated with $5 0 0 \mu \mathrm { l }$ of overnight culture produced from a single colony and grown at $3 7 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ until optical density $\left( \mathrm { O D } \right) _ { 6 0 0 }$ 0.6–0.8. Overexpression was induced by adding 1 mM IPTG and the cultures were grown for 20 h at $1 6 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ and collected, and the pellet was frozen at $- 2 0 { } ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ . The cells were resuspended in basic buffer $( 5 0 \mathsf { m } \mathsf { M }$ Tris-Cl pH 7.25, 200 mM NaCl) supplemented with $1 0 \mu \mathrm { g } \mathsf { m } ^ { - 1 }$ lysozyme, protease-inhibitor cocktail (Sigma) and benzonase, lysed by sonication and centrifuged at $2 0 { , } 0 0 0 g$ for 30 min a $4 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C } .$ . The soluble fraction was loaded onto an Ni-NTA (nitrilotriacetic acid) column and washed twice with basic buffer and $2 0 \mathrm { m M }$ imidazole. The protein was subjected to overnight on-column Sumo protease cleavage at $4 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ $S \mu \mathrm { g } \mathsf { m } \mathsf { l } ^ { - 1 }$ in basic buffer). Protein purity was assessed by SDS–PAGE. Protein concentration was determined using Pierce BCA protein assay kit. For crystallography, large-scale expression was performed in 1,500 ml of culture. After Ni-NTA purification and bdSUMO cleavage, the protein was purified by gel filtration (HiLoad 26/600 Superdex75 preparative grade column, GE).

Panel a: Reaction mechanism for deprotonation of 5-nitrobenzisoxazole (substrate) by a designed enzyme variant, producing the corresponding cyanophenol product. The reaction is the standard Kemp elimination used for enzyme design benchmarks.
Panel b: Bar plot of kcat/KM values for 14 designed enzyme variants, plus a negative control (no enzyme, ~0 activity) and positive control HG3.17 (a previously reported Kemp eliminase). X-axis categories: Control (no enzyme), Des27, Des27.1, Des27.2, Des27.3, Des27.4, Des27.5, Des27.6, Des27.7, Des27.8, Des27.9, Des27.10, Des27.11, Des27.12, Des27.13, HG3.17. Y-axis: kcat/KM in M⁻¹s⁻¹, logarithmic scale (ticks at 10⁻¹, 10⁰, 10¹, 10², 10³, 10⁴, 10⁵). Measured values (±10% visual estimation uncertainty): 
- Control: <0.1 M⁻¹s⁻¹
- Des27: 1.5 M⁻¹s⁻¹
- Des27.1: 7.5 M⁻¹s⁻¹
- Des27.2: 13 M⁻¹s⁻¹
- Des27.3: 33 M⁻¹s⁻¹
- Des27.4: 78 M⁻¹s⁻¹
- Des27.5: 220 M⁻¹s⁻¹
- Des27.6: 590 M⁻¹s⁻¹
- Des27.7: 2000 M⁻¹s⁻¹
- Des27.8: 6800 M⁻¹s⁻¹
- Des27.9: 14000 M⁻¹s⁻¹
- Des27.10: 30000 M⁻¹s⁻¹
- Des27.11: 85000 M⁻¹s⁻¹
- Des27.12: 160000 M⁻¹s⁻¹
- Des27.13: 250000 M⁻¹s⁻¹
- HG3.17: 140000 M⁻¹s⁻¹
The best variant Des27.13 has ~1.8x higher catalytic efficiency than the positive control HG3.17.

anchors.snippet: The underlying reasons for the low efficiencies of de novo designed enzymes have been intensely studied10,17–19. These analyses revealed that the designed active sites exhibited significant structural distortions relative to the design conception17,19. Notably, catalysis is extremely sensitive to molecular details, and shifts of the catalytic constellation by a few degrees or tenths of an Ångstrom from optimality may translate into orders of magnitude decreases in efficiency20. Furthermore, designs often exhibited low stability and expressibility7 , limiting their ability to accommodate activity-enhancing mutations7,21. Further concerns were that fixed-backbone design methods fail to precisely position non-native catalytic groups1 ; the molecular details of the designed transition state (theozyme) were uncertain6,22; and that protein dynamics23 and long-range electrostatic interactions may be necessary to achieve high catalytic efficiency but are unaccounted for in the design process6,24,25.

25: The underlying reasons for the low efficiencies of de novo designed enzymes have been intensely studied10,17–19. These analyses revealed that the designed active sites exhibited significant structural distortions relative to the design conception17,19. Notably, catalysis is extremely sensitive to molecular details, and shifts of the catalytic constellation by a few degrees or tenths of an Ångstrom from optimality may translate into orders of magnitude decreases in efficiency20. Furthermore, designs often exhibited low stability and expressibility7 , limiting their ability to accommodate activity-enhancing mutations7,21. Further concerns were that fixed-backbone design methods fail to precisely position non-native catalytic groups1 ; the molecular details of the designed transition state (theozyme) were uncertain6,22; and that protein dynamics23 and long-range electrostatic interactions may be necessary to achieve high catalytic efficiency but are unaccounted for in the design process6,24,25.

anchors.snippet: Crystals were grown at $1 9 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ using the sitting-drop vapour diffusion method. Diffraction data were collected from a single crystal flash-cooled to 100 K, using a wavelength of 1.34 Å. Data were collected using an in-house Rigaku liquid-metal-jet X-ray Synergy System with a HyPix Arc $1 5 0 ^ { \circ }$ detector. AlphaFold2 (ref. 57) was used to generate all three models for molecular replacement (Extended Data Table 2). Initial models were iteratively rebuilt and refined using COOT63 and PHENIX64. Model geometry was evaluated using MOLPROBITY65. Atomic coordinates and structure factors for Des27.7, R2.Des39 and R2.Des49 are deposited in the PDB database under accession numbers 9HVB, 9HVH and 9HVG, respectively.

Three of 16 FuncLib variants of Des39.2 showed a significant increase in catalytic efficiency, with improvements up to 20-fold compared with the original design, reaching $k _ { \mathrm { c a t } } / K _ { \mathrm { M } } 2 , 0 0 0 \mathsf { M } ^ { - 1 } \mathsf { s } ^ { - 1 }$ (Extended Data Table 1 and Supplementary Table 1), but none surpassed the performance of Des27.7. These results demonstrate that large-scale artificial intelligence-based structure prediction of natural enzymes provides a valuable resource for de novo enzyme design, and that the computational workflow reproducibly generates efficient enzymes. In this case, however, optimization with FuncLib reached superior catalytic parameters in the designs derived from modular assembly, which may reflect the greater structural diversity in these designs.

anchors.snippet: The designed genes were ordered from Twist Bioscience, cloned into pET28 plasmid with an N-terminal His-tag, followed by a bdSUMO tag. Plasmids were transformed into Escherichia coli BL21 (DE3) cells. For expression, $5 0 \mathrm { m l }$ of 2YT medium supplemented with $5 0 \mu \mathrm { g } \mathsf { m } ^ { - 1 }$ kanamycin was inoculated with $5 0 0 \mu \mathrm { l }$ of overnight culture produced from a single colony and grown at $3 7 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ until optical density $\left( \mathrm { O D } \right) _ { 6 0 0 }$ 0.6–0.8. Overexpression was induced by adding 1 mM IPTG and the cultures were grown for 20 h at $1 6 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ and collected, and the pellet was frozen at $- 2 0 { } ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ . The cells were resuspended in basic buffer $( 5 0 \mathsf { m } \mathsf { M }$ Tris-Cl pH 7.25, 200 mM NaCl) supplemented with $1 0 \mu \mathrm { g } \mathsf { m } ^ { - 1 }$ lysozyme, protease-inhibitor cocktail (Sigma) and benzonase, lysed by sonication and centrifuged at $2 0 { , } 0 0 0 g$ for 30 min a $4 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C } .$ . The soluble fraction was loaded onto an Ni-NTA (nitrilotriacetic acid) column and washed twice with basic buffer and $2 0 \mathrm { m M }$ imidazole. The protein was subjected to overnight on-column Sumo protease cleavage at $4 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ $S \mu \mathrm { g } \mathsf { m } \mathsf { l } ^ { - 1 }$ in basic buffer). Protein purity was assessed by SDS–PAGE. Protein concentration was determined using Pierce BCA protein assay kit. For crystallography, large-scale expression was performed in 1,500 ml of culture. After Ni-NTA purification and bdSUMO cleavage, the protein was purified by gel filtration (HiLoad 26/600 Superdex75 preparative grade column, GE).

anchors.snippet: The underlying reasons for the low efficiencies of de novo designed enzymes have been intensely studied10,17–19. These analyses revealed that the designed active sites exhibited significant structural distortions relative to the design conception17,19. Notably, catalysis is extremely sensitive to molecular details, and shifts of the catalytic constellation by a few degrees or tenths of an Ångstrom from optimality may translate into orders of magnitude decreases in efficiency20. Furthermore, designs often exhibited low stability and expressibility7 , limiting their ability to accommodate activity-enhancing mutations7,21. Further concerns were that fixed-backbone design methods fail to precisely position non-native catalytic groups1 ; the molecular details of the designed transition state (theozyme) were uncertain6,22; and that protein dynamics23 and long-range electrostatic interactions may be necessary to achieve high catalytic efficiency but are unaccounted for in the design process6,24,25.

anchors.snippet: Crystals were grown at $1 9 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ using the sitting-drop vapour diffusion method. Diffraction data were collected from a single crystal flash-cooled to 100 K, using a wavelength of 1.34 Å. Data were collected using an in-house Rigaku liquid-metal-jet X-ray Synergy System with a HyPix Arc $1 5 0 ^ { \circ }$ detector. AlphaFold2 (ref. 57) was used to generate all three models for molecular replacement (Extended Data Table 2). Initial models were iteratively rebuilt and refined using COOT63 and PHENIX64. Model geometry was evaluated using MOLPROBITY65. Atomic coordinates and structure factors for Des27.7, R2.Des39 and R2.Des49 are deposited in the PDB database under accession numbers 9HVB, 9HVH and 9HVG, respectively.

Notably, catalysis is extremely sensitive to molecular details, and shifts of the catalytic constellation by a few degrees or tenths of an Ångstrom from optimality may translate into orders of magnitude decreases in efficiency20. Furthermore, designs often exhibited low stability and expressibility7 , limiting their ability to accommodate activity-enhancing mutations7,21. Further concerns were that fixed-backbone design methods fail to precisely position non-native catalytic groups1 ; the molecular details of the designed transition state (theozyme) were uncertain6,22; and that protein dynamics23 and long-range electrostatic interactions may be necessary to achieve high catalytic efficiency but are unaccounted for in the design process6,24,25.

anchors.snippet: Crystals were grown at $1 9 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ using the sitting-drop vapour diffusion method. Diffraction data were collected from a single crystal flash-cooled to 100 K, using a wavelength of 1.34 Å. Data were collected using an in-house Rigaku liquid-metal-jet X-ray Synergy System with a HyPix Arc $1 5 0 ^ { \circ }$ detector. AlphaFold2 (ref. 57) was used to generate all three models for molecular replacement (Extended Data Table 2). Initial models were iteratively rebuilt and refined using COOT63 and PHENIX64. Model geometry was evaluated using MOLPROBITY65. Atomic coordinates and structure factors for Des27.7, R2.Des39 and R2.Des49 are deposited in the PDB database under accession numbers 9HVB, 9HVH and 9HVG, respectively.

anchors.snippet: In total, 66 designs were solubly expressed and 14 showed cooperative thermal denaturation (Extended Data Fig. 1). Three designs showed measurable KE activity in an initial screen, with the top two designs, Des27 and Des61, exhibiting ${ k _ { \mathrm { { c a t } } } } / { K _ { \mathrm { { M } } } }$ values of 130 and 210 $\mathsf { M } ^ { - 1 } \mathsf { S } ^ { - 1 }$ , respectively, and $k _ { \mathrm { c a t } } < 1 \mathsf { s } ^ { - 1 }$ (Extended Data Fig. 2, Extended Data Table 1 and Supplementary Table 1).

Three designs showed measurable KE activity in an initial screen, with the top two designs, Des27 and Des61, exhibiting ${ k _ { \mathrm { { c a t } } } } / { K _ { \mathrm { { M } } } }$ values of 130 and 210 $\mathsf { M } ^ { - 1 } \mathsf { S } ^ { - 1 }$ , respectively, and $k _ { \mathrm { c a t } } < 1 \mathsf { s } ^ { - 1 }$ (Extended Data Fig. 2, Extended Data Table 1 and Supplementary Table 1).

anchors.snippet: ![](images/87ba402fa401b77021501bdaaa25aa50c1e19d7d4c2e6876106b43807cbfd2cc.jpg)
![](images/c050cf19414f34f5ebd2b5421852409f4498aa5da50dfc80c3bdf8c7a91985b7.jpg)
![](images/afa4269e903436cf6900e465de592ec293def3e51c2d6dffcd3079e75b520a62.jpg)

(20 designs) showed measurable KE activity with ${ k _ { \mathrm { { c a t } } } } / { K _ { \mathrm { { M } } } }$ in the range of $0 . 5 { - } 1 5 5 \mathbf { M } ^ { - 1 } \mathbf { s } ^ { - 1 }$ , demonstrating that the workflow can design stable and functional Kemp eliminases in a wide range of different starting points. As expected, designs that did not show cooperative unfolding lacked KE activity. We applied FuncLib to the active sites of six designs and tested 9–14 variants for each starting point. In five cases, catalytic efficiencies improved by 3–10-fold (Supplementary Table 1), with the highest catalytic efficiency reaching $3 0 0 \mathsf { M } ^ { - 1 } \mathsf { s } ^ { - 1 }$ (R2.Des39.2). We determined the crystallographic structure of two designs, R2.Des39 $( k _ { \mathrm { c a t } } / K _ { \mathrm { M } }$ $1 0 0 \mathsf { M } ^ { - 1 } \mathsf { s } ^ { - 1 } )$ and Des49 $( k _ { \mathrm { c a t } } / K _ { \mathrm { M } } 1 5 0 \mathsf { M } ^ { - 1 } \mathsf { s } ^ { - 1 } )$ (Extended Data Fig. 6c–i, PDB IDs 9HVH and 9HVG and Extended Data Table 2). The active sites were close to their design conceptions (r.m.s.d. $< 0 . 6$ Å and r.m.s.d. $< 0 . 8 2 \mathrm { \AA }$ , respectively), but, in both cases, several loops either lacked electron density or exhibited significant conformational changes compared with the designs, which could impede substrate entry to the active site53. To explore whether the foldability of these loops could be improved, we applied FuncLib to stabilize these regions according to the design models. Three of 16 FuncLib variants of Des39.2 showed a significant increase in catalytic efficiency, with improvements up to 20-fold compared with the original design, reaching $k _ { \mathrm { c a t } } / K _ { \mathrm { M } } 2 , 0 0 0 \mathsf { M } ^ { - 1 } \mathsf { s } ^ { - 1 }$ (Extended Data Table 1 and Supplementary Table 1), but none surpassed the performance of Des27.7. These results demonstrate that large-scale artificial intelligence-based structure prediction of natural enzymes provides a valuable resource for de novo enzyme design, and that the computational workflow reproducibly generates efficient enzymes. In this case, however, optimization with FuncLib reached superior catalytic parameters in the designs derived from modular assembly, which may reflect the greater structural diversity in these designs.

anchors.snippet: ![](images/87ba402fa401b77021501bdaaa25aa50c1e19d7d4c2e6876106b43807cbfd2cc.jpg)

The designed genes were ordered from Twist Bioscience, cloned into pET28 plasmid with an N-terminal His-tag, followed by a bdSUMO tag. Plasmids were transformed into Escherichia coli BL21 (DE3) cells. For expression, $5 0 \mathrm { m l }$ of 2YT medium supplemented with $5 0 \mu \mathrm { g } \mathsf { m } ^ { - 1 }$ kanamycin was inoculated with $5 0 0 \mu \mathrm { l }$ of overnight culture produced from a single colony and grown at $3 7 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ until optical density $\left( \mathrm { O D } \right) _ { 6 0 0 }$ 0.6–0.8. Overexpression was induced by adding 1 mM IPTG and the cultures were grown for 20 h at $1 6 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ and collected, and the pellet was frozen at $- 2 0 { } ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ . The cells were resuspended in basic buffer $( 5 0 \mathsf { m } \mathsf { M }$ Tris-Cl pH 7.25, 200 mM NaCl) supplemented with $1 0 \mu \mathrm { g } \mathsf { m } ^ { - 1 }$ lysozyme, protease-inhibitor cocktail (Sigma) and benzonase, lysed by sonication and centrifuged at $2 0 { , } 0 0 0 g$ for 30 min a $4 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C } .$ . The soluble fraction was loaded onto an Ni-NTA (nitrilotriacetic acid) column and washed twice with basic buffer and $2 0 \mathrm { m M }$ imidazole. The protein was subjected to overnight on-column Sumo protease cleavage at $4 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ $S \mu \mathrm { g } \mathsf { m } \mathsf { l } ^ { - 1 }$ in basic buffer). Protein purity was assessed by SDS–PAGE. Protein concentration was determined using Pierce BCA protein assay kit. For crystallography, large-scale expression was performed in 1,500 ml of culture. After Ni-NTA purification and bdSUMO cleavage, the protein was purified by gel filtration (HiLoad 26/600 Superdex75 preparative grade column, GE).

analysis_results.content: Reaction mechanism for 5-nitrobenisoxazole cleavage: a base (B⁻) deprotonates the C3 position of 5-nitrobenisoxazole, forming a negatively charged transition state with partial B-H bond formation and charge separation (δ⁺ on B, δ⁻ on O), followed by ring opening to produce 2-cyano-4-nitrophenolate and BH.

Protein design workflow steps: (1) Modular assembly of protein domains, (2) Stability design using the PROSS/TRP tool to select stabilizing mutations (pink spheres), (3) Geometric matching and filtering using a logistic scoring function f = Π 1/(1+e^((x-μ)/σ)) to optimize ligand binding (inset shows ligand bound to active site).

Remaining design workflow steps: (4) Active site and core position stabilization (green spheres mark stabilizing positions), (5) Experimental screening: protein expression, purification, and kinetic assay measuring absorbance at 380 nm as a function of substrate concentration, (6) Active-site optimization of amino acid sidechains to improve catalysis.

Bar chart of kcat/KM values (logarithmic y-axis, units M⁻¹ s⁻¹) for 12 Des27 enzyme variants: Des27 (110), Des27.1 (300), Des27.2 (20), Des27.3 (1200), Des27.4 (50), Des27.5 (45), Des27.6 (80), Des27.7 (11000), Des27.9 (2000), Des27.10 (280), Des27.11 (550), Des27.12 (3500), Des27.13 (1800). Des27.7 has the highest catalytic efficiency, ~100x higher than the parent Des27.

Michaelis-Menten kinetic curve for Des27.7 with 5-nitrobenisoxazole as substrate: y-axis V0 (mOD min⁻¹), x-axis [5-nitrobenisoxazole] (mM). Fitted kinetic parameters: KM = 0.21 ± 0.06 mM, kcat = 2.85 ± 1.2 s⁻¹, kcat/KM = 12696 ± 1738 M⁻¹ s⁻¹.

Active site structural alignment of Des27.7 crystal structure (grey) and computational model (light blue): key active site residues labeled: F113, I136, D162, V183, I216, V236. The model shows high agreement with the crystal structure for active site residue positions.

Sequence alignment table for Des27 variants with mutations at positions 54, 84, 86, 92, 111, 115, 136, 183, 216, 236, plus measured kcat/KM (M⁻¹ s⁻¹) and Asp162 van der Waals (vdW) energy (R.e.u). Variants sorted by increasing kcat/KM: Des27.2 (21, -4.9), Des27.5 (54, -4.9), Des27.4 (59, -4.9), Des27.6 (90, -4.9), Des27 (130, -5.1), Des27.10 (327, -5.2), Des27.1 (344, -5.2), Des27.11 (718, -5.2), Des27.3 (1294, -5.2), Des27.13 (2014, -5.1), Des27.9 (2136, -5.5), Des27.12 (3837, -5.4), Des27.7 (12696, -5.4). Higher catalytic efficiency correlates with more favorable (more negative) Asp162 vdW energy.

Pie charts and stacked bars comparing binding and reactive state populations for Des27 vs Des27.7: Des27 has 63% unbound, 37% bound; of bound states, 11% are reactive 'In' conformations, 66% 'Out' conformations, 22% other. Des27.7 has 12% unbound, 88% bound; of bound states, 23% 'In' reactive conformations, 57% 'Out', 19% other. Des27.7 has ~2x higher fraction of reactive bound conformations and much higher overall binding.

Substrate binding conformations: 'In' (blue) conformation is productive for catalysis, with the nitro group positioned near the catalytic base; 'Out' (orange) conformation is non-productive, with the nitro group oriented away from the active site base.

Catalytic base (D162) packing comparison: Des27 model (green) has Leu residues at positions 183, 136, 216 surrounding D162, leading to poor packing. Des27.7 model (blue) has smaller Val residues at 183 and Ile at 136/216, improving packing around D162 to stabilize the catalytic base in an active conformation.

Steric clash relief: Des27 has Leu236 (green) which clashes with the substrate (steric overlap marked by colored spheres). Des27.7 has Val236 (blue), a smaller residue that eliminates the steric clash with the substrate, allowing better binding.

Ligand solvation comparison: Des27 has a hydrophobic active site with Phe92, Ile54, Leu183, leading to unfavorable solvation of the polar substrate. Des27.7 has His92, Val54, Val183, which improves polar interactions with the substrate nitro group and reduces desolvation penalty for binding.

Activation energy (ΔG‡, kcal mol⁻¹) comparison: Des27 experimental ΔG‡ = 19.2 ± 0.2 kcal mol⁻¹, 'Out' conformation ΔG‡ = 17.7 ± 2.2 kcal mol⁻¹, 'In' conformation ΔG‡ = 16.7 ± 2.0 kcal mol⁻¹. Des27.7 experimental ΔG‡ = 16.7 ± 0.2 kcal mol⁻¹, 'Out' ΔG‡ = 15.9 ± 1.4 kcal mol⁻¹, 'In' ΔG‡ = 13.6 ± 0.3 kcal mol⁻¹. The reactive 'In' conformation has ~2.5-3 kcal mol⁻¹ lower activation energy than the 'Out' conformation for both variants, and Des27.7 has ~2.5 kcal mol⁻¹ lower experimental ΔG‡ than Des27.

Plot of melting temperature (Tm, °C, left linear axis, decreasing from 100 to <60) vs kcat/KM (M⁻¹ s⁻¹, right logarithmic axis, increasing from 10³ to 10⁵) for design variants: Des27.7 has Tm = 86 °C, kcat/KM = 12700 M⁻¹ s⁻¹ (11 PROSS mutations, 15 active site mutations, 6 core stabilization mutations). A variant with F113L mutation has Tm = 82 °C, kcat/KM = 123000 M⁻¹ s⁻¹ (10x higher activity than Des27.7). The D162A catalytic dead variant has Tm > 100 °C (no detectable activity, ND). Other variants have Tm 57-81 °C, kcat/KM 2900-11500 M⁻¹ s⁻¹ or ND (no detectable activity). Combinining all design steps (modular assembly, PROSS stabilization, active site optimization, core stabilization) yields high stability and high catalytic activity.

anchors.snippet: Fig. 1 | Key steps in the design workflow. a, KE of 5-nitrobenzisoxazole. ‘B’ is a base, implemented as the sidechain of Asp or Glu. b, Thousands of backbones are generated through combinatorial backbone assembly (step 1) and stabilized using PROSS29 (step 2, red spheres). payload.content: Sequence alignment table for Des27 variants with mutations at positions 54, 84, 86, 92, 111, 115, 136, 183, 216, 236, plus measured kcat/KM (M⁻¹ s⁻¹) and Asp162 van der Waals (vdW) energy (R.e.u). Variants sorted by increasing kcat/KM: Des27.2 (21, -4.9), Des27.5 (54, -4.9), Des27.4 (59, -4.9), Des27.6 (90, -4.9), Des27 (130, -5.1), Des27.10 (327, -5.2), Des27.1 (344, -5.2), Des27.11 (718, -5.2), Des27.3 (1294, -5.2), Des27.13 (2014, -5.1), Des27.9 (2136, -5.5), Des27.12 (3837, -5.4), Des27.7 (12696, -5.4). Higher catalytic efficiency correlates with more favorable (more negative) Asp162 vdW energy.

Pie charts and stacked bars comparing binding and reactive state populations for Des27 vs Des27.7: Des27 has 63% unbound, 37% bound; of bound states, 11% are reactive 'In' conformations, 66% 'Out' conformations, 22% other. Des27.7 has 12% unbound, 88% bound; of bound states, 23% 'In' reactive conformations, 57% 'Out', 19% other. Des27.7 has ~2x higher fraction of reactive bound conformations and much higher overall binding.

Substrate binding conformations: 'In' (blue) conformation is productive for catalysis, with the nitro group positioned near the catalytic base; 'Out' (orange) conformation is non-productive, with the nitro group oriented away from the active site base.

Catalytic base (D162) packing comparison: Des27 model (green) has Leu residues at positions 183, 136, 216 surrounding D162, leading to poor packing. Des27.7 model (blue) has smaller Val residues at 183 and Ile at 136/216, improving packing around D162 to stabilize the catalytic base in an active conformation.

Steric clash relief: Des27 has Leu236 (green) which clashes with the substrate (steric overlap marked by colored spheres). Des27.7 has Val236 (blue), a smaller residue that eliminates the steric clash with the substrate, allowing better binding.

Ligand solvation comparison: Des27 has a hydrophobic active site with Phe92, Ile54, Leu183, leading to unfavorable solvation of the polar substrate. Des27.7 has His92, Val54, Val183, which improves polar interactions with the substrate nitro group and reduces desolvation penalty for binding.

Activation energy (ΔG‡, kcal mol⁻¹) comparison: Des27 experimental ΔG‡ = 19.2 ± 0.2 kcal mol⁻¹, 'Out' conformation ΔG‡ = 17.7 ± 2.2 kcal mol⁻¹, 'In' conformation ΔG‡ = 16.7 ± 2.0 kcal mol⁻¹. Des27.7 experimental ΔG‡ = 16.7 ± 0.2 kcal mol⁻¹, 'Out' ΔG‡ = 15.9 ± 1.4 kcal mol⁻¹, 'In' ΔG‡ = 13.6 ± 0.3 kcal mol⁻¹. The reactive 'In' conformation has ~2.5-3 kcal mol⁻¹ lower activation energy than the 'Out' conformation for both variants, and Des27.7 has ~2.5 kcal mol⁻¹ lower experimental ΔG‡ than Des27.

Plot of melting temperature (Tm, °C, left linear axis, decreasing from 100 to <60) vs kcat/KM (M⁻¹ s⁻¹, right logarithmic axis, increasing from 10³ to 10⁵) for design variants: Des27.7 has Tm = 86 °C, kcat/KM = 12700 M⁻¹ s⁻¹ (11 PROSS mutations, 15 active site mutations, 6 core stabilization mutations). A variant with F113L mutation has Tm = 82 °C, kcat/KM = 123000 M⁻¹ s⁻¹ (10x higher activity than Des27.7). The D162A catalytic dead variant has Tm > 100 °C (no detectable activity, ND). Other variants have Tm 57-81 °C, kcat/KM 2900-11500 M⁻¹ s⁻¹ or ND (no detectable activity). Combinining all design steps (modular assembly, PROSS stabilization, active site optimization, core stabilization) yields high stability and high catalytic activity.

analysis_results.content: This figure shows the reaction mechanism for 5-nitrobenzisoxazole decomposition catalyzed by a designed enzyme: a base (B⁻) deprotonates the 3-position of 5-nitrobenzisoxazole, forming a negatively charged transition state with partial bond cleavage (B--H, N--O bonds), and yielding 2-cyano-4-nitrophenolate anion plus BH as products.

analysis_results.figure_id: Design Workflow (second image, panel b, panels 1-3; third image, panels 4-6)
analysis_results.content: The 6-step enzyme design workflow is: 1) Modular assembly of protein folds, 2) Stability design using PROSS/TRP tool, 3) Geometric matching/filtering using a sigmoidal scoring function: f = product over 1/(1 + e^((x-μ)/σ)), 4) Active site and core position stabilization, 5) Experimental screening including protein expression, purification, and absorbance-based activity assays (380 nm absorbance vs substrate concentration), 6) Active-site optimization.

analysis_results.figure_id: Figure 3a (bar chart of kcat/KM for Des27 variants)
analysis_results.content: This is a log-scale bar chart of kcat/KM (units: M⁻¹ s⁻¹) for 12 designed enzyme variants: Des27 (110), Des27.1 (300), Des27.2 (20), Des27.3 (1200), Des27.4 (50), Des27.5 (45), Des27.6 (80), Des27.7 (12000, highest value), Des27.9 (2000), Des27.10 (300), Des27.11 (600), Des27.12 (4000), Des27.13 (1800). The y-axis is logarithmic ranging from 10² to 10⁴ M⁻¹ s⁻¹. Error bars represent standard deviation.

This Michaelis-Menten plot measures initial velocity V₀ (units: mOD min⁻¹) as a function of 5-nitrobenzisoxazole concentration (mM, 0 to 1.0 mM). The fitted kinetic parameters are: Kₘ = 0.21 ± 0.06 mM, k_cat = 2.85 ± 1.2 s⁻¹, k_cat/Kₘ = 12,696 ± 1,738 M⁻¹ s⁻¹. Scatter points are experimental measurements, the solid line is the fitted curve.

analysis_results.figure_id: Figure 3c (active site crystal structure/model alignment, Des27.7)
analysis_results.content: This structure overlay shows the Des27.7 crystal structure (light gray) and design model (light blue) active site, with labeled residues: F113, I136, D162, V183, I216, V236 surrounding the 5-nitrobenzisoxazole substrate, showing good agreement between designed and experimental structures.

analysis_results.figure_id: Figure 4a (sequence and activity table for Des27 variants)
analysis_results.content: This table shows residue identities at positions 54, 84, 86, 92, 111, 115, 136, 183, 216, 236 for each variant, plus kcat/KM (M⁻¹ s⁻¹) and Asp162 van der Waals energy (R.e.u) values. Variants are ordered by increasing activity: Des27.2 (21, -4.9), Des27.5 (54, -4.9), Des27.4 (59, -4.9), Des27.6 (90, -4.9), Des27 (130, -5.1), Des27.10 (327, -5.2), Des27.1 (344, -5.2), Des27.11 (718, -5.2), Des27.3 (1294, -5.2), Des27.13 (2014, -5.1), Des27.9 (2136, -5.5), Des27.12 (3837, -5.4), Des27.7 (12696, -5.4). Higher activity correlates with more favorable (more negative) Asp162 vdW energy and mutations at key active site residues.

analysis_results.figure_id: Figure 4c (conformational state pie charts for Des27 and Des27.7)
analysis_results.content: For Des27: 63% of substrate is unbound, 37% bound. Of the bound fraction: 11% in reactive 'In' conformation, 66% 'Out' non-reactive, 22% other. For Des27.7: 12% unbound, 88% bound. Of the bound fraction: 23% 'In' reactive, 57% 'Out' non-reactive, 19% other. Des27.7 has significantly higher bound fraction and higher proportion of reactive binding mode.
Shows the two substrate binding modes: 'In' (proton at reaction site facing catalytic base D162, reactive), 'Out' (proton facing away from D162, non-reactive).

Comparison of Des27 model (green residues) vs Des27.7 model (blue residues) active site packing around catalytic base D162 (red): Des27 has L136, L183, I216; Des27.7 has I136, V183, I216, improving packing around the catalytic base to stabilize the reactive conformation.
Mutating L236 (Des27, green) to V236 (Des27.7, blue) removes steric clash with the substrate, improving binding.
Des27 (green) has F92, L183, I54; Des27.7 (blue) has H92, V183, V54, optimizing solvation and stacking interactions with the substrate to favor the reactive 'In' conformation.

Bar chart of activation free energy ΔG‡ (kcal mol⁻¹) for Des27 and Des27.7: Experimental Des27 = 19.2 ± 0.2, Des27 'Out' conformation = 17.7 ± 2.2, Des27 'In' conformation = 16.7 ± 2.1; Experimental Des27.7 = 16.7 ± 0.2, Des27.7 'Out' = 15.9 ± 1.4, Des27.7 'In' = 13.6 ± 0.6. The reactive 'In' conformation has lower activation energy, and Des27.7 has lower experimental ΔG‡ than Des27, matching the higher activity.

This plot shows melting temperature Tₘ (°C, left axis, 100 to 60 °C reverse scale) and kcat/KM (M⁻¹ s⁻¹, right log axis) for design variants with different design steps applied: The top variant Des27.7 (all 4 design steps: modular assembly, PROSS (11 muts), active site (15 muts), core stabilization (6 muts)) has Tₘ = 86 °C, kcat/KM = 12,700 M⁻¹ s⁻¹. Variants missing PROSS have ND (not detected) activity, Tₘ 57/69 °C. Variant missing active site design has Tₘ 60 °C, kcat/KM = 2,900 M⁻¹ s⁻¹. Variant missing core stabilization has Tₘ 81 °C, kcat/KM = 11,500 M⁻¹ s⁻¹. The D162A active site mutation has Tₘ >100 °C, no activity (ND). The F113L optimized variant has Tₘ = 82 °C, kcat/KM = 123,000 M⁻¹ s⁻¹, ~10x higher activity than Des27.7 while maintaining high stability.

extraction_items.title: Design Workflow (second image, panel b, panels 1-3; third image, panels 4-6)

payload.figure_id: Design Workflow (second image, panel b, panels 1-3; third image, panels 4-6)
payload.content: The 6-step enzyme design workflow is: 1) Modular assembly of protein folds, 2) Stability design using PROSS/TRP tool, 3) Geometric matching/filtering using a sigmoidal scoring function: f = product over 1/(1 + e^((x-μ)/σ)), 4) Active site and core position stabilization, 5) Experimental screening including protein expression, purification, and absorbance-based activity assays (380 nm absorbance vs substrate concentration), 6) Active-site optimization.

payload.content: Mutating L236 (Des27, green) to V236 (Des27.7, blue) removes steric clash with the substrate, improving binding.
Des27 (green) has F92, L183, I54; Des27.7 (blue) has H92, V183, V54, optimizing solvation and stacking interactions with the substrate to favor the reactive 'In' conformation.

Bar chart of activation free energy ΔG‡ (kcal mol⁻¹) for Des27 and Des27.7: Experimental Des27 = 19.2 ± 0.2, Des27 'Out' conformation = 17.7 ± 2.2, Des27 'In' conformation = 16.7 ± 2.1; Experimental Des27.7 = 16.7 ± 0.2, Des27.7 'Out' = 15.9 ± 1.4, Des27.7 'In' = 13.6 ± 0.6. The reactive 'In' conformation has lower activation energy, and Des27.7 has lower experimental ΔG‡ than Des27, matching the higher activity.

payload.content: This plot shows melting temperature Tₘ (°C, left axis, 100 to 60 °C reverse scale) and kcat/KM (M⁻¹ s⁻¹, right log axis) for design variants with different design steps applied: The top variant Des27.7 (all 4 design steps: modular assembly, PROSS (11 muts), active site (15 muts), core stabilization (6 muts)) has Tₘ = 86 °C, kcat/KM = 12,700 M⁻¹ s⁻¹. Variants missing PROSS have ND (not detected) activity, Tₘ 57/69 °C. Variant missing active site design has Tₘ 60 °C, kcat/KM = 2,900 M⁻¹ s⁻¹. Variant missing core stabilization has Tₘ 81 °C, kcat/KM = 11,500 M⁻¹ s⁻¹. The D162A active site mutation has Tₘ >100 °C, no activity (ND). The F113L optimized variant has Tₘ = 82 °C, kcat/KM = 123,000 M⁻¹ s⁻¹, ~10x higher activity than Des27.7 while maintaining high stability.

analysis_results.content: Reaction mechanism for 5-nitrobenzisoxazole deprotonation catalyzed by a base (B⁻): Step 1: B⁻ abstracts the proton at position 3 of 5-nitrobenzisoxazole, forming a transition state with partial charges (δ⁺ on H-B, δ⁻ on O). Step 2: Ring opening occurs to form 2-cyano-4-nitrophenolate and BH. This is the reaction catalyzed by the designed enzymes in the study.

First three steps of the enzyme design workflow: 1) Modular assembly of protein building blocks, 2) Stability design using PROSS/Trp stabilization tools, 3) Geometric matching and filtering using a sigmoidal scoring function (f = Π 1/(1 + e^((x-μ)/σ)) to select active site configurations that match the reaction transition state geometry.

Final three steps of the enzyme design workflow: 4) Active site and core position stabilization, 5) Experimental screening: protein expression, purification, and kinetic assay measuring absorbance at 380 nm as a function of substrate concentration, 6) Active-site optimization to improve catalytic performance.

Bar chart showing kcat/KM values (units: M⁻¹ s⁻¹, y-axis log scale from 10² to 10⁴) for 12 designed enzyme variants: Des27 (110 ± 10 M⁻¹ s⁻¹), Des27.1 (320 ± 20 M⁻¹ s⁻¹), Des27.2 (20 ± 5 M⁻¹ s⁻¹), Des27.3 (1200 ± 100 M⁻¹ s⁻¹), Des27.4 (50 ± 10 M⁻¹ s⁻¹), Des27.5 (45 ± 10 M⁻¹ s⁻¹), Des27.6 (80 ± 10 M⁻¹ s⁻¹), Des27.7 (11,000 ± 1000 M⁻¹ s⁻¹, highest value), Des27.9 (2000 ± 200 M⁻¹ s⁻¹), Des27.10 (280 ± 30 M⁻¹ s⁻¹), Des27.11 (600 ± 50 M⁻¹ s⁻¹), Des27.12 (3500 ± 300 M⁻¹ s⁻¹), Des27.13 (1800 ± 200 M⁻¹ s⁻¹). The dashed line indicates 10² M⁻¹ s⁻¹.

Michaelis-Menten plot for variant Des27.7, measuring initial velocity V₀ (units: mOD min⁻¹, y-axis 0 to 50) as a function of 5-nitrobenzisoxazole concentration (units: mM, x-axis 0 to 1.0). The fitted kinetic parameters are: K_M = 0.21 ± 0.06 mM, k_cat = 2.85 ± 1.2 s⁻¹, k_cat/K_M = 12,696 ± 1,738 M⁻¹ s⁻¹. The black line is the fitted curve, black points are experimental measurements.

Active site overlay of Des27.7 crystal structure (gray) and computational design model (light blue). Key active site residues are labeled: F113, I136, D162 (catalytic base), V183, I216, V236. The ligand (5-nitrobenzisoxazole or transition state analog) is shown in the center, demonstrating high agreement between the designed model and experimental crystal structure.

Table of 12 designed variants with amino acid identities at 10 positions (54, 84, 86, 92, 111, 115, 136, 183, 216, 236), plus measured kcat/KM (M⁻¹ s⁻¹) and calculated Asp162 van der Waals (vdW) energy (R.e.u). Values are sorted by increasing kcat/KM: Des27.2 (21, -4.9), Des27.5 (54, -4.9), Des27.4 (59, -4.9), Des27.6 (90, -4.9), Des27 (130, -5.1), Des27.10 (327, -5.2), Des27.1 (344, -5.2), Des27.11 (718, -5.2), Des27.3 (1,294, -5.2), Des27.13 (2,014, -5.1), Des27.9 (2,136, -5.5), Des27.12 (3,837, -5.4), Des27.7 (12,696, -5.4). Lower (more negative) Asp162 vdW energy correlates with higher catalytic activity for higher-performing variants.

analysis_results.figure_id: Figure 4c: Binding and reactive state pie charts
analysis_results.content: Comparison of substrate binding and reactive state populations for Des27 (low activity) and Des27.7 (high activity): For Des27: 63% Unbound, 37% Bound. Of the bound states, 11% are In (reactive conformation), 66% are Out (non-reactive conformation), 22% are Other. For Des27.7: 12% Unbound, 88% Bound. Of the bound states, 23% are In, 57% are Out, 19% are Other. Des27.7 has substantially higher binding and a 2x higher fraction of bound substrate in the reactive In conformation.

Two substrate binding conformations in the active site: 1) In conformation (blue): substrate is oriented for proton abstraction by the catalytic base (productive for reaction), 2) Out conformation (orange): substrate is flipped, non-productive for reaction.

Comparison of catalytic base (D162) packing in Des27 model (green) and Des27.7 model (blue): Des27 has residues L136, L183, I216 around D162; Des27.7 has I136, V183, I216, leading to tighter packing of D162 for improved catalysis.

Comparison of steric interactions in Des27 (left, green) and Des27.7 (right, blue): Des27 has L236 which causes steric overlap with the substrate; Des27.7 has V236 which relieves this steric clash, improving substrate binding.

Comparison of ligand solvation and active site interactions in Des27 (left, green) and Des27.7 (right, blue): Des27 has residues I54, F92, L183; Des27.7 has V54, H92, V183, leading to improved desolvation and stabilization of the bound substrate in the reactive conformation.

Bar chart showing experimental and calculated activation free energies (ΔG‡, units: kcal mol⁻¹, y-axis 10 to 22) for Des27 and Des27.7: For Des27: Experimental ΔG‡ = 19.2 ± 0.2 kcal mol⁻¹, Out conformation calculated ΔG‡ = 17.6 ± 2.1 kcal mol⁻¹, In conformation calculated ΔG‡ = 16.6 ± 2.1 kcal mol⁻¹. For Des27.7: Experimental ΔG‡ = 16.7 ± 0.2 kcal mol⁻¹, Out conformation calculated ΔG‡ = 15.9 ± 1.5 kcal mol⁻¹, In conformation calculated ΔG‡ = 13.6 ± 0.4 kcal mol⁻¹. Des27.7 has a ~2.5 kcal mol⁻¹ lower experimental activation energy, consistent with its higher catalytic activity, and the In conformation matches experimental values best.

Chart showing melting temperature (Tₘ, °C, left x-axis from 60 to 100+, decreasing) and kcat/KM (M⁻¹ s⁻¹, right x-axis log scale from ~10³ to 10⁵) for designed variants with different combinations of design steps (modular assembly, PROSS (11 muts), active site (15 muts), core stabilization (6 muts)). Selected values: Des27.7: Tₘ = 86 °C, kcat/KM = 12,700 M⁻¹ s⁻¹; Variant with core stabilization omitted: Tₘ = 60 °C, kcat/KM = 2,900 M⁻¹ s⁻¹; Variant with active site optimization omitted: Tₘ = 81 °C, kcat/KM = 11,500 M⁻¹ s⁻¹; D162A active site mutant: Tₘ >100 °C, no detectable activity (ND); F113L mutant: Tₘ = 82 °C, kcat/KM = 123,000 M⁻¹ s⁻¹ (highest activity, ~10x higher than Des27.7).

payload.content: Reaction mechanism for 5-nitrobenzisoxazole deprotonation catalyzed by a base (B⁻): Step 1: B⁻ abstracts the proton at position 3 of 5-nitrobenzisoxazole, forming a transition state with partial charges (δ⁺ on H-B, δ⁻ on O). Step 2: Ring opening occurs to form 2-cyano-4-nitrophenolate and BH. This is the reaction catalyzed by the designed enzymes in the study.

First three steps of the enzyme design workflow: 1) Modular assembly of protein building blocks, 2) Stability design using PROSS/Trp stabilization tools, 3) Geometric matching and filtering using a sigmoidal scoring function (f = Π 1/(1 + e^((x-μ)/σ)) to select active site configurations that match the reaction transition state geometry.

payload.content: Table of 12 designed variants with amino acid identities at 10 positions (54, 84, 86, 92, 111, 115, 136, 183, 216, 236), plus measured kcat/KM (M⁻¹ s⁻¹) and calculated Asp162 van der Waals (vdW) energy (R.e.u). Values are sorted by increasing kcat/KM: Des27.2 (21, -4.9), Des27.5 (54, -4.9), Des27.4 (59, -4.9), Des27.6 (90, -4.9), Des27 (130, -5.1), Des27.10 (327, -5.2), Des27.1 (344, -5.2), Des27.11 (718, -5.2), Des27.3 (1,294, -5.2), Des27.13 (2,014, -5.1), Des27.9 (2,136, -5.5), Des27.12 (3,837, -5.4), Des27.7 (12,696, -5.4). Lower (more negative) Asp162 vdW energy correlates with higher catalytic activity for higher-performing variants.

payload.figure_id: Figure 4c: Binding and reactive state pie charts
payload.content: Comparison of substrate binding and reactive state populations for Des27 (low activity) and Des27.7 (high activity): For Des27: 63% Unbound, 37% Bound. Of the bound states, 11% are In (reactive conformation), 66% are Out (non-reactive conformation), 22% are Other. For Des27.7: 12% Unbound, 88% Bound. Of the bound states, 23% are In, 57% are Out, 19% are Other. Des27.7 has substantially higher binding and a 2x higher fraction of bound substrate in the reactive In conformation.

payload.content: Comparison of catalytic base (D162) packing in Des27 model (green) and Des27.7 model (blue): Des27 has residues L136, L183, I216 around D162; Des27.7 has I136, V183, I216, leading to tighter packing of D162 for improved catalysis.

Comparison of steric interactions in Des27 (left, green) and Des27.7 (right, blue): Des27 has L236 which causes steric overlap with the substrate; Des27.7 has V236 which relieves this steric clash, improving substrate binding.

Comparison of ligand solvation and active site interactions in Des27 (left, green) and Des27.7 (right, blue): Des27 has residues I54, F92, L183; Des27.7 has V54, H92, V183, leading to improved desolvation and stabilization of the bound substrate in the reactive conformation.

anchors.snippet: After matching, Rosetta sequence design was performed in an 8 Å shell around the ligand and catalytic residues. The design was performed under theozyme and PSSM constraints. To constrain the sequence space, the catalytic residues of the IGPS family, as described by the M-CSA database61, underwent Rosetta computational mutation scanning, and all mutations with $\Delta \Delta G _ { \mathrm { s y s t e m } } < + 1$ R.e.u. compared with the starting identity were included as allowed for design. The Rosetta Match and design steps generated $1 0 ^ { 5 }$ to $1 0 ^ { 6 }$ designs for each starting structure. Designs were filtered on the basis of a ‘fuzzy’-logic objective function45 that balanced potentially conflicting criteria: energy density (system energy divided by the protein length), energy rank relative to other designs in the same backbone, active-site vdW energy, catalytic base vdW, ligand solvation and accuracy of theozyme geometry. vdW energy is defined as the sum of the Rosetta atomistic energy terms fa_atr and fa_rep (as weighted in Rosetta scoring function62).

payload.content: Bar chart showing experimental and calculated activation free energies (ΔG‡, units: kcal mol⁻¹, y-axis 10 to 22) for Des27 and Des27.7: For Des27: Experimental ΔG‡ = 19.2 ± 0.2 kcal mol⁻¹, Out conformation calculated ΔG‡ = 17.6 ± 2.1 kcal mol⁻¹, In conformation calculated ΔG‡ = 16.6 ± 2.1 kcal mol⁻¹. For Des27.7: Experimental ΔG‡ = 16.7 ± 0.2 kcal mol⁻¹, Out conformation calculated ΔG‡ = 15.9 ± 1.5 kcal mol⁻¹, In conformation calculated ΔG‡ = 13.6 ± 0.4 kcal mol⁻¹. Des27.7 has a ~2.5 kcal mol⁻¹ lower experimental activation energy, consistent with its higher catalytic activity, and the In conformation matches experimental values best.

payload.content: Chart showing melting temperature (Tₘ, °C, left x-axis from 60 to 100+, decreasing) and kcat/KM (M⁻¹ s⁻¹, right x-axis log scale from ~10³ to 10⁵) for designed variants with different combinations of design steps (modular assembly, PROSS (11 muts), active site (15 muts), core stabilization (6 muts)). Selected values: Des27.7: Tₘ = 86 °C, kcat/KM = 12,700 M⁻¹ s⁻¹; Variant with core stabilization omitted: Tₘ = 60 °C, kcat/KM = 2,900 M⁻¹ s⁻¹; Variant with active site optimization omitted: Tₘ = 81 °C, kcat/KM = 11,500 M⁻¹ s⁻¹; D162A active site mutant: Tₘ >100 °C, no detectable activity (ND); F113L mutant: Tₘ = 82 °C, kcat/KM = 123,000 M⁻¹ s⁻¹ (highest activity, ~10x higher than Des27.7).
